title: Enjoying the classic Peking Opera 'Drunken Concubine' at Mid Autumn Festival
description:
The Mid-Autumn Festival falls on October 1 this year, marking one of the most important and poetic Chinese festivals. People across the country reunite with family and friends, and enjoy the charm of moon together. 

Some people will choose to watch Peking Opera during the festivities, classic opera "Drunken Concubine" is one of the most widely circulated ones. It is also a must-see Chinese opera for die-hard opera fans around the world.

title: Peking Opera:《The Drunken Concubine》京剧: 贵妃醉酒, [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Hi everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: www.bilibili.com/video/BV1HG4y1Z7A6/?spm_id_from=333.337.search-card.all.click
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

The most well-known work of the famous opera master Mei Lanfang, The Drunken Concubine is more of a one-person performance than an opera that follows a long plot. It is a one-act opera that explores themes of the fragility of life, power and sorrow, and requires high skill from the performer. 

This opera is about Concubine Yang, a real person who was one of the Four Ancient Beauties of China. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Four_Beauties

I hope you enjoy~


Cultural notes:

Chang'e is the chinese goddess of the moon who is accompanies by the jade rabbit. They live in the Guanghan palace on the moon.

title: Chinese - Beijing / Peking opera (京剧): Yandang Mountain (雁荡山) - complete
description:
The war between the rebellious peasants and the imperial army.
Meng Haigong (farmer general) – Xue Yongkang
He Tianlong (emperial general) – Lan Yumin
The plot of this play was made up, and there is neither song nor lyrics throughout. All is depicted by vivid movements, acrobatics and miming. The story is set in 589 AD. 
The name "yandang" comes from the lake of beautiful view on the top of the mountain, where the spreading reed, and the wild geese come and live here in autumn.
Peking Opera of Shanghai, director: Li Yuru, 1984.

title: Peking opera
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00418
Description: Peking opera is a performance art incorporating singing, reciting, acting, martial arts. Although widely practised throughout China, its performance centres on Beijing, Tianjin and Shanghai. Peking opera is sung and recited using primarily Beijing dialect, and its librettos are composed according to a strict set of rules that prize form and rhyme. They tell stories of history, politics, society and daily life and aspire to inform as they entertain. The music of Peking opera plays a key role in setting the pace of the show, creating a particular atmosphere, shaping the characters, and guiding the progress of the stories. 'Civilian plays' emphasize string and wind instruments such as the thin, high-pitched ''jinghu ''and the flute ''dizi, ''while 'military plays' feature percussion instruments like the ''bangu ''or ''daluo. ''Performance is characterized by a formulaic and symbolic style with actors and actresses following established choreography for movements of hands, eyes, torsos, and feet. Traditionally, stage settings and props are kept to a minimum. Costumes are flamboyant and the exaggerated facial make-up uses concise symbols, colours and patterns to portray characters' personalities and social identities. Peking opera is transmitted largely through master-student training with trainees learning basic skills through oral instruction, observation and imitation. It is regarded as an expression of the aesthetic ideal of opera in traditional Chinese society and remains a widely recognized element of the country's cultural heritage.
Country(ies): China

Having already proven himself a master of kinetic action with Zu Warriors from the Magic Mountain, legendary director Tsui Hark would add lashings of comedy and frenzied expressionism to spectacular effect with 1986’s Peking Opera Blues.

Beijing, 1913. In a time of violent turmoil when local tyrants vie for supremacy, three women’s lives converge in the local Peking Opera house. Together they hatch a revolutionary plan to stop president Yuan Shikai from reinstating the monarchy and proclaiming himself emperor. With the help of a righteous freedom-fighter and a disillusioned palace guard, the rebels must use all their wits and brawn to protect their democratic principles and save the emerging Chinese republic.

Showcasing all of Tsui’s talent for visual spectacle and featuring captivating performances from Brigitte Lin, Sally Yeh and Cherie Chung in the three leading roles, Peking Opera Blues is an explosive thrill ride presented here in a stunning 4K restoration and packed with extras.

title: Incredible Vocal in Beijing Opera (1) (Lyrics Translation + Pinyin) 铡美案 - 包龙图打坐在开封府【中英文唱词】
description:
京剧 《铡美案》 片段 【中英文唱词字幕】
Part from Beijing Opera “Execution of Chen Shimei” (Chinese Lyrics + English Translation + Pinyin)

In Song (宋) dynasty (960-1279), Cheng Shimei (陈世美) won the 1st seed in the entrance exam for central government officials. Appreciating his abilities, Princess married him. 
The truth is Shimei already married Qin Xianglian (秦香莲) at his hometown. Years later, Xianglian and their kids came to Kaifeng (开封), the capital of Song, to find him. To hide the truth, Shimei ordered Han Qi (韩琦) to assassinate Xianglian and the kids. After knowing the truth, Han Qi let them go. Han Qi was forced to suicide for this.  
After escaping the assassination, Xianglian files lawsuit against her husband.
Bao Zheng (包拯), or Bao Longtu (包龙图), is the judge of the Supreme Court of Kaifeng.

孟广禄 - Meng Guanglu as Bao Longtu

title: The Legendary Life of Mei Lanfang - How Peking Opera Conquered the World
description:
Discover the captivating story of Peking opera and the legendary Mei Lanfeng, the master who transformed Chinese performing arts forever.

Dive deep into the rich history and evolution of Peking opera, China’s most iconic theatrical tradition. This video unpacks how Peking opera emerged as the nation’s cultural treasure, outshining hundreds of local operatic forms. You’ll learn why it’s called “Peking” opera, explore its roots in Beijing, and see how the genre blends singing, acting, martial arts, and intricate stagecraft. The spotlight is on Mei Lanfeng, the visionary artist who overcame early setbacks and skepticism to revolutionize Peking opera with his creativity, discipline, and courage.

Follow Mei Lanfeng’s journey from his challenging childhood and rigorous training to his rise as a superstar on China’s grandest stages. Witness his relentless drive for innovation - from modernizing costumes and choreography to introducing new themes that resonated with contemporary audiences. The video also reveals how Mei Lanfeng became an international cultural ambassador, dazzling audiences from Shanghai to Broadway and forging connections between East and West. Even during turbulent times, his artistry and integrity shone, leaving a legacy that continues to inspire new generations.

Whether you’re a fan of Chinese opera, fascinated by world music and theater, or curious about the life of one of China’s greatest artists, this story will leave you in awe of the artistry, perseverance, and global impact of Peking opera and Mei Lanfeng.

title: Decoding Peking Opera Masks: From Colors to Characters
description:
#chineseopera #chineseculture #facepainting #painting #funfacts

Did you know the colors on Peking Opera face paint have a secret code? Unlike in the West where blue means sadness, in China, a character's personality is revealed by the colors on their face. We're breaking down the full guide, from heroes to villains! Red shows loyalty and courage, black stands for justice, and blue represents rebellion but also kindness. You'll see gold and silver on mythical figures. On the flip side, villains wear white for cunning, yellow for cruelty, and green is often used for lower-level demons. After this video, you'll be able to guess a character's personality just by their face!

title: Peking Opera:《Hongniang》京剧: 红娘 (1/2), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Hi everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: tv.cctv.com/2019/09/26/VIDEgMn4SmeuyHJYaQazrqvS190926.shtml?spm=C89386.P7wa09jwQaQR.EVc74vQbb7Ue.260
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

Hongniang is a classic romantic comedy, telling the story of how the clever maidservant Hongniang united her Lady, Yingying and the scholar Zhang Junrui together in love.
Hongniang (红娘) literally meaning "red maiden, " is nowadays the common term for a female matchmaker, speaking to the opera's renown.

I hope you Enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

2:35 I don't know what he means by "smelt" tranquility either

3:49 Imperial examinations are exams that determine bureaucracy based on merit. If passed one may become an official and rise on the social ladder. It's a pretty big deal. wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_examination

7:22 Yingying's concern with the parrot is that it might copy what she says and other people might hear it.

10:11 Sometimes called ancestor worship, Chinese culture has a custom of offering food and incense to deceased relatives as a sign of respect and thanks.

The dates the characters speak of are of the traditional Chinese lunar calendar, but I've just used Gregorian terms to avoid confusion

When characters talk about respect and honour they are usually referring to li (礼) which can be roughly translated to courtesy or etiquette; rules of what one can do or say in certain situations.

12:29 A courtesy name is a name given to usually men when they teach adulthood at 20 as it was believed to be rude for others to address an adult man by their given name.

24:34 When everyone refers to Sun Feihu's troops as bandits, it is more metaphorical as they call his troops something more like "thieving troops"

24:55 When Madam Cui refers to family values (门风) it can be roughly translated as family principles or honour

26:34 When Zhang suggests Yingying is still in mourning, he is actually saying she's still donning xiao robes (孝服), literally "filial piety clothes." These were worn during periods of mourning where people would abstain from events perceived as joyful out of respect for the deceased.
Filial Piety was an important part of Chinese culture due to Confucian influences.

38:00 Traditional Chinese weddings feature wedding wines from both families, wine a family ferments until the child is married. The White Horse General means he will not be able to stay to drink their wedding wines.

41:16 The 'flowery candles' are a reference to marriage, a reference to the candles that are found in traditional bridal chambers when a couple is married

46:52 Traditional Chinese marriages have the bride and groom first bow to the heavens and the earth, second bow to their parents, and third bow to each other.

49:07 "Quickly sweep the smoke to the pureland, the sun and moon shine together" is a proverb meaning two people will be united together. Hongniang finishes saying the proverb for Yingying, showing they're on the same page.

52:05 I've translated silence from duoko (多口) which directly translates to too much mouths, meaning something more like you speak too much but that doesn't convey the same emotion 多口 usually implies.

54:14 Hongniang's original words "男女有别" is a Chinese proverb translating to "men and women have their differences." This proverb is used by Hongniang to tell Zhang not to touch her so casually.

56:32 The story of Sima Xiangru and Zhao Wenjun is a classical love story of two poets eloping together. 

57:27 Hongniang makes a reference to their wedding wines as winter melon soup as melons tend to symbolise family abundance. Hongniang's saying they'll have a happy family whilst poking fun at how silly the two are by calling their wedding wines melon soup.

title: Peking Opera 《execute ChenShimei》 Tom and Jerry meme Provenance  Ching Chen Hanji  京剧《铡美案》
description:
During the Northern Song Dynasty, Chen Shimei entered the capital to take the exam, and was the number one champion, and was selected as emperor's son-in-law. His hometown has been drought year after year, his parents died, his wife Qin Xianglian and her children went to Beijing to find her husband, but broke into the palace and was expelled. Prime Minister Wang Yanling sympathized with her and tried to make Qin Xianglian play at the banquet dressed as a singer on Chen Shimei's birthday to help them reconcile, but failed. Wang Yanling sent Qin Xianglian a fan as a token and told her to go to Kaifeng Mansion to find Bao Zheng to sue (Bao Zheng was a famous, powerful and upright official in ancient China). Chen Shimei sent a private arm to Han Qi to hunt down. Han Qi let Qin Xianglian and her son go because he sympathized with them, but committed suicide because he failed to complete the task. Qin Xianglian fled and went to Bao Zheng's official office to accuse Chen Shimei of "killing his wife and child." Bao Zheng summoned Chen Shimei to Kaifeng Mansion to persuade them to reconcile, but Chen Shimei did not cooperate. Bao Zheng asked Qin Xianglian and Chen Shimei to confront each other. Chen Shimei relied on his noble status to argue with strong words; Bao Zheng was angry and prepared to execute Chen Shimei. The princess and queen mother rushed to stop the execution. In order to maintain the justice of the law, Bao Zheng continued to execute his sentence and executed Chen Shimei.
This video tells one of the stories above, what happened after Qin Xianglian arrived at Bao Zheng’s official office

title: 京剧电影《大闹天宫》The Peking Opera film Havoc in heaven千古絕唱The Eternal Masterpieces of Chinese Opera
description:
京剧电影《大闹天宫》根据古典小说《西游记》第五、第六回改编，讲述了孙悟空大闹天宫的故事。孙悟空到龙宫求借兵器，得到定海神针作为兵器。龙王告状，李长庚设计诱使孙悟空到天宫，最终孙悟空大闹天宫，打败李天王率领的天兵天将‌。孙悟空在得到定海神针后，返回花果山自封“齐天大圣”。李长庚再次邀请孙悟空参加蟠桃宴，孙悟空识破阴谋，大闹蟠桃宴并盗食金丹。随后，孙悟空被太上老君投入八卦炉内，炼成火眼金睛。最终，孙悟空打败了李天王率领的天兵天将，取得了胜利‌。
The Peking Opera film "The Havoc in Heaven" is adapted from chapters 5 and 6 of the classic Chinese novel Journey to the West. It tells the story of Sun Wukong (the Monkey King) creating chaos in Heaven.
The plot begins with Sun Wukong going to the Dragon Palace to borrow a weapon, where he obtains the Ruyi Jingu Bang, also known as the Sea-Controlling Staff, as his weapon. After the Dragon King complains to Heaven, Li Changgeng (the Celestial Court’s official) devises a plan to lure Sun Wukong to Heaven. Eventually, Sun Wukong wreaks havoc in Heaven, defeating Li Tianwang (Heavenly King Li) and his army of celestial soldiers.

After obtaining the Sea-Controlling Staff, Sun Wukong returns to his home, Mount Huaguo, where he declares himself the "Great Sage Equal to Heaven." Li Changgeng then invites Sun Wukong to the Peach Banquet, but Sun Wukong sees through the plot against him. In response, he causes chaos at the banquet and steals the magical elixirs.

Afterward, Sun Wukong is captured and thrown into Taishang Laojun's Eight Trigrams Furnace, where he is supposed to be refined into ashes. However, instead of being destroyed, he emerges with his "Fiery Golden Eyes," which grant him the ability to see through illusions. In the end, Sun Wukong triumphs over the celestial soldiers led by Li Tianwang and emerges victorious.

This film adaptation brings to life the thrilling and mischievous adventures of Sun Wukong, capturing his rebellious spirit, as well as the mythological elements of Journey to the West.

For centuries, they were the stuff of legends – the Emperor's shadow, draped in embroidered uniforms, their blades flashing in the dark. The Embroidered Uniform Guard, or Jinyiwei, served as the imperial secret police of China's Ming Dynasty (1368-1644), feared for their power, secrecy and unwavering loyalty to the throne. Their stories, however, were long confined to history books – until now.

Since its premiere in late 2024, the Chinese musical The Emperor's Shadow: Blades and Flowers has been redefining how traditional Chinese history is told on stage. More than 130 performances later, all sold out, the production has grossed over 80 million yuan (about $12 million), according to the show's production company.

"When we were young, we loved foreign musicals," said Liu Xiaoyi, director and choreographer of the musical.

"But once we started creating our own, we realized that Chinese stories and culture can be just as moving through this art form. Young people today are more confident and more interested in traditional culture," Liu said.

At its heart, the musical follows two unlikely protagonists swept into the turbulent aftermath of the Jingnan Campaign, a three-year civil war that reshaped the early Ming Dynasty.

Feng Wuyang, played by Zhang Zhihan, is a grassroots hero who seizes his chance to become a Jinyiwei, tasked with capturing the "world's greatest cricket" for the emperor.

"Audiences relate to Feng Wuyang because he's an ordinary person. People want to see how someone like him survives, grows and makes a difference in a turbulent era," Zhang Zhihan explained.

In contrast, Mei Shuanghua, portrayed by Zhang Ze, is a eunuch official of the Eastern Depot – the Ming court's internal spy agency. Taken from his family at age five and trained for a life of servitude, he secretly plots revenge over decades, moving his pieces like a chess master. But just when he believes he has everything under control, he fails.

"I think audiences find that heartbreaking," Zhang Ze said. "People always feel sorry for things left unresolved."

What sets the musical apart is its embrace of "new Chinese style" – a contemporary aesthetic that reimagines tradition through a modern lens. Composer and arranger Zhang Bo describes the score of the show's music as a fusion of Peking Opera percussion, traditional pentatonic melodies, rap and electronic music.

He believed that the soul of each song lies in its lyrics and melody, which carry a distinctly Chinese cultural identity.

"Once a melody is built around traditional Chinese musical modes, its cultural identity naturally emerges. The arrangements can be incredibly diverse – electronic, symphonic or amplified instruments – but those are enhancements to the core melody," Zhang Bo said.

The musical's impact has also spilled beyond the stage. During its run in Nanjing City, east China's Jiangsu Province, audiences visited the local Ming Dynasty heritage sites inspired by the story. In Wenling City, east China's Zhejiang Province, more than 60% of theatergoers traveled from outside the city, giving a noticeable boost to local tourism, according to the production company.

"If audiences are willing to travel along with the show, that's truly remarkable," Liu said. "But we still have a long way to go. I'd love to see people from around the world come to China to experience original Chinese musicals. These are experiences you can only have in China. The day that happens, I'll be very happy."

For composer Zhang Bo, the show's success reflects a broader cultural shift.

"As China continues to develop, more people will become interested in Chinese culture. With greater understanding comes deeper appreciation, and that will help Chinese culture reach even more people around the world," he said.

title: Peking Opera:《Sun An Presents a Statement》京剧: 孙安动本 (1/2), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Helloo everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://www.bilibili.com/video/BV1Eb411S7o8/?spm_id_from=333.337.search-card.all.click&vd_source=aa56d470ff18f9d6ef03e66266525b0b
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

This is the first opera of 2026! "Sun An Presents a Statement" tells a story based in the Ming dynasty. This version is one of the newer versions, but the opera itself is a Peking opera classic. The plot follows an ordinary but upright official and his story as he attempts to save the country from a disaster hidden in plain sight.

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

3:21 In the Ming dynasty, the Grand Tutor (太师, tàishī) was a high-ranking title given to the Emperor's personal advisor. 

11:43 Relief grain refers to the emergency food in storage that is to be used in case of crisis such as famine or other disasters.

16:56 The County Magistrate/Prefect is the lowest ranking official in the imperial Chinese governance system, responsible for governing their county.

17:39 What I've translated as "great honourable lord" is actually 青天 (qīng tiān) is a term used to refer to a proper and upright official.  It literally means 'blue sky' and comes from 包青天 (Bao of the Blue Sky), a title of Bao Zheng, who was a famous official known for his honesty, most famously in the story of Qin Xianglian/Mei Beheading Case.

18:17 Mausoleums are only built for Emperors, so by building one, you are elevating yourself to the same status as an Emperor, or suggesting that you are plotting to become the Emperor–treason, treason!

18:37 White banners, and white fabric are traditionally worn/adorned during mourning. White is the funeral colour of Chinese culture.

22:38 The Emperor riding the crane means that he has passed away. West is a direction associated with the god, the Western Queen Mother (西王母), who is associated with death.

01:01:42 Yunnan is a southern province in China today. In ancient times it was considered to be very barren, wild, and basically the equivalent of 'middle of nowhere,' hence why people tended to be exiled there.

01:02:29 "Calamity upon my family" is my attempt of translating 灭门之祸 (miè mén zhī huò) which literally means "the disaster of the eradication of the family." In imperial times, under the influences of Confucian thought, the ending of one's family line was thought to be essentially the most devastating and horrible thing that could happen.

title: Peking Opera:《Wild Boar Forest》京剧: 野猪林 (1/2), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Helloo everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://tv.cctv.com/2026/03/21/VIDEiXImxHZhiMEKJwoOyz2y260321.shtml
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

Welcome to the fifth opera of 2026! "Wild Boar Forest" is a famous story from The Water Margin (水浒传, shuǐhǔ zhuàn), a classical Chinese novel set during the Northern Song dynasty about 108 outlaw heroes who band together to overthrow the corrupt government. Each hero is unique and has their own history (it's literally ancient Chinese Marvel/DC). Wild Boar Forest tells of the injustice of Lin Chong, one of these heroes and his story; a righteous and loyal army instructor befallen with injustice and disaster.

The Water Margin one of the Four Great Classic Novels in Chinese literature, the other three being: The Journey to the West (西游记, xīyóujì), Dreams of the Red Chamber (红楼梦, hóng lóu mèng), and Romance of the Three Kingdoms (三国演义, sānguó yǎnyì).

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

12:36 Night lamps and yellow scrolls is a translation of 青灯黄卷 (qīng dēng huáng juàn), a Chinese idiom meaning "green lamp and yellow scrolls," referring to the dim light of an oil lamp and the yellowed pages of books, representing a life of hardship and study, like a that of a monk's. Lu Zhishen basically saying, "why did I start a monastic life?"

13:19 Dongjing (东京, lit. eastern capital) was another name for the city of Kaifeng in Henan.  Daxiangguo Temple (大相国寺) is also a very famous and important temple in Chinese Buddhism.

15:48 "Heresy" here is a translation of 野狐禅 (yě hú chán, lit. wild fox zen), is a term used in Zen Buddhism to describe those who falsely claim enlightenment. It's used to describe Zen that is superficial and misleading, referring to actions that do not align with the true meaning of Zen, yet claim to do so. This includes twisting words and opinions to distort Buddhist teachings. Eventually, this term came to refer to unorthodox  or deviant practices. It's linguistic origin comes from the story of the wild fox koan: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wild_fox_koan

27:29 Every hero in The Water Margin has a nickname/title. Lin Chong's is  Panther Head (豹子头, bàozǐ tóu), because it is said that he is tall and has piercing eyes like a panther's. He is also called Little Zhang Fei (小张飞, xiǎo zhāng fēi) in reference to his martial prowess and supposed physical resemblance to Zhang Fei, a famous figure from the Three Kingdoms period.

28:11 Lu Zhishen was once held a government position in Guanxi before he inadvertently killed a man, but that's another story from his arc in The Water Margin.

30:40 Master-Uncle is my only way of translating 师叔 (shī shū), which literally means "master uncle." This term comes from the Chinese kinship system. If Lu Zhishen's disciples are considered below him like his children, then, Lin Chong who is Lu Zhishen's brother would be considered their uncle. Hence, they call Lin Chong in this way.

36:16 Grand Commandant (太尉, tàiyù) is a title referring to the supreme military commander in ancient China. The title itself varies depending on the dynasty, but they hold ultimate authority over the army and other military affairs. 

48:40 Bianliang (汴梁) is another, much older name, for the city of Kaifeng.

52:03 Attendant is my translation of (虞侯, yú hóu). Yuhou refers to an official military post with varying levels of authority. Lu Qian as a Yuhou acts more like an attendant as his post is not that high.

1:12:43 Lending a knife to kill (借刀杀人, jièdāo shārén) refers to the act of getting someone else your dirty work, or using the strength of another to attack. It is one of the Thirty-Six Stratagems: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thirty-Six_Stratagems

title: Fengbao Finger Dance & Sing Cover Shuo Chang Lian Pu Peking Opera Rap  Original Choreograph
description: Welcome to Fengbao’s daily finger dance and sing cover series on YouTube. In this video, I present my original finger dance interpretation of Shuo Chang Lian Pu, also known as Peking Opera Rap. This iconic Chinese song blends traditional Peking Opera elements with modern rap rhythms, creating a powerful expression of cultural pride and artistic innovation. The lyrics celebrate the vibrant masks and heroic stories of Peking Opera, while questioning outdated stereotypes and inviting a new generation to embrace this heritage. Through my performance, I aim to convey the song’s core emotions: excitement, defiance, and a deep love for traditional art forms reimagined for today’s world. The rapid beats and dramatic shifts in melody mirror the dynamic energy of the painted faces, each mask telling a different tale of loyalty, betrayal, or courage. My finger dance and singing interpretation focuses on rhythm flow, emotional peaks, and the joyful fusion of old and new. This is not a tutorial, but a piece of daily creative expression shared with global audiences. Whether you are a fan of Chinese opera, rap music, or creative finger dancing covers, this video offers a fresh perspective. Keywords: finger dance cover, Peking Opera Rap, Shuo Chang Lian Pu, original finger choreography, daily finger tutting, Chinese traditional music fusion, YouTube finger dance female, emotional dance cover, modern reinterpretation of Peking Opera, vocal and finger performance. Thank you for watching Fengbao’s channel. Your support encourages me to keep exploring music and movement across cultures.

title: Jackie Chan Evolution: From Stunt Kid to Global Legend 🔥 Then Vs Now!
description:
Witness the legendary evolution of Jackie Chan – the man who redefined action cinema with death-defying stunts and infectious humor! 🔥 From his humble beginnings in 1954 Hong Kong to becoming one of the biggest stars in the world.

Born Chan Kong-sang on April 7, 1954, Jackie trained rigorously at the China Drama Academy in acrobatics, martial arts, and Peking Opera. He started as a child actor and stuntman before breaking through with hits like Snake in the Eagle’s Shadow (1978) and Drunken Master. He created his signature style of blending high-octane action, comedy, and real stunts – often performing them himself despite numerous injuries.

His Hollywood breakthrough came with Rush Hour (1998), followed by Shanghai Noon and many more. Over his career, he has appeared in over 100 films that grossed billions worldwide. Achievements include an Honorary Oscar (2016), Guinness World Records for most stunts and credits in one film, and being one of the highest-paid actors.

Family Life: Married to Taiwanese actress Joan Lin since 1982, they have a son, Jaycee Chan. He also has a daughter, Etta Ng, from a past relationship.

From daring young fighter to respected global icon and philanthropist – Jackie Chan’s journey is pure inspiration! ❤️

Which era of Jackie is your favorite? Comment below!
Like, Subscribe & Share for more celebrity evolutions!

title: “I sing faster when I’m irritated.” 😠
description:
In Beijing Opera, 板 (bǎn) refers to the structured beat cycle system that organizes rhythm. 流水板 (liú shuǐ bǎn) or liushui rhythmic pattern, specifically indicates a continuous, unbroken, flowing rhythmic style, typically:

* fairly fast
* evenly pulsed
* designed to create a sense of motion or urgency (like water flowing)

Awarded Beijing Opera performer Shang Wei shares examples of liushui in real plays. Subscribe for more.

Cherie Chung holds several notable records and distinctions in her film career:
- Awards and Distinctions:
+ Only Hong Kong actress to win 2 Best Actress awards at the Asia Pacific Film Festival (for Hong Kong, Hong Kong in 1984 and An Autumn's Tale in 1987)
+ 3 Best Actress nominations at the Hong Kong Film Awards (for Eclipse in 1983, Hong Kong, Hong Kong in 1984, and An Autumn's Tale in 1988)
+ 3 Best Leading Actress nominations at the Golden Horse Awards (for Hong Kong, Hong Kong in 1983, An Autumn's Tale in 1987, and Wild Search in 1989)
+ Won the Hong Kong Film Award for Most Popular Actress for Hong Kong, Hong Kong (1983)
+ Won the Hong Kong Director's Choice Award for Best Actress for An Autumn's Tale (1987)
- Career Records:
+ Appeared in almost 50 films during the 1980s, becoming one of Hong Kong's top actresses
+ Known as the "Marilyn Monroe of Hong Kong" and of the Chinese entertainment industry due to her iconic beauty
+ Retired from acting at age 31 at the peak of her career to marry
+ Formed the first of many classic pairings with Chow Yun-Fat in The Story of Woo Viet (1981), with other notable films including An Autumn's Tale and Spiritual Love
+ Her film An Autumn's Tale grossed over HK$25.5 million and became one of the most beloved Hong Kong romance films ever made

title: The passionate performance of Chinese drum 1 (a type of traditional Chinese drum)#musicalinstrument
description:
Chinese dadao is a traditional percussion instrument with a long history. Archaeological findings have confirmed that as early as the Longshan Culture period (about 3,000 to 4,000 years ago), China had pottery or wooden drums. Its typical structure is a wooden cavity covered with animal skins such as cowhide, and it produces sound by being struck with a drumstick, with a powerful and deep tone. 
In Chinese culture, the large drum has an extremely wide range of applications, spanning military affairs, rituals, religion and daily life. It was once an instrument used to command troops on the battlefield and to display the ceremonial processions in the court; it was also frequently used in temples to gather the congregation, to announce the time (also known as the "morning warning drum"), as well as in folk rituals, festivals and wedding and funeral ceremonies. 
Furthermore, the drum is also the core accompanying instrument for many traditional performing arts forms (such as Peking Opera Drumming, Shandong Drumming, etc.). To this day, its inspiring drumbeats still reverberate in stage performances and folk activities, serving as an important carrier of the memory of Chinese sounds.

title: The Taiwan Strait: 180 KM That Could Trigger World War III
description:
This narrow stretch of water could decide the future of global power. The Taiwan Strait separates China from Taiwan by just about 180 kilometres, and at its closest point, the stakes could not be higher.

Beijing considers Taiwan part of its territory, while Taiwan operates as a self-governing democracy with its own elected government, military, and economy. In recent years, military drills, fighter jet crossings, and naval patrols in the strait have increased sharply, raising fears of a potential confrontation.

In this video, we break down why the Taiwan Strait matters, what's driving the tension, and what a potential conflict could mean for the world.

title: TOPNEWS｜Harvard Alumni Peking Opera: Farewell My Concubine，HongSheng & Haochen ,Youth Reporters
description:
Hi, I'm Hongsheng from the youth reporters corps. Today we are at the cambridge library where a traditional peking opera will be held by the harvard alumni peking opera club. There will also be martial art performances, Without further ado, let's take a look.
Farewell my concubine is the most successful peking opera piece to date. It has been performed by many famous opera singers. Having been made into many films. The vibrant clothing, choreography and singing is very successful in conveying the story.
 
Around 200 participants attended this event. The oldest performers is 81 years old.The event itself lasted for 2 hours and 45 minutes. The first part was the video of Farewell my Concubine and the rest was multiple other classic pieces. This was a very educational event on culture and other things.
Event Organizers

The event was a tribute to the enduring legacy of Chinese Peking Opera. It brought together people of all ages to celebrate a tradition many have cherished since childhood. While more Peking Opera performances are expected in the future, they will likely be held in different locations.

Today was really entertaining. It feels like we were in an actual peking opera house. The performers performing these cultural specialties was a really special experience. I hope this opera could let people understand more about cultures. This is Hongsheng and Haochen. See yall next time.

title: Tribute to Peking Opera master's genius
description:
Exhibition marks 130th birthday of #MeiLanfang whose legacy continues to resonate.

Amid the grand halls of the China Intangible Cultural #Heritage Museum, a splendid tribute unfolds to commemorate the 130th birthday of Mei Lanfang (1894-1961), the master of #PekingOpera whose life and artistry continue to resonate profoundly within Chinese culture and beyond.

From old photos to Peking Opera costumes, from paintings to vinyl records, this retrospective exhibition running from July 23 to Oct 23 celebrates Mei's illustrious career as well as delves deep into his early influences, his pioneering efforts in promoting Peking Opera globally and the enduring legacy he forged.

Peking Opera, or jingju, is a 200-year-old art form that combines singing, dancing, acrobatics and martial arts. In 1790, when four Huiju Opera troupes visited Beijing as part of the 80th birthday celebrations of Emperor Qianlong (1711-99) of the Qing Dynasty (1644-1911), they stayed in the capital. Huiju Opera is a traditional opera form from Anhui province, out of which Peking Opera was later born.

Largely influenced by Huiju Opera, Peking Opera began to formally take shape around 1840, rapidly growing as an art form appealing to royal families and the masses.

Peking Opera was inscribed in 2010 on the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

title: 京剧《赤壁》The Peking opera “Red Cliff”
description:
《赤壁》是由国家大剧院制作的京剧剧目，基于正史《三国志》，结合小说《三国演义》和传统三国戏改编而成。故事发生在汉献帝建安十三年（公元208年）的冬天，曹操率领八十三万水陆大军南下侵略。消息传到东吴，朝廷上下陷入恐慌，主战与主降意见不一，吴主孙权犹豫不决。

诸葛亮舌战东吴群儒，分析利弊；周瑜胸怀谋略，精算胜负；小乔为国难不惜挺身而出，愿意牺牲自己。最终，这些努力促使孙权下定决心与刘备结盟，共同抵御强敌。

风云际会，赤壁之战打响，英雄逐鹿，各显风采。团结凝聚力量，政治化为智慧，军事升华为艺术。

在孔明和周瑜的巧妙计策下，通过草船借箭、火烧连环船，曹操的百万大军瞬间化为灰烬。

孙刘联军欢庆胜利，然而诸葛亮独自站立在赤壁之上，酹酒祭江，缅怀战场。

“Red Cliff” is a Peking opera production by the National Centre for the Performing Arts, based on the historical text Records of the Three Kingdoms and adapted from the novel Romance of the Three Kingdoms and traditional Three Kingdoms plays. The story is set in the winter of the 13th year of the Jian’an era during the reign of Emperor Xian of Han (208 AD). Cao Cao invades the south with a massive army of 830,000 troops by land and water. News of the invasion reaches Eastern Wu, throwing the court into panic, with ministers divided over whether to fight or surrender. Sun Quan, the ruler of Wu, hesitates.

Zhuge Liang debates with the scholars of Wu, outlining the advantages and disadvantages; Zhou Yu holds strategies in his mind, calculating the odds of success; and Xiao Qiao, determined to save her nation, is willing to sacrifice herself. Their efforts finally convince Sun Quan to form an alliance with Liu Bei to resist the powerful enemy together.

As the winds of destiny converge, the battle at Red Cliff unfolds. Heroes vie for supremacy, each showcasing their brilliance. Unity brings strength, politics transform into wisdom, and warfare becomes an art.

With Kongming (Zhuge Liang) and Zhou Yu’s clever strategies—such as borrowing arrows with straw boats and setting fire to the chained ships—Cao Cao’s million-strong army is reduced to ashes in an instant.

While the allied forces of Sun and Liu celebrate their victory, Zhuge Liang stands alone at Red Cliff, offering wine to the river in a solemn tribute to the battlefield.

title: PENY CHAN, Revisit and Reinterpret the Peking Opera, Forecast Forum 7, July, 2022
description:
Polyglot Malaysian singer and performer Peny Chan seeks to highlight the cultural traditions as well as the transformations within the Peking opera’s style of singing. Her project aims to deconstruct the opera’s original structure and reconstruct it into a new experimental genre of singing. 

Working together with musician and vocalist Rully Shabara on producing the work, Chan will use south-east Asian languages and dialects to read and sing traditional Chinese opera librettos, and push the envelope on the accepted sounds within it. Her goal is not to undo but rather to innovate the genre’s historic conventions, in order to carve out space for contemporary impulses.

Shabara said: "Peny Chan is inspired by singing techniques and styles of Peking Opera to create a modern piece that is more fitting to her new path as an experimental solo vocalist. I choose to further my mentorship with Chan and accompany her explorations for a piece that would be more focused on developing and crafting a voice which is uniquely hers.”

title: Peking Opera Schedule 2026 Trailer
description:
Hello hello 🎉

The results of the Peking Opera Voting Event are out! Thanks again to everyone who participated. Although your opera may not have been selected this time, there's always a next time :)

This is the 'schedule' of 2026. There's no set release time for each video, but it is the list that I will be working through for the next 10 translated operas.


1. Sun An Presents a Statement  孙安动本 🍀
Sun An and his family travels to the capital on imperial orders, hoping to ask for relief grain for his disaster-struck county, only to encounter the unexpected. In the face of injustice and a national crisis hidden in plain site, a small official challenges authority to restore justice for the people.

2. A Match of Spring and Autumn  春秋配 🍀
A tale of love between the unfortunate Jiang Qiulian and Li Chunfa, who chance upon each other one day in the outskirts. Misfortune befalls them both after their meeting, riddling their path together with difficulties and dangers.

3. Xu Liang Purges the Tyrant  徐良除霸 (voted, randomly selected)
A young gallant passes a village and discovers injustice. Out of his good heart, he thinks of a plan to rid the local tyrant. Although his appearance is strange, he is the village's only hope.

4. A Spring Boudoir's Dream  春闺梦 (voted, randomly selected)
Lady Zhang waits in her home for her husband to return from the war. With no news or letters, her only dream is to be reunited with her love so that those peaceful days may return once more.

5. Wild Boar Forest  野猪林 🍀
After protecting his wife from an immoral young lord, Lin Chong is placed in a difficult position by higher ups. Seeking to eliminate him, Lin Chong is exiled. With the help of his sworn brother, will he be able to overcome the obstacles in his way?


6. Mu Guiying Breaks Through the Heaven's Gate Formation  穆桂英大破天门阵 (voted, randomly selected)
The Yang family reaches a stalemate at the warfront, unable to overcome the enemy's Heaven's Gate Formation. With a plan in mind, the Yang family needs a special wood that can only be found on one mountain. The only problem, is that the mountain belongs to the Mu family, and is guarded by the fearsome Mu Guiying.

7. Against the Tassel Spear  对花枪 🍀
After 40 years, thought-to-be-widow Jiang Guizhi receives news that her husband is alive. Overjoyed, the three generations of martial Luo family send word to be reunited only to encounter an unexpected obstacle that soon sees the old 'widow' to the battlefield.

8. Chuncao Storms the Court  春草闯堂 🍀
In order to save the life of her benefactor from a false accusation, Chuncao, a mere maidservant, disrupts the court hearing. Without other options, she tells a small lie which eventually spirals out of control, as she is forced to keep up with her own shenanigans.

9. The Cosmic Sword  宇宙锋 (voted)
Zhao Yanrong, the daughter of a powerful scheming official is caught in the midst of a political plot. Used like a bargaining chip, she is forced to find ways to survive the struggles of the inner palace.

10. Dingjun Mountain & Yangping Pass  定军山・阳平关 (voted)
These two operas are usually performed together, telling the story of Huang Zhong, a general from the Three Kingdoms period and his exploits on the battlefield. Although he is old in age, his strong spirit lifts him high above his enemies.

title: 🎭 The Thirteen Masters: The Birth of Peking Opera
description:
The “Thirteen Masters of the Tongzhi–Guangxu Era.”
A single group portrait that shaped the foundation of Peking Opera.
These thirteen performers refined vocal systems, role types, and performance standards—
transforming opera from popular entertainment into a disciplined art form.
Though the painting’s authorship remains debated,
its influence has endured for over a century,
becoming a visual anchor for the origins of modern Peking Opera.

同光十三绝。
一幅人物画，奠定了京剧的艺术根基。
十三位同治、光绪年间的名伶，
系统化了唱腔、行当与表演规范，
使京剧从民间戏曲走向成熟的舞台艺术。
虽画作者尚有争议，
但其形象早已成为京剧诞生与发展的象征。

title: 京剧电影《红楼二尤 》Peking Opera The Two Yous from Dream of the Red Chamber
description:
《红楼二尤》是传统京剧剧目，别名《鸳鸯剑》，由荀慧生编演，荀派代表作之一。本剧取材于名著《红楼梦》，剧情围绕尤二姐和尤三姐的命运展开。尤二姐和尤三姐是曹雪芹笔下的一对悲剧人物。20世纪30年代经过荀慧生先生的创造搬上京剧舞台以后，成为花旦的重头戏，久演不衰。剧情梗概：少年柳湘莲，素嗜串戏，适赖尚荣庆寿，邀之串演《雅观楼》。贾珍、贾琏弟兄前往祝寿，贾珍妻妹尤二姐、尤三姐与其母尤老娘亦随去观剧。贾琏垂涎尤二姐姿容，私以九龙玉佩为聘，背其妻王熙凤偷娶之。贾珍对尤三姐也心怀叵测，不想反被尤三姐嘲笑痛斥。尤三姐自看戏后，爱慕柳湘莲，将心愿诉之老娘与尤二姐。贾琏自愿为媒，寻到柳湘莲提亲。柳湘莲允婚，并以所佩鸳鸯剑作为聘礼。其后因误信薛蟠之言，疑尤三姐与贾琏已有暧昧，往贾府索剑退婚。尤三姐为明心迹，掣鸳鸯剑自刎。柳湘莲悔之不及，疯癫而去。王熙凤觉察贾琏偷娶尤二姐之事，乘其外出之际，将尤二姐诓入荣国府，唆使贾琏侍妾秋桐百般凌辱，其后，又将尤二姐初生婴儿烫死，勒逼尤二姐至死。尤氏姐妹双双成为封建制度的牺牲者。

“The Two Yous from Dream of the Red Chamber” is a classic play in the Xun school of Peking Opera, also known as “The Mandarin Duck Sword,” adapted and performed by Xun Huisheng. It is one of the representative works of the Xun style. The play is based on the renowned novel Dream of the Red Chamber, focusing on the tragic fates of You Erjie (Second Sister You) and You Sanjie (Third Sister You), two tragic figures created by Cao Xueqin. In the 1930s, Xun Huisheng brought this story to the Peking Opera stage, turning it into a major performance piece for young female roles (Huadan), which has remained popular ever since.

Plot Summary:

Young Liu Xianglian, a lover of performances, is invited to participate in a play titled Elegant Tower at the birthday celebration of Lai Shangrong. Jia Zhen and Jia Lian, two brothers, attend the celebration, and they are accompanied by Jia Zhen’s sisters-in-law, You Erjie and You Sanjie, along with their mother, Old Lady You. Jia Lian, infatuated with You Erjie’s beauty, secretly offers a jade pendant as a betrothal gift and marries her in secret, hiding it from his wife, Wang Xifeng. Meanwhile, Jia Zhen harbors improper intentions towards You Sanjie, but she mocks and scolds him.

After watching the play, You Sanjie admires Liu Xianglian and confides her feelings to her mother and You Erjie. Jia Lian volunteers to act as a matchmaker and proposes the marriage to Liu Xianglian. Liu agrees and sends his Mandarin Duck Sword as an engagement gift. However, later, due to believing false rumors from Xue Pan, Liu Xianglian suspects that You Sanjie and Jia Lian have an improper relationship. In anger, he goes to the Jia residence to retrieve his sword and break off the engagement. To prove her innocence, You Sanjie unsheathes the Mandarin Duck Sword and kills herself. Liu Xianglian regrets his actions too late and leaves in madness.

Wang Xifeng, upon discovering Jia Lian’s secret marriage to You Erjie, tricks You Erjie into moving into the Rongguo Mansion while Jia Lian is away. She then incites Jia Lian’s concubine, Qiu Tong, to subject You Erjie to constant humiliation. Later, Wang Xifeng even has You Erjie’s newborn baby scalded to death and forces You Erjie into suicide. Both You sisters ultimately become victims of the feudal system.

This opera not only portrays the tragic fates of You Erjie and You Sanjie but also reveals, through the interactions between Liu Xianglian, Jia Lian, Wang Xifeng, and others, the complex relationships and the helpless struggles of women under the oppression of feudal society. Xun Huisheng’s artistic adaptation seamlessly combines the emotional tension of the original novel with dramatic techniques, making it a masterpiece of the Xun school that continues to be performed and admired today.

Peking opera - is a form of traditional
Chinese theatre which combines music, vocal performance, mime, dance, and acrobatics.

-Peking opera was born when the 'Four Great Anhui Troupes' brought Anhui opera, or what is now called Huiju, in 1790 to Beijing, for the eightieth birthday of the Qianlong Emperor on 25 September.

title: The Remarkable Life and Legacy of Peking Opera Master Cheng Yanqiu
description:
Step into the world of Peking opera and discover how Cheng Yanqiu revolutionized Chinese theater with his artistry, innovation, and enduring legacy.

This video takes you on an inspiring journey through the life and impact of Cheng Yanqiu, one of Peking opera’s greatest masters. From humble beginnings and rigorous training to overcoming setbacks and breaking social barriers, Cheng Yanqiu developed the iconic Cheng School vocal style - a technique admired for its elegance and technical difficulty. You'll learn how he drew from both Chinese traditions and Western influences, pioneering reforms in opera performance, education, and staging. The video also explores his collaborations with mentors and students, his role in elevating the status of opera artists, and his groundbreaking work in operatic composition, choreography, and teaching.

Delve into the unique differences between Peking opera and Western opera, the history of amateur and professional performers, and how Cheng Yanqiu’s innovations shaped new generations of artists. See how his signature works, like “The Lucky Purse,” continue to captivate audiences and inspire adaptations, blending traditional techniques with modern experimentation. Through rare stories, cultural context, and contemporary perspectives, this documentary offers a comprehensive look at Cheng Yanqiu’s contribution to Chinese opera and the ongoing evolution of this treasured art form.

title: An Aria from the Peking Opera "The Unicorn Purse 锁麟囊" (Translated into Filipino)
description:
CREDITS • PASASALAMAT
(1) Much thanks to Nikhi Chau for the original English translation which was the  basis of my translation into Tagalog/Filipino. The said translation may be found here:   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rENUrYQQqqw
(2) The video I translated in this video is different but is performed by the same actress, Zhang Huoding. The video I translated may be accessed here:    https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2RbBnUt2TaM
(3) I don't claim any of the credits to the work aside from the Tagalog translation, all original content creators retain their rights to the materials used.

For my Filipino audiences, you might notice that the language used here is a bit uncommon. This is because I wanted to keep the poeticism of Nikhi's original translation and the Peking Opera play itself, and I found that to maintain the cadence of the original play, it would be best to emulate the style of classical Tagalog (or rather my attempt to sound like it), which is what you see here.

TRANSLATOR'S NOTES
IMPORTANT NOTE: This translation uses a lot of equivocations (some phrases are hard to translate into Filipino).

1. "Kubol ng Tagsibol at Taglagas," my amateur translation of "Spring and Autumn Pavilion" in the sense that "kubol" means shelter from rain. Tagalog, and by extension, the Philippines does not have pavilions the way China does.

2. "mamahaling lukbutan" refers to a Qilin purse. A Qilin is a Chinese creature which resembles a unicorn, hence, "the Unicorn Purse," and part of Xue Xiangling's trousseau is a Qilin-embroidered purse believe to expedite the arrival of a talented son, which she gives to Zhao Shouzhen on the day of their wedidngs.

3. "tubusin ang utang na loob," creative localization of "repay a debt." Literally, it means "to pay off a debt of gratitude."

4. "kapatirin" means "take one as a sibling."

5.  "Ano't kay paurong-sulong naman nitong buhay (How forward-backward is this life!)" is my attempt to translate "How unexpected is this life!"  Paurong-sulong in this sense means something like "how filled with downs and ups is this life!"

6. "di-masuungang along magiting," literally "fearsome waves which cannot be traversed"

7. "pagmamabuting minsan kong itinanim..." the original Chinese and Nikhi's translation uses a phrase which means "fragrant peach." Now, if the word "peach" had a direct translation to Tagalog I'd have used the phrase in its entirety. However, peaches are not native to the Philippines and, in trying to emulate classical Tagalog, I intentionally kept the number of Spanish loanwords down, which is why I used "pagmamabuti (benevolence)".

title: Peking Opera “Da Deng Dian” —— 湖北京剧院“云上京韵”优秀剧目线上展演（3）大登殿
description:
湖北省京剧院 简介
Hubei Provincial Peking Opera Theatre
湖北武汉是中国九省通衢之地，从20世纪30年代起京剧演出在此风靡，被称为中国京剧的三大码头，京剧重镇。成立于1970年的湖北省京剧院异军突起，逐渐成为湖北京剧的领军，湖北文化的品牌，并于2005年被国家文化部命名为国家重点京剧院团，于2008年被国务院命名为国家非物质文化遗产（京剧）保护单位。
As a "thoroughfare to nine provinces" in Hubei, China, Wuhan has witnessed the popularity of Peking opera since 1930s and is known as one of the "Three Wharves of Peking Opera" and a key city for Peking opera in China. Founded in 1970, Hubei Provincial Peking Opera Theatre has developed rapidly and has become the leader of Peking opera circle in Hubei as well as a culture brand of Hubei. It was titled "National Key Peking Opera Troupe" by China's Ministry of Culture in 2005.
建院五十多年来该院继承京剧的优秀传统，上演了优秀传统剧目300余出，并致力于改革创新。创作演出了《徐九经升官记》等众多优秀剧目，屡获国家文华大奖、中国戏曲学会奖、全国优秀保留剧目大奖、中国京剧艺术节金奖、银奖，国家舞台艺术精品工程十大精品剧目等荣誉。
In the five decades after the foundation of the theatre, it has carried forward the fine tradition of Peking opera. More than 200 excellent traditional operas have been staged in this theatre, and has been bestowed with many honors, such as the National Wenhua Award, Award of Association of Chinese Theatre Arts, Grand Award of Excellent Repertoire in China, Golden Award and Silver Award of China Peking Opera Art Festival, and Award of Top Ten Fine Operas of National Fine Stage Arts Program.
剧院拥有一批艺术造诣很高的艺术家，如著名京剧表演艺术家朱世慧，以及梅花奖获得者裴咏杰、王小蝉、万晓慧等。剧院可谓人才辈出、流派纷呈、阵容整齐、实力雄厚。
The theatre boasts of a number of artists with profound attainments, for example, the famous Peking opera performing artist Zhu Shihui, and the Plum Performance Award winner Pei Yongjie and Wang Xiaochan, as well as CCTV Young Peking Opera Actor Contest Gold Medalist Wan Xiaohui, Tan Yuan, Wang Ming and Tang Kai, etc. The performers of the Theatre are all professional of Peking Opera, having been trained for years with the four skills of Peking opera including singing, dialogue, dancing and martial art. Singing is utilized to intensify the appeal of the art by all kinds of tones. Dialogue is the complement of singing which is full of musical and rhythm sensation. Dancing refers to the body movements requiring high performing skills. Martial art is the combination and transformation of traditional Chinese combat exercises with dances. 
剧院屡赴欧洲、澳洲、美国、俄罗斯、日本、加拿大、韩国、埃及、智利、香港、澳门、台湾等国家和地区演出，蜚声海外，是一个深受国内外观众喜爱，具有较高艺术水准的京剧艺术表演团体。
The theatre has once sent performing groups to many countries and regions, such as the USA, Russia, Canada, Australia, Italy, Japan, South Korea, Malta, and Mauritius.

title: Bàwáng Yìn (霸王印) - Seal of the Conqueror | Epic Peking Opera Fusion
description:
Welcome to Global Audio Lab. Prepare for a theatrical and explosive musical experience with "Bàwáng Yìn" (Seal of the Conqueror), a track that brings the raw power of traditional Peking Opera (Jingju / 京剧) into the modern era of epic cinematic music.

In stark contrast to softer opera styles, Peking Opera is fierce, colorful, and commanding. This track features the signature high-pitched, piercing sound of the Jinghu (a traditional two-stringed fiddle), aggressive dramatic gongs, and the rhythmic wooden clappers (Bangu). Fused with heavy, modern cinematic bass, this song tells the tragic and heroic tale of an ancient conqueror facing his destiny.

description:
The Beauty of Peking Opera: Spotlight on Hua Dan 🎭 | #Shorts #BeijingOpera #ChineseCulture ✨

This video shows the makeup and costume features of the flower girl of Peking Opera, allowing you to enjoy the charm of traditional Chinese opera. Hua Dan is a female character in Peking Opera who plays the role of a lively, cheerful and gentle woman, often in red color, highlighting the contour of the eyes and the lips. The costumes of Hua Dan are gorgeous and colorful, often made of silk, brocade and other textures, with common styles such as lapel gowns and waistcoats. This video takes you into the world of Chinese aesthetics to appreciate the artistic charm of Peking Opera Hua Dan.

title: 《霸王别姬》中英文双语字幕精简版Classic Peking Opera Farewell My Concubine: Concise English Subtitles
description:
一曲霸王别姬，千古英雄绝唱。
经典京剧剧目《霸王别姬》中英文字幕精简版
主演：许翠 许立仁 于帅 裘识
One song of Farewell My Concubine, an eternal hero's swan song.
Classic Peking Opera Farewell My Concubine: Concise English Subtitles
Starring: Xu Cui, Xu Liren, Yu Shuai, Qiu Shi

京剧《霸王别姬》是京剧艺术大师梅兰芳表演的梅派经典名剧之一。该剧目讲述了楚汉相争，西楚霸王项羽刚愎自用，神勇寡谋，听信汉臣李左车诈降之言，不纳虞姬众将之谏，起兵伐汉。大军至九里山，又中韩信十面埋伏之计，被困垓下，时楚歌四起，楚军将士顿起思乡之情，军心涣散。项羽知大势已去，抚骓长叹，慷慨悲歌，虞姬置酒舞剑，噙泪相慰。汉军四面攻来，项羽欲携虞姬突围，虞姬恐成其所累，拔剑自尽。项羽兵败，自愧无颜面见江东父老，自刎乌江。刘邦遂定汉室天下。
有幸成为京剧推荐官，对京剧文化喜爱已久，精彩的历史故事，独特的节奏韵律，丰富的脸谱造型，酷炫的舞台动作……这次完整的看完《霸王别姬》，剪辑分享的时候发现，如果观众没有字幕，由于京剧唱腔、韵辙、古汉语用法表达等因素，相关的背景故事、成语、人物关系等，视频很难全部听清唱词，更不用说国际朋友了，所以剪辑的时候不断磨耳朵，逐字逐句分析，查阅相关资料，把整场戏配上双语字幕。Peking Opera Farewell My Concubine is one of the signature masterpieces of the Mei School, performed by the grand master of Peking Opera, Mei Lanfang. The play tells the story of the Chu-Han Contention: Xiang Yu, the Hegemon-King of Western Chu, is stubborn and overconfident, brave in battle but poor in strategy. He falls for the false surrender of Han minister Li Zuoche, ignores the advice of Yu Ji and his generals, and marches to attack the Han forces. At Nine Mile Mountain, his army is ambushed in Han Xin’s Ten-Sided Trap and surrounded at Gaixia. As Chu songs echo from all sides, homesickness grips the soldiers, and morale collapses. Realizing the end is near, Xiang Yu strokes his steed and sighs, singing a heroic lament. Yu Ji pours wine, dances with her sword, and comforts him through tears. As Han troops close in, Xiang Yu tries to break out with Yu Ji, but fearing she will burden him, she draws her sword and takes her own life. Defeated, Xiang Yu, ashamed to face the elders of Jiangdong, ends his life at the Wu River. Liu Bang thus establishes the Han dynasty.
It’s an honor to serve as a Peking Opera recommender—I’ve long been passionate about this art form. The captivating historical tales, unique rhythms and rhymes, vibrant facial makeup, and dazzling stage movements… After watching the full Farewell My Concubine this time, I realized while editing and sharing clips that without subtitles, viewers—especially international friends—struggle to follow the lyrics. The stylized singing, rhyming patterns, and classical Chinese make it hard to catch the backstory, idioms, and character relationships. So, I listened intently, analyzed line by line, consulted references, and added bilingual subtitles to the entire performance.

title: 弘揚中華傳統京劇藝術 — 香江國粹情2025 / Embracing Peking Opera – China Peking Opera Foundation 2025 Hong Kong Tour
description:
【弘揚中華傳統京劇藝術 — 香江國粹情2025】 

京劇是中華民族的優秀傳統藝術，也是人類戲劇文化中濃縮中國傳統道德精華、蘊含中華審美智慧的一朵奇葩。京劇通過「角兒」唱念做打的真功夫表演，展現文武兼備的真本領及生旦淨丑的獨特魅力。京劇的傳承與推廣是馬會支持香港打造「中外文化藝術交流中心」的重要實踐之一。

今年馬會繼續支持「香江國粹情2025中國京劇藝術基金會香港行」活動，以藝術為橋樑，加強內地與香港的文化聯繫，讓京劇這一國粹藝術成為增強文化認同的重要載體，豐富市民的文化生活。

 [Embracing Peking Opera – China Peking Opera Foundation 2025 Hong Kong Tour]

Peking Opera is a distinguished Chinese national traditional art form and a unique gem in the history of world theatre. It is a treasure trove of traditional Chinese culture and aesthetic wisdom, standing as a brilliant cultural treasure. Peking Opera is truly the art of the stars, aims to demonstrate the full artistry of Peking Opera – its mastery of singing, recitation, acting and fight; its seamless balance of civil and martial skills; and the breathtaking brilliance of the roles of sheng, dan, jing, and chou. The promotion and inheritance of Peking Opera is an important practice supported by The Hong Kong Jockey Club to establish “East-meets-West centre for international cultural exchange”.

This year, the Club is proud to continue its support for the “Hong Kong National Essence - 2025 China Peking Opera Art Foundation Hong Kong Tour”. By celebrating Peking Opera as a national treasure, the initiative strengthens cultural ties between the Mainland and Hong Kong, fosters a shared sense of cultural identity, and enriches the cultural life of the community.

title: Peking Opera:《A Match of Spring and Autumn》京剧: 春秋配 (1/2), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Helloo everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://tv.cctv.com/2019/09/22/VIDERxJlfhexOjZY4HVpdx4N190922.shtml
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

The second opera of 2026! "A Match of Spring and Autumn" is a classic staple of Peking opera. It tells the tale of the maiden Jiang Qiulian (姜秋莲) and the scholar Li Chunfa (李春发). Their names, Qiulian literally means 'autumn lotus,' and Chunfa literally means 'spring prosperity.' Hence, this play is called 春秋配 (literally 'spring autumn pair') as it tells the story of their two fates and how they became entangled with each other.

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

4:14 "Nanny" is how I've translated 乳娘 (rǔ niáng), which actually means wet nurse. She is Qiulian's wet nurse from when she was a baby.

11:43 "Golden peace," (金安) is a traditional Chinese respectful term, used to express blessings to elders in greetings and letters.

15:19 Some info on courtesy names - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Courtesy_name

15:41 'Jinshi' (进士) is the highest rank obtainable from the imperial examination system.

29:12 君子 (jūnzǐ) is often translated as 'gentleman.' This translation works, but doesn't really convey what junzi actually means. Junzi is a Chinese philosophical term about an 'ideal moral' person. For more info - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Junzi

45:14 秀才 (xiùcái) is a low-ranking title for those who had passed the 院试/college exams, an exam above the country-level exams in the imperial examination system. Xiucais are the second lowest rank in the exam system, and have the privileges of sitting the provincial-level exams to increase their rank.

49:08 I've translated as 弱冠 (ruò guàn) as "came of age." Ruò guàn refers to the act of having completed 冠礼 (guàn lǐ, literally meaning 'hat rite'), a coming of age ceremony for young men during which they receive their 冠 (guàn, hat/cap/crown) and are considered adults. More info - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guan_Li

57:27 An derivative of the idiom 萍水相逢 (píng shuǐ xiāng féng), meaning "for the duckweed to meet." Duckweed gathers and scatters on the water with uncertainly; it is an idiom expressing the rare fate of two strangers meeting by chance.

title: Peking Opera The Precious Pouch Conveys Kindness and Karma
description:
The 1st China Plum Blossom Award International Drama Festival has grandly kicked off in Shanghai. As one of the opening highlights, the iconic Cheng-school Peking Opera The Precious Pouch is led by Plum Blossom Award winner Chi Xiaoqiu. 

It tells the story of Xue Xiangling, a wealthy young lady, who meets a poor girl Zhao Shouzhen amid a rainstorm on her wedding day. Out of compassion, Xue gives Zhao a jewel-filled pouch. Years later, fortunes reverse: a destitute Xue encounters Zhao, now a wealthy noblewoman. Recognized by the pouch, Xue is saved—kindness begets kindness. 

With melodious, subtle vocals and refined lyrics, this masterpiece embodies Eastern virtues of generosity and gratitude, showcasing the profound charm of Cheng-school Peking Opera.

本專輯榮獲台灣金鼎獎，收錄1950年代珍貴歷史錄音。

title: An Aria from the Peking Opera "Legend of the White Snake 白蛇传" (Translated into Filipino)
description:
CREDITS • PASASALAMAT
(1) Much thanks to Nikhi Chau for the original English translation which was the  basis of my translation into Tagalog/Filipino. The said translation may be found here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UDO-PbJaOSs
(2) The video I translated in this video is different but is performed by the same actress, Zhang Huoding. The video I translated may be accessed here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vIzH8UUDPDE
(3) I don't claim any of the credits to the work aside from the Tagalog translation, all original content creators retain their rights to the materials used.

For my Filipino audiences, you might notice that the language used here is a bit uncommon. This is because I wanted to keep the poeticism of Nikhi's original translation and the Peking Opera play itself, and I found that to maintain the cadence of the original play, it would be best to emulate the style of classical Tagalog (or rather my attempt to sound like it), which is what you see here.

TRANSLATOR'S NOTES
1. "painumin ng matapang na alak sa piging ng Duanyang (had me drink strong liquor on the feast of Duanyang)" refers to Xu Xian making Bai Suzhen ingest a type of realgar wine ("xionghuang") which makes her reveal her true snake form. Realgar nor "xionghuang" doesn't have a direct Filipino translation so I equivocated the liquor's name to "matapang na alak (strong liquor)."

2. "sa iyong pamamanata sa Bituing Kambal, (by your oath to the Twin Stars)" Bituing Kambal (Twin Stars) is my closest equivocation to Chinese 双星 (shuangxing). Nikhi uses "Double Stars." 

3. "bago sundan si Fahai sa kaniyang panuluyan (before following Fahai to his chambers)," the gist of this line and its preceding ones is how Xu Xian swore to the Twin Stars (a Chinese cultural symbol for love) before ultimately "abandoning" Bai Suzhen to go to Fahai's temple. "Panuluyan" here means chambers and is my closest equivocation to "meditation hall." A closer yet less poetic translation is "bulwagan sa pagninilay."

4. "walang pakundangan sa ating pagmamabutihan (without regard for our shared benevolence)." The original translation uses "to say nothing of," which, frankly, I had a hard time finding a suitable equivalent to in Filipino. I settled with "walang pakundangan (without regard for)." On the word "pagmamabutihan," Nikhi uses the word "common kindness."

5. "...ligtas at walang pakialam (unharmed and indifferent)." This is an equivocation to the original passage "you looked on from the mountainside with your hands up your sleeve," (which, frankly is hard to equal in Filipino) and states that Suzhen thinks Xu Xian was indifferent to her suffering.

6. "gaano katibay ang iyong bituka upang ako'y masikmurang harapin? (lit. how strong is your gut for you to stomach facing me today?)" This is a creative localization of the phrase "have you no shame, coming to see me?"  "Matibay ang sikmura" is a Filipino idiom that means "able to stomach anything." Bituka means gut.

title: Peking Opera:《Sun An Presents a Statement》京剧: 孙安动本 (2/2), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Hello everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://www.bilibili.com/video/BV1Eb411S7o8/?spm_id_from=333.337.search-card.all.click
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

This is the second half of "Sun An Presents a Statement." You might want to watch the first half if you haven't, otherwise you'll probably be very confused. Anyywayyss, the end is nigh–for Sun An, or for injustice? Who knows, let's find out :)

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

1:03 Imperial Chinese palaces/magistrates/courts have a drum that anyone can strike to summon authorities in case of emergencies or to report injustices.

4:43 As with many cultures, death is a very taboo subject. This is even more so when around the Emperor.

6:36 明察秋毫 (míng chá qiū háo) is an idiom to express to pay attention and to see the truth, to look closely and diligently as if able to distinguish the different furs and feathers of autumn animals.

9:35 国公 (guó gōng) is a hereditary title (Duke) the Emperor bestows to worthy subjects. 定国公 (dìngguó gōng) literally means 'Duke who pacifies/stabilises/upholds the country.'

11:38 NOT Zhong Cong's Mausoleum. This is an actual late Emperor's Mausoleum.

15:29 天子 (tiānzǐ) "Son of Heaven" is a title referring to the Emperor, because the Emperor supposedly holds the Mandate of Heaven which gives him the right to rule all beneath the Heavens.

28:45 御林军 (yùlín jūn) are the armed forces that protect and serve the imperial family.

title: 《春闺梦.被纠缠》张火丁 Peking Opera《The Dream in lady's chamber》
description:
Background:
A young lady waited her husband to come back from war, and fell asleep because of the tiredness worrying about her man's life. What she didn't know is that her man, who was forced to enter the army just a couple months after their wedding, died already in the front from arrow attacks.

In the dream, her husband came back safely. They were extremely happy and obviously excited after separating from each other for a while.

This scene comes right at this moment.

The actress is  a third generation successor of the Peking opera master CHENG Yanqiu. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cheng_Yanqiu
She is also one of the very best Peking opera actress following the representation style of CHENG, which is famous for vocal resonant technique in the part of hindbrain. This is quite different from the French and Italien opera.

title: Peking Opera:《Mu Guiying Breaks Through the Heaven's Gate》京剧 穆桂英大破天门阵 (2/2), [Chinese Opera Eng Sub]
description:
Helloo everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://tv.cctv.com/2022/07/06/VIDE1BuihoeyYc00ugRTnQBN220706.shtml
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

This is the second half of "Mu Guiying Breaks Through the Heaven's Gate Formation". Feel free to check out the first half, otherwise the story may not make much sense :D 

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

12:40 Xiao and Song are the ruling families of the Liao and Song dynasties who are at war.

26:21 木 (mu) + 易 (Yi) = 杨 (Yang)

41:36 Imperial Uncle (国舅, guó jiù) is a title referring to the emperor's maternal uncle or the brother of the emperor's principal wife, and sometimes the brothers of favoured concubines.

43:24 - 45:40 This whole section is in reference to the eight trigrams of Bagua: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bagua

52:56 This is referencing another story in which Mu Guiying, while pregnant, lead an army to save the city of Hongzhou from Liao troops. This happened after her meeting with Yang Zongbao but before the battle of the Heaven's Gate Formation.


Alternative Versions:
This opera is only one rendition of the story. A more common version of the story goes;  Mu Guiying let Yang Zongbao go after he agreed to her marriage proposal. When Yang Zongbao returned to camp and reported what happened to his father (Yang Yanzhao), his father was furious and ordered his disgraced son to be executed. To save Yang Zongbao, Mu Guiying came down to the camp and duelled Yang Yanzhao for Yang Zongbao's life. Yang Yanzhao could not win, and let his son live. Mu Guiying then offered herself to helping Song defeat Liao.

There are also versions in which Mu Guiying comes to rescue Yang Zongbao, but negotiates instead of fighting, because everyone is so deathly terrified of her.

description:

On 13 April 2024, the third Mother Of Mercy Concert and the first Australian International Taoist Culture Festival were held at The Concourse, Chatswood. This event, themed around the "Taoist Mythological Tales of the Classic of Mountains and Seas", showcased the profound charm and mysterious aspects of Chinese Taoist culture.


Since 2022, the Australian Cultural Education Association has successfully held three editions of the Mother Of Mercy Concert, each time receiving widespread acclaim for its unique artistic allure and deep cultural significance. This year's event was orchestrated by Ms. Yan Meixi, an enthusiastic promoter of Chinese culture and a devoted follower of traditional music and arts, who aims to foster a harmonious society through the wisdom of Taoist culture.


The programme featured spectacular performances including lion dancing, Peking opera, and Taoist music, along with outstanding presentations by national-level artists, all exploring the mysteries of the "Classic of Mountains and Seas". Join us for this audio-visual feast to experience the harmony and balance of life, feel the motherly love in the world, and delve into the profound and expansive Taoist culture.


Don't forget to subscribe to our channel for more content about Chinese culture and the arts!


2024年4月13日在The Concourse, Chatswood举办的第三届母娘慈爱音乐晚会及首届澳大利亚国际道教文化节。本次活动以“道家神话故事之山海经”为主题，展现了中国道家文化的深刻魅力和神秘色彩。


从2022年起，澳大利亚文化教育协会连续成功举办了三届母娘慈爱音乐晚会，每一次都以其独特的艺术魅力和深刻的文化内涵赢得了观众的广泛赞誉。今年的活动由中华文化的热忱弘扬者阎美希女士策划与组织，她是一位对传统音乐与艺术充满热爱的追寻者，致力于通过道家文化的智慧引领和谐社会的构建。


节目包括了舞狮、京剧、道乐等精彩表演，以及多位国家级艺术家的杰出演出，共同探寻“山海经”中的奥秘。让我们通过这场视听盛宴，体验生活的和谐与平衡，感受到世间母亲的慈爱，以及道家文化的深邃与博大。


不要忘记订阅我们的频道，享受更多关于中国文化与艺术的内容！

title: Becoming the Monkey King: A boy’s harsh Beijing opera training | Kids on the Silk Road (10/10)
description:
Watch all episodes here: https://bit.ly/kids_on_the_silk_road

11-year old Yang from Beijing plays the leading role as the Monkey King at the famous Chinese Opera. But his passion for this traditional art form is misunderstood by his peers. In the attempt to fit in at school, he invites three classmates to the premiere. Will they now understand his fascination with the myth of the Monkey King, and accept him as a friend?

The Silk Road was an ancient network of trade routes that were central to cultural interaction connecting the East and West. It created curiosity among cultures, for example with tea and silk from China suddenly sold in Venice. The trading may be a thing of the past, but the culture is still there… We chose the Silk Road because we want to show the kids how colorful the world is and to create understanding and curiosity of the different cultures. What if we all had less prejudice and more curiosity? Would the world be more colorful and less hierarchical? Would there be more empathy? Could we in the West try to learn from other cultures?

title: Classic Peking Opera经典京剧脸谱《是唱脸谱》，蓝脸的窦尔敦，盗御马，红脸的关公战长沙，黄脸的典韦，白脸的曹操，黑脸的张飞，叫喳喳
description:
京剧《说唱脸谱》里唱到：“蓝脸的窦尔敦，盗御马，红脸的关公战长沙，黄脸的典韦，白脸的曹操，黑脸的张飞，叫喳喳……”关公，曹操，典韦我们都知道，都是《三国》里的人物，那窦尔敦是谁？

京剧脸谱

在京剧的脸谱里，每种颜色代表不同的人物形象和性格。红脸代表忠义、血性，比如关羽；黑脸代表正直，严肃，比如张飞；白脸代表奸诈、多疑，比如曹操；黄脸代表勇猛、暴躁，比如典韦；蓝脸代表刚直，桀骜，多是些绿林好汉，比如本文要讲的窦尔敦。

窦尔敦京剧形象

窦尔敦是京剧《盗御马》里的人物，在评书《连环套》、公案小说《彭公案》和《施公案》里也出现过。由于不同人的创作和艺术表现需要，关于窦尔敦的身世和结局各个版本都有所不同，在这里我以单田芳的评书《连环套》为蓝本结合其它文学作品里的窦尔敦形象

In the Beijing opera "Speaking and Singing with Facial Masks," it sings: "Du Er Dun with blue face, stealing imperial horses; Guan Yu with red face fights in Changsha; Dian Wei with yellow face; Cao Cao with white face; Zhang Fei with black face, making a clamor..." We know about Guan Yu, Cao Cao, and Dian Wei from the "Three Kingdoms," but who is Du Er Dun?

In Beijing opera facial masks, each color represents a different character and personality. Red symbolizes loyalty and valor, such as Guan Yu; black represents integrity and seriousness, like Zhang Fei; white signifies deceitfulness and suspicion, like Cao Cao; yellow represents bravery and impulsiveness, like Dian Wei; blue signifies forthrightness and assertiveness, often portraying righteous outlaws, like the character Du Er Dun in this context.

Du Er Dun's Character in Beijing Opera

Du Er Dun is a character from the Beijing opera "Stealing the Imperial Horses" and also appears in storytelling series like "The Interlocking Trap," detective novels such as "Peng's Case," and "Shi's Case." Due to variations in different creators' interpretations and artistic expressions, there are differences in Du Er Dun's background and fate across various versions. Here, I'll base his portrayal on the storytelling series by Shan Tianfang, "The Interlocking Trap," while incorporating elements from other literary works portraying Du Er Dun.

title: Peking Opera Highlights EP2 —— 湖北京剧院“云上京韵”优秀剧目线上展演（2）京剧演唱会 下
description:
湖北省京剧院 简介
Hubei Provincial Peking Opera Theatre
湖北武汉是中国九省通衢之地，从20世纪30年代起京剧演出在此风靡，被称为中国京剧的三大码头，京剧重镇。成立于1970年的湖北省京剧院异军突起，逐渐成为湖北京剧的领军，湖北文化的品牌，并于2005年被国家文化部命名为国家重点京剧院团，于2008年被国务院命名为国家非物质文化遗产（京剧）保护单位。
As a "thoroughfare to nine provinces" in Hubei, China, Wuhan has witnessed the popularity of Peking opera since 1930s and is known as one of the "Three Wharves of Peking Opera" and a key city for Peking opera in China. Founded in 1970, Hubei Provincial Peking Opera Theatre has developed rapidly and has become the leader of Peking opera circle in Hubei as well as a culture brand of Hubei. It was titled "National Key Peking Opera Troupe" by China's Ministry of Culture in 2005.
建院五十多年来该院继承京剧的优秀传统，上演了优秀传统剧目300余出，并致力于改革创新。创作演出了《徐九经升官记》等众多优秀剧目，屡获国家文华大奖、中国戏曲学会奖、全国优秀保留剧目大奖、中国京剧艺术节金奖、银奖，国家舞台艺术精品工程十大精品剧目等荣誉。
In the five decades after the foundation of the theatre, it has carried forward the fine tradition of Peking opera. More than 200 excellent traditional operas have been staged in this theatre, and has been bestowed with many honors, such as the National Wenhua Award, Award of Association of Chinese Theatre Arts, Grand Award of Excellent Repertoire in China, Golden Award and Silver Award of China Peking Opera Art Festival, and Award of Top Ten Fine Operas of National Fine Stage Arts Program.
剧院拥有一批艺术造诣很高的艺术家，如著名京剧表演艺术家朱世慧，以及梅花奖获得者裴咏杰、王小蝉、万晓慧等。剧院可谓人才辈出、流派纷呈、阵容整齐、实力雄厚。
The theatre boasts of a number of artists with profound attainments, for example, the famous Peking opera performing artist Zhu Shihui, and the Plum Performance Award winner Pei Yongjie and Wang Xiaochan, as well as CCTV Young Peking Opera Actor Contest Gold Medalist Wan Xiaohui, Tan Yuan, Wang Ming and Tang Kai, etc. The performers of the Theatre are all professional of Peking Opera, having been trained for years with the four skills of Peking opera including singing, dialogue, dancing and martial art. Singing is utilized to intensify the appeal of the art by all kinds of tones. Dialogue is the complement of singing which is full of musical and rhythm sensation. Dancing refers to the body movements requiring high performing skills. Martial art is the combination and transformation of traditional Chinese combat exercises with dances. 
剧院屡赴欧洲、澳洲、美国、俄罗斯、日本、加拿大、韩国、埃及、智利、香港、澳门、台湾等国家和地区演出，蜚声海外，是一个深受国内外观众喜爱，具有较高艺术水准的京剧艺术表演团体。
The theatre has once sent performing groups to many countries and regions, such as the USA, Russia, Canada, Australia, Italy, Japan, South Korea, Malta, and Mauritius.

The fourth opera of 2026; "A Spring Boudoir's Dream" is here! This is a one-act opera tells the story of a the newly-wed Lady Zhang's wait for her husband's return from war. The story doesn't have a long epic complicated plot line, but is nonetheless a very tragically beautiful play, exploring themes including love, war, helplessness, and the fragility of life.

I actually translated this one quite a while ago but never came around to uploading it. Please excuse the messy subtitles.

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

20:22 "Sister" here is a translation of 嫂嫂 (sǎosǎo), being a respectful term for one married woman to refer to another.
 
35:33 "furnace" here is a translation of 薰笼 (xūn lóng), a frame placed over a brazier for drying things. It can also mean prison, or a situation difficult to escape from–it's a pun!

35:47 Begonias can represent homesickness and longing in Chinese culture.

47:56 Spring night here has a double meaning, if you know, you know.
