title: Hotpot Talk: Why a Swede Learned Peking Opera？火锅坦白局：瑞典人为啥会学京剧？
description:
🧐Can you imagine a Swedish actor🇸🇪 traveling alone to China🇨🇳—without speaking Chinese—simply because of his admiration for kung fu icon Jackie Chan, and dedicating more than a decade to learning Peking Opera and promoting Chinese culture? 🌹Driven by his passion, Timothy has brought Peking Opera to Sweden, introducing it to local audiences and even carrying a lion dance head through the streets of Stockholm, allowing more people to experience the richness of Chinese culture. 🎬 In this episode, me sits down with Swedish actor Timothy over a creative, modern take on traditional old Beijing hotpot. Through food and conversation, Timothy shares his personal journey with Peking Opera and his long-standing commitment to cultural exchange between China and Sweden. Join us for this heartwarming episode, where food warms the body and stories bring cultures closer together.
你敢相信吗，一个🇸🇪瑞典人只因为对中国🇨🇳功夫巨星成龙的喜爱，就只身一人在不会中文的情况下来到中国学习戏曲，并且坚持十几年如一日传播中国文化。他将中国京剧带到瑞典，在斯德哥尔摩街头举起舞狮狮头，让更多瑞典人见到中国文化。在本期视频中，我将用一顿充满创新创意的新派老北京火锅和瑞典演员天木换一段京剧人生故事。一起走进这期暖胃又暖心的视频吧。

title: Peking Opera Highlights EP1 —— 湖北京剧院“云上京韵”优秀剧目线上展演（1）京剧演唱会 上
description:
湖北省京剧院 简介
Hubei Provincial Peking Opera Theatre
湖北武汉是中国九省通衢之地，从20世纪30年代起京剧演出在此风靡，被称为中国京剧的三大码头，京剧重镇。成立于1970年的湖北省京剧院异军突起，逐渐成为湖北京剧的领军，湖北文化的品牌，并于2005年被国家文化部命名为国家重点京剧院团，于2008年被国务院命名为国家非物质文化遗产（京剧）保护单位。
As a "thoroughfare to nine provinces" in Hubei, China, Wuhan has witnessed the popularity of Peking opera since 1930s and is known as one of the "Three Wharves of Peking Opera" and a key city for Peking opera in China. Founded in 1970, Hubei Provincial Peking Opera Theatre has developed rapidly and has become the leader of Peking opera circle in Hubei as well as a culture brand of Hubei. It was titled "National Key Peking Opera Troupe" by China's Ministry of Culture in 2005.
建院五十多年来该院继承京剧的优秀传统，上演了优秀传统剧目300余出，并致力于改革创新。创作演出了《徐九经升官记》等众多优秀剧目，屡获国家文华大奖、中国戏曲学会奖、全国优秀保留剧目大奖、中国京剧艺术节金奖、银奖，国家舞台艺术精品工程十大精品剧目等荣誉。
In the five decades after the foundation of the theatre, it has carried forward the fine tradition of Peking opera. More than 200 excellent traditional operas have been staged in this theatre, and has been bestowed with many honors, such as the National Wenhua Award, Award of Association of Chinese Theatre Arts, Grand Award of Excellent Repertoire in China, Golden Award and Silver Award of China Peking Opera Art Festival, and Award of Top Ten Fine Operas of National Fine Stage Arts Program.
剧院拥有一批艺术造诣很高的艺术家，如著名京剧表演艺术家朱世慧，以及梅花奖获得者裴咏杰、王小蝉、万晓慧等。剧院可谓人才辈出、流派纷呈、阵容整齐、实力雄厚。
The theatre boasts of a number of artists with profound attainments, for example, the famous Peking opera performing artist Zhu Shihui, and the Plum Performance Award winner Pei Yongjie and Wang Xiaochan, as well as CCTV Young Peking Opera Actor Contest Gold Medalist Wan Xiaohui, Tan Yuan, Wang Ming and Tang Kai, etc. The performers of the Theatre are all professional of Peking Opera, having been trained for years with the four skills of Peking opera including singing, dialogue, dancing and martial art. Singing is utilized to intensify the appeal of the art by all kinds of tones. Dialogue is the complement of singing which is full of musical and rhythm sensation. Dancing refers to the body movements requiring high performing skills. Martial art is the combination and transformation of traditional Chinese combat exercises with dances. 
剧院屡赴欧洲、澳洲、美国、俄罗斯、日本、加拿大、韩国、埃及、智利、香港、澳门、台湾等国家和地区演出，蜚声海外，是一个深受国内外观众喜爱，具有较高艺术水准的京剧艺术表演团体。
The theatre has once sent performing groups to many countries and regions, such as the USA, Russia, Canada, Australia, Italy, Japan, South Korea, Malta, and Mauritius.

description:

2026马年「欢乐春节」海外新年戏曲演出已于2026年2月13日在多伦多历史悠久的Winter Garden Theatre圆满落幕。
本场演出由加中文化发展协会与加拿大中国戏曲艺术协会联合主办，中国银行全面战略合作支持，特邀当代京剧艺术殿堂——中国国家京剧院三十余位优秀演员与乐队演奏员倾情献演。
由京剧舞台上的黄金搭档——著名表演艺术家于魁智与李胜素领衔主演，整场演出阵容鼎盛、唱念做打俱佳，充分展现了国家级院团的艺术高度与专业水准，赢得现场观众阵阵掌声与高度赞誉。
值得一提的是，加拿大中国戏曲艺术协会的学员们也与来自中国的京剧名家同台演出，在交流与合作中传承国粹艺术，展现了海外戏曲新生力量的风采。
这场跨越山海的艺术盛会，不仅为多伦多观众带来一场高水准的文化盛宴，更在马年新春之际，谱写了中加文化交流的动人篇章，让京剧艺术在海外焕发出更加蓬勃的生命力。
The 2026 Year of the Horse “Happy Spring Festival” Overseas Chinese New Year Opera Gala was successfully held on February 13, 2026, at the historic Winter Garden Theatre in Toronto.
Co-presented by the Canada-China Cultural Development Association and the Canada Chinese Opera Arts Center, with comprehensive strategic support from the Bank of China, the event featured more than 30 distinguished performers and orchestra members from the prestigious National Peking Opera Company of China.
Headlined by the celebrated Peking Opera duo Yu Kuizhi and Li Shengsu, the performance showcased the highest artistic standards of contemporary Jingju. Their masterful singing and refined stage artistry captivated the audience and earned prolonged applause throughout the evening.
Students from the Canada Chinese Opera Arts Center also had the honor of sharing the stage with the visiting masters from China, demonstrating the vitality of overseas opera training and the ongoing transmission of this treasured art form.
More than a performance, the gala was a meaningful cultural gathering that strengthened artistic exchange and celebrated the enduring beauty of Peking Opera in Canada.

title: Peking Opera
description:
Peking Opera is China’s national treasure and one of the most influential traditional Chinese oper of the most influential traditional Chinese operas. With a history of over 200 years, it originated in the late 18th century and combines singing, reciting, acting, and acrobatics into a unique artistic form.
Performances feature elaborate costumes, vivid facial makeup (each color symbolizes a character’s personality—red for loyalty, white for treachery, black for integrity), and iconic stage props. The music is accompanied by traditional instruments like the erhu (a two-stringed fiddle) and gongs.
Classic repertoires often draw from historical stories, folk legends, and literary works, conveying values such as loyalty, filial piety, and justice. It not only reflects Chinese culture but also was inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage List in 2010.

It blends graceful singing with powerful martial arts movements, balancing feminine elegance and warrior courage.

title: Peking Opera: Why tradition is the new cool
description: For BaTeer, a 4th-generation inheritor of the Mei School of Peking Opera, the art form is the ultimate “cool” – a timeless aesthetic where tradition meets universal resonance. Having toured from Japan to Australia, he has seen how the grace of shenduan (stage movements) transcends linguistic barriers to strike a chord with global audiences. To this young artist, “cool” is synonymous with a beauty so refined it becomes an art form in every gesture, from shielding against the wind to catching the morning dew.

title: Peking Opera Horse Whip Dance 🐎 | Chinese Elegance
description:
“Peking Opera Horse Whip (京剧马鞭)” showcases a classic movement technique from Peking Opera (Beijing Opera), where a simple prop — the horse whip — is used to symbolically represent riding a horse on stage. This iconic performance element reflects the artistry and imagination of traditional Chinese opera.

✨ Through stylized gestures, precise rhythm, and expressive body coordination, the performer brings the illusion of horseback riding to life. Every movement — from lifting the whip to shifting posture — conveys speed, direction, and emotion without the need for a real horse.

💮 Rooted in traditional Chinese opera performance, the horse whip technique highlights the beauty of symbolic storytelling, refined movement, and theatrical expression. It is a perfect example of how minimal props can create vivid and dynamic stage imagery.

🔔 Subscribe for more Chinese opera and traditional performance arts, where culture, technique, and storytelling come alive on stage.

title: Peking Opera:《Xu Liang Purges the Tyrant》京剧: 徐良除霸, [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Helloo everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://www.bilibili.com/video/BV1Ps411F7Wd/?spm_id_from=333.337.search-card.all.click&vd_source=aa56d470ff18f9d6ef03e66266525b0b
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

It's the third opera of 2026! "Xu Liang Purges the Tyrant" is about Xu Liang, a peculiar-looking-but-otherwise-righteous gallant. The story follows Xu Liang as he journeys upon Oolong Port, a town under the reign of fearsome tyrant. After encountering a distraught father who's daughter had been kidnapped by the tyrant, Xu Liang decides to rid the tyrant and save the village.

This opera is a Peking opera but the main character, Xu Liang performs as if in a Shanxi clapper opera, thus creating an interesting performance of a mix between two opera genres. Xu Liang is the son of Xu Qing, a character from the traditional novel, The Seven Heroes and Five Gallants (七俠五義) and its sequal, The Five Younger Gallants (小五義), the latter in which Xu Liang is also in. 

Blurb Translation:
In Oolong Port, Gao Xie, an evil tyrant who runs a shady inn, tries to forcibly take the commoner woman Wang Yanfang as his concubine. Her father goes to the county magistrate to plead his case but is driven away by corrupt officials when he coincidentally chances upon Xu Liang, a good man who is headed to the capital in search of his father. Upon hearing what happened, Xu Liang is filled with righteous indignation and rallies the people to overthrow the bandits and thus purge the tyrant. This play is a pioneering achievement. Xu Liang's makeup is peculiar, with glued-on white-eyebrows. His spoken dialogue (niànbái 念白) is said using Shanxi dialect and he sings in Shanxi clapper opera (shānxī bāngzǐ 山西梆子). As the civilians participating in the fight are all farmers, they primarily use farming tools as their weapons, leading to novel fighting routines. The original play belongs to the unique repertoire of the Ye school (Yè pài 叶派) martial chou (wǔ chǒu 武丑). This play has undergone significant modifications in both its concept and stage design.

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

17:33 "Westie" or 老西儿 (lǎo xī'er) is a term referencing people from Shanxi.

17:56 Wei Zhen is a character from The Five Younger Gallants (小五義).

20:41 It's Bao Zheng! Yes, the same one from Qin Xianglian.

23:50 A Peng/Pengkun is a mythological bird: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peng_(mythology)

27:41 Jie should be Xie 0_0

45:52 All famous wine names.

56:52 Please save me from translating wordplay ;-;

1:21:06 omg that should be "to stay"

1:22:15 Gao Jie's family name 高, literally means "tall," his name is literally 'tall Xie.' Ai = 矮 = short. Xie (in a different tone) = crab. Therefore, Xu Liang is basically saying "I'm gonna get every tall Xie, short Xie, and crab.
Please save me from wordplay.

title: Peking Opera:《Mu Guiying Breaks Through the Heaven's Gate》京剧 穆桂英大破天门阵 (1/2), [Chinese Opera Eng Sub]
description:
Helloo everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://tv.cctv.com/2022/07/06/VIDE1BuihoeyYc00ugRTnQBN220706.shtml
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

Welcome to the sixth opera of 2026; "Mu Guiying Breaks Through the Heaven's Gate Formation". The title was so long, I had to shorten it in the video title. If you're familiar with any of the Yang Family operas (Mu Guiying Takes Command, Silang Visits His Mother, etc) you would have heard of this story before. This opera tells the story of Mu Guiying, the only person to have broke through the Heaven's Gate Formation. To defeat neighbouring Liao dynasty, the Yang family needed a special Dragon-Tamer Wood found only on a mountain guarded by the Mus. This would lead to one thing and another, eventually resulting in Mu Guiying meeting with Yang Zongbao, and her joining into the Yang family.

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

2:43 The Twin-Dragon Meeting is another Yang family tale in which the Yang family was lured into a trap, resulting in the death of most of the sons of the third generation.

6:35 In ancient China, a banner of command was a physical symbol representing the authority of the commander and the mission or task they bestowed upon someone. It acts as prove of the commander's authority.

11:03 The three passes (三关, sān guān) is an expression referring to the country's border regions. 

18:38 An arrow of command (令箭, lìng jiàn)is similar to a banner of command, and acts as prove of the commander's authority.

27:36 Penglai Island (蓬莱岛, pénglái xiāndǎo) is a legendary island in Chinese mythology: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Penglai
It is said to be the home of many immortals including the Eight Immortals: https://youtu.be/1i05UY0Fcn0?si=q01-1Z-FS7JSygb1

41:07 二仙传道 (èr xiān chuán dào), literally meaning "two immortals spreading taoism" is a proverb referring to when two people (or hands) work together to accomplish something in secret. 

48:01 Rice bucket (饭桶, fàn tǒng) is a term referring to an incompetent person–so incompetent that the only thing they're good for is storing (eating) food.

title: Peking Opera:《Tears of the Barren Mountain》京剧: 荒山泪 (1/2), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Hi everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tf_TYe7LagM
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

Tears of the Barren Mountain (also known as 祈祷和平 A Prayer for Peace) is set in the late Ming dynasty, and tells the story of ordinary people during an era of corruption, political tension and heavy taxation. The play is based on the aphorism "苛政猛于虎" or "a tyrannical government is more fierce than a tiger" and is a social comment on many aspects of society. It is a famous tragedy with beautiful music and singing.

This video took a while because I also added the Chinese subtitles :)


I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

5:09 A string was a unit of currency. Old coins used to have a hole in the middle that allowed them to be stringed together. https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/String_of_cash_coins_(currency_unit)

5:19 Wang Sixiang sounds like 往四乡 (go to the villages) and Cui Defu sounds like 催得赋 (collect the taxes).

28:48 Villages and cities in ancient China often had a drum tower, which would sound at night at certain times as a way of telling time. Night time was divided into 5 更 (geng) from 7 pm to 5 am in our time today. 1 更 is equivalent to 2 hours today.

This is the second half of "A Match of Spring and Autumn." Check out part 1 if you haven't already. What will happen? Who knows? You will if you keep watching :)

Translator's Notes:

10:01 老爷 (lǎoyé) is a respectful title like 'lord' to refer to powerful figures. It's not really 'master' an owner of something or someone. I've already used lord so I had to settle on using Master 0_0

10:22 In front of every court/office/magistrate in ancient China had a drum that anyone could beat to summon the lord inside, if they needed to file an accusation, file a court case, resolve a dispute, report a major incident, etc.

11:23 回笼觉 (Huílóng jiào) actually means 'nap that you take after just waking up,' but I had no idea how to fit that in the sentence so I just said nap.

13:43 Headman/乡约 (xiāng yuē) refers to a minor official appointed by the government in villages responsible for conveying government orders and mediating disputes. Local constable/地保 (dì bǎo) refers to another type of minor official, but responsible for village patrols, handling disputes–basically like local police.

20:39 "Seventh-grade" because the imperial official system had seven different levels of officials, and therefore seven levels of courts for them to hold. The county magistrate is the lowest of the seven.

22:09 Omg I'm so sorry, that should be "disregard," not "disgre."

25:58 "King" here doesn't mean literally 'King' as in the royal sovereign. Bandit leaders often refer to themselves as "Mountain Kings" 山大王 (shān dàiwáng). It's like the title 'bandit king.' 

27:26 The special examinations 恩科 (ēn kē) is a examination type in the imperial examination system used in addition to the regular and non-regular examinations. It is usually personally held by the Emperor.

39:30 Qiulian is referring to herself.

title: 【賽博京劇】《勸列位 早低頭》| Cyber Peking Opera: A Corporate Satire
description:
「勸列位，氣話慢出口。且聽老朽，批春秋。」

當傳統京劇老生蒼勁、帶有膛音的獨特嗓音，遇上現代打工人滿腹委屈的血淚日常。這不是一首簡單的網梗惡搞，而是一幅嚴格依循傳統戲曲「油求」轍、像素級對齊板眼骨架的「職場現形記」。

西裝革履，鳳冠大翅。畫面與唱腔撕裂的背後，是冷眼旁觀的黑色幽默：

 老闆：畫餅強中手，篤信富貴險中求。
 Mentor：暗表忠心假裝仁厚，後輩風頭照單全收。
 甲方爸爸：一紙合同鎖死咽喉，提到尾款白日做夢去將周公奏。
 精緻女友 & 事後諸葛家長：恩威並施巧利誘，萬語千言嘮叨不休。

凡事莫要太深究，處處碰壁愁上愁。早早低頭，多多遷就。因為終有一天，熬過這場人間苦戲的列位，也會成長為台上的下一個主角。

諸位，聽老朽開戲了。

#勸列位早低頭 #賽博京劇 #職場現形記 #打工人神曲 #國潮新勢力 #高級感自嘲

"Listen up, my peers, dry your angry tears... Hear an old soul speak of our corporate critique."

What happens when a traditional Peking Opera "Laosheng" (old man role) puts on a modern sharp business suit to critique the endless cycle of corporate burnout?

This track is a pixel-perfect fusion of traditional Chinese theatrical vocal physics and the grim reality of modern white-collar life. Styled in a dystopian cyber-opera aesthetic, it holds a darkly humorous mirror up to our daily grind:

 The Boss: A master of illusionary dreams, chasing high risks for his own greedy schemes.
 The Mentor: A toxic corporate gatekeeper, stealing credit for every junior's report.
 The Client: The almighty king who chokes your neck with contracts but prays to the god of sleep when the invoice is due.
 The Sweet Nightmare at Home & The Hindsight Dad: Endless nagging, micromanagement, and zero real aid.

Don't look too deep into the pain, or you'll go insane. Go with the flow, bend low, and bow down early—because the ultimate irony of the corporate ladder is that one day, you might just become the very monster you once despised.

description:
Hi everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: www.youtube.com/watch?v=gt9b1EGhZeg
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

The story of Wang Baochuan has many names (Lady Precious Stream/The Red-maned Stallion/Wujia Hill). Set in Tang China, the opera tells the famous tale of Wang Baochuan and Xue Pinggui, a story of love, endurance and hope. It is comprised of two halfs and is a veryyyyy long opera. Both halves are long enough to stand as individual operas.

It took me so long to translate but its finally donee. The subs might be slightly out of sync because of an editing issue :(

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

10:08 Silver was used as money in ancient china.

11:06 Prime minister in an ancient Chinese context doesn't mean the leader of the country, but rather the top minister in the emperor's court.

11:23 Usually, when a woman marries they are married out of their family into the husband's. However, Baochuan is tossing the ball for something called 招赘 Zhaozhui, which is when the man marries into the woman's family. Hence I've translated it as "continue lineage" as having a son was important in the patrilineal ancient Chinese society.

11:03 Rainbow used to mean five colours in ancient China; red, blue, yellow, white and black.

18:51 Jiang Ziya was the famous duke of ancient ancient China.

40:39 To 调和鼎鼐 tiaohe dingnai or 'tune the ding' means to run the country smoothly.

40:55 The imperial court had three departments. The Prime Minister is above them. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three_Departments_and_Six_Ministries

42:00 Inheritance in ancient China was patrilineal.

50:05 Baochuan is saying honesty is what makes a human, human–the way different pieces are essential to chariots. She uses Confucius and Tai Gong as examples of good people who were based in honesty.

59:50 The story of Lady Meng Jiang is a classic love story. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lady_Meng_Jiang

1:00:47 SO BASICALLY, Wang Yun is using examples of historical figures to say that officials and nobleclass people are virtuous, etc while Baochuan is using historical figures that were both virtuous, etc and poor to argue against his point.
- Zhang Liang, Han Xin and Su Qin were government officials during the warring states period - Han Xin was the only one of lowly birth.
- The story of the celestial maiden and Dong Yong is also a classic love story.

1:03:14 父女之情 fu nu zhi qing can be understood as the deep love and bond a father and daughter share.

1:19:09 A 寒窑 hanyao is a type of traditional house built into a cave in northern China.

1:29:41 Baochuan and Pinggui address each other with differently from before they were married;
- Pinggui calls Baochuan 三姐 san jie meaning 'third sister' as like a nickname since she's the third daughter of the wang family. 
- Baochuan calls Pinggui 薛郎 xue lang which means something like my husband xue.

1:39:51 I have no idea what he was saying.

1:54:16 The camera doesn't show it well, but Pinggui used his hilt to knock Baochuan back.

title: Peking Opera:《Wang Baochuan (Second Half)》京剧: 王宝钏 (下), [Chinese Opera English Subtitles]
description:
Hi everyone!
Please excuse my amateur translation, feel free to make any corrections in the comments! Many expressions and phrases are untranslatable, I recommend looking at the original Chinese if you have the time: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PAKJ7BqxhI0&t=948s
I don't claim any credit of this work aside from the translations.

Back with the second, and arguably much more famous half of the story of Wang Baochuan! Please check out the first half if you haven't already. 

Extra info at the very end :)

I hope you enjoy~


Translator's Notes:

3:24 In Chinese culture, geese are used to symbolise the passage of time and a pair marital bliss.

3:44 Imperial Minister 太宰 taizai was the minister in charge matters within the Emperor's family.

5:15 大嫂 dasao, more accurately translated as aunty, is a respectful way to refer to a married woman unrelated to you.

6:35 People used to refer to letters as 万金书信 wanshu jiaxin, literally 10,000 gold letters because a letter to home was worth seen as extremely emotionally valuable.

19:43 Baochuan is worried her shabby appearance will make Pinggui lose face.

21:41 Pinggui is referencing other famous Chinese tales where a husband tests his wife.

23:11 Baochuan often uses proverbs from Confucius or other famous classical poetry works.

31:15 Chang'an is the ancient name for what is known today as Xi'an. It is a historical city that served the capital for many flourishing Chinese dynasties.

32:34 Ancient Chinese marriage certificates used to be like a contract. Like wedding vows on paper that the couple agreed to.

36:37 The most famous part of the whole opera, LE ARGUMENTE EPIC

38:32 Baochuan is listing traditional Chinese funeral preparations. White was the colour of funerals like how black is in the west.

46:54 Pangu is a primordial being that created the universe. 

47:40 In China there is an Emperor who is the imperial ruler. Under the Emperor, certain regions hard to rule, or other rebelling states, they might have a King or Duke. The Emperor's palace is called the golden hall. Any other ruler's is called the silver hall.

58:39 The first character of Daizhan's name 代 dai sounds the same as 带 dai, meaning to carry. Baochuan got confused.

1:15:40 The 貔貅 pixiu is a mythological creature of protection.

1:25:03 In Chinese, birds are say to fly in the shape of the word for person 人. Daizhan says, "above the clouds the geese fly as a person, below on the ground the person picks up their bow."

1:53:32 Chi is a Chinese unit of measurement, 1/3 a metre.

1:53:43 Baochuan is referred to as 娘娘千岁 niangniang qiansui, a title meaning literally Lady a thousand years.

2:07:02 Daizhan calls Baochuan 眼光娘娘, Goddess of glowing eyes, A goddess from Taoism. 

2:09:16 The jade rabbit lives on the moon and with Chang'e. www.waiyeehong.com/oriental-culture/jade-rabbit-yutu/ 


I hope you enjoyed the opera. This opera is just one rendition of the story. "Wang Baochuan" has many scenes, not all of them are included in this rendition. There are scenes showing Pinggui's suffering in the war, his first encounter and battle with Daizhan and his defeat and subsequent time in Western Liang. There's even a scenes showing Baochuan's time alone in the cave dwelling, her writing her blood letter, and the time her mother secretly visits her to convince her to come back to the Minister's estate. 
In the scenes where Su Long and Wei Hu plot against Pinggui, Wei Hu actually genuinely wants to harm him whereas Su Long tries to defend for him, hence Pinggui's different treatment to them when he became Emperor.

title: A Night At The Opera - Peking Opera
description:
Peking Dancers Light Up Auckland – A Night of Colour, Culture, and Movement.

We were honoured to witness a breathtaking performance by a visiting troupe of Peking dancers right here in Auckland, a vibrant celebration of traditional Chinese culture through movement, costume, and expression.

Members of our photography club were invited to attend this special event and capture the magic behind the lens. The result? A stunning collection of images that celebrate both the art of dance and the art of photography.

Watch as grace, tradition, and rhythm come together on stage and get a behind-the-scenes look at how local photographers froze these fleeting moments in time.

 Special thanks to the performers, organisers, and all the club members who participated.

Don’t forget to like, comment, and subscribe to see more events where culture and creativity collide!

Gardenia arrives in court! This second half of the opera uses a lot of ancient official legal language which I've tried my best to translate, but excuse any translations that don't make sense. Watch part one for context!

title: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00425
Description: Acupuncture and moxibustion are forms of traditional Chinese medicine widely practised in China and also found in regions of south-east Asia, Europe and the Americas. The theories of acupuncture and moxibustion hold that the human body acts as a small universe connected by channels, and that by physically stimulating these channels the practitioner can promote the human body's self-regulating functions and bring health to the patient. This stimulation involves the burning of moxa (mugwort) or the insertion of needles into points on these channels, with the aim to restore the body's balance and prevent and treat disease. In acupuncture, needles are selected according to the individual condition and used to puncture and stimulate the chosen points. Moxibustion is usually divided into direct and indirect moxibustion, in which either moxa cones are placed directly on points or moxa sticks are held and kept at some distance from the body surface to warm the chosen area. Moxa cones and sticks are made of dried mugwort leaves. Acupuncture and moxibustion are taught through verbal instruction and demonstration, transmitted through master-disciple relations or through members of a clan. Currently, acupuncture and moxibustion are also transmitted through formal academic education.
Country(ies): China

Dr Yoann Birling left France at the age of 18 years old to learn the wisdom of Chinese medicine in Beijing, China. He obtained a Bachelor of Medicine and a Master of Medicine from Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, arguably the most reputed Chinese medicine university. During his studies, Yoann followed many TCM experts including Pr Dong Zhenhua, Pr Liu Qingguo, Pr Hao Wanshan and Pr Wang Zhaoyang. In 2022, he completed a PhD at Western Sydney University, exploring the treatment of insomnia with Chinese medicine.

Yoann has practiced Chinese medicine in China, France and Australia for more than 8 years. His clinical focus is on the treatment of mental health issues with Chinese medicine and psychotherapy. Yoann has produced 17 peer-reviewed publications in the field of mental health and one clinical manual on the treatment of insomnia with Chinese medicine. He is also undergoing important work on clinical reasoning in Chinese medicine and the improvement of Chinese medicine education.

title: Moxa Therapy for Breech Baby #alternativemedicine
description:
Moxa therapy for breech baby! 
Acupuncturist can help turn a breech baby, with a combination of acupuncture and moxabustion. Moxabustion is a healing method that’s falls under the umbrella of Chinese medicine. It’s an age-old way of applying heat and can be used as a stand-alone treatment or in combination with acupuncture or other treatments. 

Moxibustion involves warming points on your body by burning the herb, artemesia (also called mugwort or Ai Ye) that’s been compressed and rolled into a long stick. It’s considered to be both warming and drying. This kind of heat is also very moving, which is why it’s a common way to turn an improperly positioned baby in Chinese medicine.

Researchers who have studied the use of moxibustion for correcting breech presentations found that, when combined with acupuncture, it resulted in fewer births by caesarean section.

Using moxibustion on this point is a well-known treatment for breech presentations, and causes your baby to increase its movements and in many cases change position entirely.

title: Moxibustion - What Can It Do For Your Health?
description:
Moxibustion is an essential part of Oriental medicine, which includes traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) as well as Japanese medicine. Moxibustion is an ancient heat treatment, which complements acupuncture and herbal medicine. 

Moxibustion involves the burning of mugwort, a particular tinder of dried herbs, on or near the skin to facilitate healing. It has been used throughout Asia for more than 3,000 years and was first practised in cold climates to generate warmth for the body and to reduce pain. The purpose of moxibustion, as with most forms of Traditional Oriental medicine, is to build and strengthen Blood, improve circulation by stimulating the flow of Qi (energy) and obtain or maintain health.

Ondol Oriental Medicine Clinic is a holistic medical practice based in Toowong Brisbane, Australia.

For more information visit: www.ondol.com.au
For more information on moxibustion in Brisbane see https://www.ondol.com.au/moxibustion/

_ what is moxa?
-Ling Shu, Guan Neng says that where needle does not work, moxibustion does. 
-TCM theory holds that moxibustion has a dual effect of tonification and purgation. 
-Different from needles and drugs, characteristics of moxibustion in materials and using fire determine that its efficacy is inclined to warming and nourishing. 
-So, moxibustion is often applied in deficiency-cold syndrome, though some excess-heat syndrome can also use it. 
-The roles of moxibustion can be broadly grouped into warm nourishing, warm dredging, and warm melting. 
-Warm nourishing refers to the benefits of warming Yang, tonifying qi, nurturing blood, and relieving depletion; warm dredging refers to the functions of activating blood, dissolving stasis, promoting qi, dredging channels, and relieving pain; 
-warm melting refers to the roles of reducing phlegm, eliminating stagnation, removing wind, dispelling dampness, drawing out poison, and purging heat.

project_name_en: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
title: Moxibustion - Haynes Acupuncture Gold Coast
description:
Moxibustion is a traditional Chinese medicine technique that involves the burning of mugwort, a dried herb, to promote healing and alleviate various health conditions. This ancient practice is often used with acupuncture but can be performed independently. During moxibustion, a small cone or stick of dried mugwort is ignited and held over specific acupuncture points or body areas. The radiant heat generated by the burning mugwort penetrates the skin, stimulating circulation and warming the underlying tissues. This process is believed to balance the flow of vital energy, known as qi, within the body and restore health.

There are two primary types of moxibustion: direct and indirect. In direct moxibustion, a small moxa cone is placed directly on the skin at the acupuncture point and burned until it smoulders, producing a localized warming sensation. Indirect moxibustion involves holding the burning moxa stick above the skin, typically about an inch away, to avoid direct contact with the skin. The heat from the burning moxa penetrates the skin without causing burns, making it a gentler option for sensitive individuals. Both direct and indirect moxibustion can effectively address a wide range of health concerns, including pain, digestive disorders, menstrual irregularities, and immune system imbalances.

Moxibustion is often used in traditional Chinese medicine to complement acupuncture treatments and enhance their therapeutic effects. It is believed to strengthen the body's vital energy, boost the immune system, and promote overall well-being.

title: Moxibustion Acupuncture / What should I do if I have indigestion, bloating or abdominal pain?
description:
What to do with indigestion and bloating?
Today I will teach you a method.
First, we prepare a piece of ginger.
Cut the ginger into slices. The thickness is about the same as a coin.
Poke a few small holes on the ginger.
Pinch the moxa stick. This makes it easier to burn the moxa stick.
Then light it with a lighter.
Be sure to burn the moxa stick completely.
You can use it on your hand.
Put the ginger slices at the base of our thumb.
Because there is an acupuncture point called Hegu acupuncture point.
This acupuncture point is a big switch in the human body.
It can regulate the flow of qi and blood throughout the body.
It can also regulate various pains and discomforts.
So the effect is very good.
In addition, add ginger and moxa leaves.
They are good things for warming the meridians and dispersing cold.
If the two are used together, they have a good effect on stomach pain, abdominal pain and diarrhea caused by spleen and stomach deficiency and cold.

title: Find Relief from Knee Pain with Acupuncture and Moxibustion!…2
description:
For years, a persistent, grinding pain in his knees had been the unwelcome companion to Arthur’s every step. Once an avid gardener and a man who found joy in long walks through the countryside, Arthur’s world had shrunk to the confines of his home, the relentless ache a constant reminder of his body’s betrayal. He had tried everything the doctors recommended: powerful anti-inflammatory drugs that upset his stomach, cortisone shots that offered only fleeting relief, and even the grim prospect of surgery loomed on the horizon.[1][2]
Discouraged, Arthur’s daughter, Clara, suggested an alternative. "Dad, have you ever considered acupuncture?" she asked, her eyes filled with a hopeful earnestness. Arthur, a man of tradition and skepticism, was hesitant. The idea of needles seemed more like a prelude to more pain, not a solution. But Clara’s persistence, coupled with his own desperation, finally wore him down.
He found himself in a quiet, calming clinic, the air scented with a faint, earthy aroma. The acupuncturist, a woman named Li, had a serene presence that immediately put him at ease. She listened patiently as Arthur recounted his long battle with knee osteoarthritis, a condition where the cartilage cushioning his joints had worn down, leading to inflammation and pain.[3]
Li explained that in Traditional Chinese Medicine, pain is often seen as a sign of blockage in the body's energy flow, or "qi".[1] Acupuncture, she elaborated, involves inserting incredibly thin needles into specific points on the body to clear these blockages, reduce inflammation, and stimulate the body’s natural painkillers.[1][3] She also mentioned moxibustion, a therapy that involves burning an herb called mugwort near the skin to warm the area, further promoting circulation and easing stiffness.[1][4]
During the first session, Arthur lay on the treatment table, his heart thumping with a mixture of anxiety and a flicker of hope. Li’s hands were gentle and sure as she placed the fine needles around his knees, particularly in a region she called "xiyan," or the "eyes of the knee."[3] He barely felt a prick, and to his surprise, a wave of relaxation washed over him. Then came the moxibustion. Li lit a stick of moxa, a processed form of mugwort, and held it near his knees.[5] A pleasant, warming sensation penetrated deep into his joints, a stark contrast to the sharp, cold ache he was so accustomed to.[1]
After the first few treatments, the change was subtle but undeniable. The constant, throbbing pain began to recede, replaced by a duller ache that was easier to ignore.[6] He found himself moving with a little more ease, the stairs in his home seeming less like a mountain to be conquered. Encouraged, he continued his weekly sessions.
With each visit, the improvement became more pronounced. The warmth of the moxibustion seemed to melt away the stiffness that had plagued his mornings, and the acupuncture needles worked their quiet magic, diminishing the inflammation.[7] He was able to spend more time in his beloved garden, kneeling on a soft pad to tend to his roses, a simple act that had been impossible just a few months prior.
One crisp autumn afternoon, Arthur found himself on a familiar path in the countryside, a path he hadn’t walked in years. The crunch of fallen leaves under his feet was a welcome sound, and the familiar rhythm of his stride was a testament to his recovery. He still felt an occasional twinge, a gentle reminder of his condition, but the debilitating pain that had once held him captive was gone. Acupuncture and moxibustion hadn't regrown the cartilage in his knees, but they had given him something just as precious: relief from pain and the freedom to once again enjoy the simple, profound joys of a life in motion.

title: The Clinical Application of Laser in Korean Medicine: Laser acupuncture, moxibustion and others
description:
#isams #acupuncture #internationalconference
Abstract: Laser acupuncture have a long history since it was firstly performed by Plog in 1973.
The principle of laser acupuncture is a combination of biostimulation of photobiomodulation (Low level laser therapy (LLLT)), and acupuncture specific effects 
As shown in many reviews, one of the most common indications is pain control and recovery of function in musculo-skeletal disorders: low back pain, shoulder pain, ankle sprain, tendon lesion, osteoarthritis, plantar fasciitis, postoperative recovery, trigeminal neuralgia and etc. 
In addition, many indications in the pediatric area are being reported: nocturia, asthma, bronchitis, rhinitis, sinusitis, postoperative nausea and vomiting, various pediatric pain disorders, pediatric headaches, and etc. It is also used in neyurogical disease such as stroke sequelae, brain injury, headache, migraine. Studies have been reported in  dermatological indication, including acne, allergic diseases, burns and skin ulcer. Advantages of laser acupuncture are that there is no pain in low level laser such as indium laser, and there are advantages such as ease of procedure and hygiene safety as well. 
In the future, with the development of various devices, the scope of the laser acupuncture and moxibustion is expected to expand further.

title: Japanese Moxibustion and Acupuncture for Arthritis
description:
https://acupuncturecentertoronto.com/ This time-honored herbal heat therapy is applied directly over areas of joint discomfort. When joints feel cold or stiff, Moxibustion helps to warm the area and dispel the cold and dampness that contributes to stiffness and pain.

Practiced for millennia, Moxibustion has shown remarkable effectiveness in addressing musculoskeletal conditions.

Our Registered Acupuncturists are trained in Moxibustion by our Clinic Owner, who has received advanced mentorship from leading Moxibustion practitioners in Japan. We are committed to using only the finest mugwort and Moxibustion materials, which we carefully source during our visits to Japan.

Small amounts of medicinal herbs are placed on or near the targeted joints.

These herbs are gently heated over a protective, burn-preventing salve.

The warming process is initiated using traditional Japanese incense.

Moxibustion can be added to any clinical acupuncture session at no additional charge.

Please note: An assessment is required to determine whether Moxibustion is appropriate and safe for your specific condition. What Is Moxibustion In Fertility Acupuncture? Are you curious about how traditional Chinese medicine can support fertility? In this video, we explore the technique of moxibustion and its role in enhancing reproductive health. We’ll explain what moxibustion is and how it is commonly used alongside acupuncture to promote balance within the body. You’ll learn about the process of burning moxa, a substance made from dried Artemisia leaves, and how practitioners apply heat to specific points on the body to stimulate circulation and energy flow.

We’ll discuss how moxibustion may help address common fertility concerns such as poor blood flow, hormonal imbalances, and conditions like a cold uterus. Additionally, we’ll review scientific studies that investigate its potential benefits for women undergoing fertility treatments like IVF, including improving ovulation and hormone regulation. While more research is needed, some evidence suggests that this therapy could support ovarian and uterine health, especially when used as part of a comprehensive treatment plan.

This video also highlights the importance of consulting qualified practitioners and not replacing standard medical care with alternative therapies. Whether you’re dealing with endometriosis or other fertility challenges, understanding how moxibustion might fit into your health journey is essential. title: Moxa - Moxibustion - Moxacology Seminar, Calgary, Alberta, Alberta College of Acupuncture & TCM
description:
Three Days with Dr. Chen Rixin

Date: April 20th - 22nd, 2017

Why is Heat Sensitive Moxibustion founded by Dr. Chen Rixin Revolutionary?

Although Heat Sensitive Moxibustion was mentioned in all Chinese Ancient Medical classic books, in modern days, moxibustion is mostly applied under acupuncture system and known TCM Fundamentals. 

Then mention in Yellow Emperor: "Use Fire To Tonify; Use First To Reduce", clearly stated "Heat can reduce Heat". The true meaning of Moxibustion is now rediscovered by Dr. Chen Rixin!

- Moxibustion has it's Own Points, different from Acupuncture

- Moxibustion has it's Own Theory, which hasn't been taught in TCM Universities yet

- Moxibustion has it's Own Sensation, both local and distal

- Moxibustion uses Heat to Reduce Heat, this treatment principle does not apply to Acupuncture and Herbal Medicine

- Moxibustion is an independent treatment method, Not complementary to Acupuncture

title: Acupuncture Course in China–World Federation of Acupuncture Moxibustion Societies  طب سوزنی چین WFAS
description:
Taking advantage of equipped educational system and hospitals for Traditional Chinese Medicine in Beijing, China, the World Federation of Acupuncture Moxibustion Societies is a NGO in official relations with WHO that holds specialized courses of Acupuncture for those practitioners who learned the science and achieved the skills of acupuncture previously but interested in taking some complementary and more practical knowledge.
In addition, a standard international proficiency test of Acupuncture is taken and sponsored by the World Federation of Acupuncture Moxibustion Societies (WFAS), granting the qualification of ‘Acupuncture Doctor’ to those who successfully pass the theoretical and practical exams.
In this video, my personal experience of an educational trip to China to learn practical complementary skills of Acupuncture and Cosmetology in training courses of WFAS and TCM hospital of Beijing is displayed. There, I was able to take the certificate of ‘Acupuncture Doctor’ after the international proficiency exams. I also managed to visit and talk to Professor Liu Baoyan, the president of WFAS and other authorities and instructors of the federation, among which I got a press interview with Dr. Luo published in the English section of Iranian Students News Agency ISNA.

title: Moxa on Spleen 1 point to relieve cramps and heavy bleeding. #timiliff #acupuncture #womenshealth
description:
Burning moxa (the product made from the mug wort herb) on acupuncture points is a great form of at-home treatment. Burning moxa on the acupuncture point spleen 1 can help women slow bleeding and relief cramps while menstruating. In this treatment you can use a moxa cigar (available on Amazon) on the points that are on the big toe right next to the bottom corner of the nail. 

Slowly move the lit cigar toward and away from the points until they feel warm. Acupuncture and Chinese herbology offer many tools to help with menstrual health.

title: Acupuncture & Moxibustion at Sustain Health
description:
At Sustain Health Clinic we combine gentle acupuncture with moxibustion to help the body relax, restore balance, and support overall wellbeing ✨

We use tiny 0.2mm acupuncture needles, which are precise and almost painless, making treatments comfortable even for those new to acupuncture.

Moxibustion, made from the herbAi Ye (mugwort, provides gentle, penetrating heat. It’s placed on top of the needles or directly on the body to enhance the effects of acupuncture.

Benefits include:

👉 Relieving cramps and abdominal tension
👉 Easing tight muscles in the back, shoulders, and abdomen
👉 Supporting circulation and Qi flow throughout the body
👉 Deepening the effect of acupuncture with warm, soothing heat
👉 Creating a calming, comforting experience that many patients find very relaxing

Sessions typically last about 10 minutes, leaving you feeling warm, relaxed, and refreshed. This approach is especially helpful for female health concerns, menstrual cramps, or general muscle tension, helping the body function at its best naturally 💛

✨ Bienvenue au centre BioMed MTC – Votre espace de bien-être et d’harmonie énergétique à Genève ! ✨

Au cœur de notre centre, nous combinons l’expertise de la médecine traditionnelle chinoise avec des approches modernes pour vous offrir un accompagnement global et personnalisé. Notre mission est de vous aider à retrouver équilibre, vitalité et sérénité, que ce soit pour gérer le stress, améliorer votre sommeil, soutenir votre fertilité ou renforcer votre énergie.

💆‍♀️ Acupuncture
Nos séances d’acupuncture stimulent les méridiens et les points stratégiques du corps pour harmoniser l’énergie vitale (Qi), soulager la douleur, réduire le stress et soutenir vos organes internes. Chaque traitement est adapté à vos besoins spécifiques, pour un bien-être immédiat et durable.

🌀 Ventouses
Les ventouses traditionnelles permettent de stimuler la circulation sanguine, libérer les tensions musculaires et favoriser l’élimination des toxines. Une méthode douce mais puissante pour retrouver légèreté et énergie dans tout le corps.

🔥 Moxibustion
La moxibustion utilise la chaleur du moxa pour tonifier l’énergie du corps, réchauffer les zones froides et soutenir les fonctions organiques essentielles. Idéale pour renforcer le système immunitaire et améliorer le confort digestif ou articulaire.

🌿 Phytothérapie chinoise
Nous sélectionnons pour vous des plantes médicinales de qualité pour rééquilibrer l’organisme, soutenir vos cycles physiologiques et améliorer votre vitalité. Chaque formule est personnalisée selon votre constitution et vos objectifs de santé.

🛋️ Table relaxante binaurale (Welnamis)
Découvrez la relaxation profonde grâce à notre table innovante combinant vibrations, musique et sons binauraux. Une expérience unique pour détendre le corps, apaiser l’esprit et relancer votre énergie intérieure.

Chez BioMed MTC, chaque soin est pensé pour vous offrir un voyage vers le bien-être, alliant tradition, innovation et écoute attentive. Venez vivre une expérience holistique où votre équilibre physique et émotionnel est notre priorité.

🌟 **Unlock the Benefits of Acupuncture with Community Zen Acupuncture!** 🌟

Are you curious about how acupuncture can transform your well-being? Join us as we delve into the incredible benefits that acupuncture offers, especially for seniors, with insights from Community Zen Acupuncture.

🌿 **What is Acupuncture?**  
Acupuncture is an ancient practice that involves inserting fine needles into specific points on the body to stimulate energy flow and promote healing. At Community Zen Acupuncture, this holistic approach is tailored to meet individual needs, enhancing overall health and vitality.

💪 **Benefits for Seniors:**  
1. **Pain Relief:** Acupuncture is renowned for alleviating chronic pain, including back pain, arthritis, and joint discomfort. It helps reduce inflammation and promote natural healing.
2. **Improved Mobility:** By enhancing circulation and reducing muscle tension, acupuncture can improve flexibility and mobility, making everyday activities easier and more enjoyable.
3. **Stress Reduction:** Seniors often face emotional stress, and acupuncture is a gentle way to promote relaxation and mental clarity, helping to manage anxiety and depression.
4. **Enhanced Sleep Quality:** Struggling with sleep issues? Acupuncture can help regulate sleep patterns and improve overall restfulness, leading to better health and energy levels.

✨ **Experience the Difference**  
Community Zen Acupuncture is dedicated to providing personalized care to support your well-being. Discover how acupuncture can make a difference in your life!

🔗 **Learn More:** Visit [Community Zen Acupuncture](https://www.communityzenacupuncture.com/) to explore their services and start your journey towards better health!

Don't forget to like, comment, and subscribe for more health tips and wellness insights! 💬🔔

title: Acupuncture for Pelvic Health and Marketing After Relocating a Clinic with Krytal Couture
description:
In case you missed it last week, I shared a fun conversation with @thepelvicacu, Krystal Couture on the Acupuncture Marketing School podcast. ⁠
⁠
We talk a little bit about acupuncture for pelvic health and what a big impact it can have for patients. ⁠
⁠
And then we dive into what marketing has worked to build Krystal's practice over the years, including how she rebuilt after relocating about 8 years ago. ⁠
⁠
I think you'll really enjoy Krystal's very natural and authentic perspectives on marketing. She's all about figuring out what works for YOU, and forgetting about all the hype and noise on the internet about what you "should" be doing.⁠
⁠
I agree with her. If it's not a good fit for your energy, it's not likely you can sustain it long term. And marketing requires repetition and consistency to work, so you've got to find the marketing that's a good fit for your energy.  ⁠
⁠
🎧 Listen to Episode 94 with Krystal Couture: https://michellegrasek.com/marketing-energy-krystal-couture-the-pelvic-acu/

title: Acupuncture & Moxibustion: Needles and Smoke with Corinna Loo.
description:
There is no doubt that Traditional Chinese Medicine has evolved over time and became one of the most widespread practices in complementary and alternative medicine nowadays offering several different modalities. 

Two of them are - Acupuncture and Moxibustion.

But what do we know about these two exactly?
How do they suppose to work?
What to expect of them?

In brief, Acupuncture is a technique in which the practitioner stimulates specific points on the body, usually by inserting thin needles through the skin, as the result releasing the body's natural painkillers and affecting areas in the brain involved in processing pain.

Moxibustion is a form of heat therapy in which "moxa" are burned on or very near the surface of the skin, with intention of warming and invigorating the flow of Qi in the body and dispelling certain pathogenic influences.

In this video, I explore and try both methods of Acupuncture and Moxibustion myself under the guidance of Licensed Acupuncturist  -  Corinna Loo @tiny.medicine  https://www.instagram.com/tiny.medicine/

title: 5 Acupuncture Points to Avoid During Pregnancy | Peace of Zen TV
description:
Welcome to Peace of Zen TV 🌿 Today’s video is essential for pregnant women, home massage enthusiasts, massage therapists, and acupuncturists alike.

We’ll walk through 5 key acupressure/acupuncture points that are traditionally contraindicated during pregnancy, due to their ability to influence uterine contractions and down bearing energy flow (ki/qi/chi). Whether you're receiving a massage, doing self-care, or working with a practitioner, these are points to be cautious with:

🔸 GB21 (Gallbladder 21) – Top of the shoulder, can strongly descend energy and stimulate contractions.
🔸 LI4 (Large Intestine 4) – Between thumb and index finger, commonly used for pain and headaches, but should be avoided during pregnancy.
🔸 SP6 (Spleen 6) – Above the inner ankle, affects the uterus and hormonal regulation.
🔸 BL60 (Bladder 60) – Behind the ankle, historically used to help release the placenta.
🔸 BL67 (Bladder 67) – Tip of the pinky toe, used in the final weeks of pregnancy with moxa to turn breech babies.

⚠️ These points are powerful tools—but timing matters. While contraindicated during most of pregnancy, some of these may be intentionally used around week 39–40 to help prepare for labor, always under professional care.

Subscribe for follow-up videos where we’ll discuss safe acupressure techniques for pregnancy and natural ways to prepare for labor.

📌 TIMESTAMPS & HIGHLIGHTS
0:00 – Intro: Points to avoid during pregnancy
0:35 – GB21 and shoulder tension
1:20 – LI4 (hand point) and safe massage guidance
2:05 – SP6 and its effect on uterus and hormones
3:00 – BL60: historical use and modern context
3:45 – BL67 and using moxa for breech position
4:25 – Wrap-up & future video previews

title: Absolute Zero Pain!  Authentic Acupuncture & Moxibustion in Japan
description:
His "needles" were not sharp.  Literally they were round-end  sticks. Copper, aluminium and titanium sticks.
I don't know how it worked, however, the intense backache of patient has gone like a magic. He also did moxibustion which was not hot. He read the pulse of a patient  many times after attaching a tiny metal piece on a certain point to make sure the point was dead-on.  
The therapist Mr. Matsuyama  was one of the therapists I visited in Japan for my learning. 
Special Thanks to Sensei Matsuyama! This video is just a part of our session over there.

By the way, this needleless acupuncture is called "teishin".

My quest for the ultimate healing never stops. 

Feel free to comment or email if you have any questions.  Thank you for watching my video.

I am working as a Registered Massage Therapist since 2012. Before coming to Canada, I was an orthopaedic Doctor in Ukraine, and worked at hospitals and sport injury clinics was treating the patients and developing injury prevention programs. To make my treatments versatile, I acquired the certificates of acupuncture and cupping practitioner, and became an NSCA Certified Personal Trainer. My treatment is customised for each patient, and is a unique combination of Eastern and Western medicine. I perform :General Swedish Massage, Trigger point Therapy, Myofascial Techniques, Deep Tissue Massage, Sport Massage, Strengths and Stretching, Joint mobilization technique, Relaxation massage, Yamato -Breathing- Seitai therapy; a Japanese massage, TMJ massage, acupuncture and cupping. Some people come to me when the conventional medicines such as surgeries and medications and their therapists cannot help. I am always up to such challenging cases. I've been passionate about HEALTH all my life.

title: How to do moxibustion at Du20 acupuncture point on your own: Beaverton acupuncture
description:
How to do moxibustion at Du20 acupuncture point on your own!If possible, please wear your hair short.  If you wear your hair long, please use pins to make the moxa point visible in the mirror.
With an incense positioned between your right index finger and middle finger, please roll a moxa cone which is taller than usual.  This is the way you can make it visible on your head.  Hold the tall moxa cone between your right thumb and index finger.  To make it stand on the point, put a little saliva or water on the nail of your left thumb, and then put the cone on the nail of your left thumb.
Please touch the moxa point with your left index or middle finger.  Once your finger have found the point, please switch it with the moxa cone at the same time.
In the mirror, you will see the cone standing on the head when you lower your head a little.  Then, please light it with the incense.  It will take a few trials in the beginning, but you will get used to it very soon.As for the next four cones, you can put each of them on the ash.

How Moxibustion Acupuncture Can Restore Balance
Yin and yang are complementary forces in the universe, including our bodies. Yin represents the cooler, darker, and more passive aspects, while yang represents the warmer, brighter, and more active aspects. They are interconnected and dependent on each other for harmony and balance. 
Moxibustion acupuncture plays a crucial role in regulating the body's balance between yin and yang energies. Yin and yang are opposing forces that need harmony for optimal health. If there is an imbalance between yin and yang, it can manifest as physical or emotional symptoms.
A strong immune system is vital for maintaining physical balance and protecting the body from illnesses. Moxibustion acupuncture can enhance immune function by stimulating specific acupuncture points associated with the immune system.
By activating acupuncture points, stimulating the flow of qi and blood, regulating yin and yang energies, and addressing specific bodily systems, moxibustion acupuncture targets the root causes of imbalances and promotes overall well-being.
If you are ready to experience the transformative effects of moxibustion acupuncture, contact us today and begin your path to restored balance and vitality. Our dedicated team of practitioners is here to support you on your wellness journey.

title: How to Use Moxibustion (Moxa) Sticks? #Moxibustion #moxa #moxastand #kstar
description:
Moxibustion is a treatment within traditional Chinese medicine. A practitioner burns the herb “moxa” on or above the skin to warm and stimulate specific points on the body. Practitioners use moxibustion either as a stand-alone therapy or in combination with acupuncture.

In traditional Chinese medicine, moxibustion aims to stimulate the body’s flow of vital energy or “qi” and remove toxins.

Western practitioners may use moxibustion to help alleviate pain and inflammation from various ailments. However, there is insufficient scientific evidence to support its effectiveness.

This article describes what moxibustion is and how it works, including its potential uses. It also describes how practitioners perform moxibustion and discusses this complementary therapy’s safety and effectiveness.

project_name_en: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
title: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
description:
Acupuncture is the insertion of needles into superficial structures of the body (skin, subcutaneous tissue, muscles) – usually at acupuncture points (acupoints) – and their subsequent manipulation; this aims at influencing the flow of qi. According to TCM it relieves pain and treats (and prevents) various diseases. The US FDA classifies single-use acupuncture needles as Class II medical devices, under CFR 21. 
Acupuncture is often accompanied by moxibustion – the Chinese characters for acupuncture (针灸; 針灸; zhēnjiǔ) literally meaning "acupuncture-moxibustion" – which involves burning mugwort on or near the skin at an acupuncture point. According to the American Cancer Society, "available scientific evidence does not support claims that moxibustion is effective in preventing or treating cancer or any other disease".
In electroacupuncture, an electric current is applied to the needles once they are inserted, to further stimulate the respective acupuncture points. 
A recent historian of Chinese medicine remarked that it is "nicely ironic that the specialty of acupuncture -- arguably the most questionable part of their medical heritage for most Chinese at the start of the twentieth century -- has become the most marketable aspect of Chinese medicine." She found that acupuncture as we know it today has hardly been in existence for sixty years. Moreover, the fine, filiform needle we think of as the acupuncture needle today was not widely used a century ago. Present day acupuncture was developed in the 1930s and put into wide practice only as late as the 1960s.

🌟 **Unlock the Benefits of Acupuncture with Community Zen Acupuncture!** 🌟

Are you curious about how acupuncture can transform your well-being? Join us as we delve into the incredible benefits that acupuncture offers, especially for seniors, with insights from Community Zen Acupuncture.

🌿 **What is Acupuncture?**  
Acupuncture is an ancient practice that involves inserting fine needles into specific points on the body to stimulate energy flow and promote healing. At Community Zen Acupuncture, this holistic approach is tailored to meet individual needs, enhancing overall health and vitality.

💪 **Benefits for Seniors:**  
1. **Pain Relief:** Acupuncture is renowned for alleviating chronic pain, including back pain, arthritis, and joint discomfort. It helps reduce inflammation and promote natural healing.
2. **Improved Mobility:** By enhancing circulation and reducing muscle tension, acupuncture can improve flexibility and mobility, making everyday activities easier and more enjoyable.
3. **Stress Reduction:** Seniors often face emotional stress, and acupuncture is a gentle way to promote relaxation and mental clarity, helping to manage anxiety and depression.
4. **Enhanced Sleep Quality:** Struggling with sleep issues? Acupuncture can help regulate sleep patterns and improve overall restfulness, leading to better health and energy levels.

project_name_en: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
title: “Moxibustion 🔥 Ancient Chinese Therapy | A Must-Know for BNYS Students 🌿✨”
description:
🔥 Moxibustion is a traditional therapy in Chinese Medicine that involves burning the dried leaves of mugwort (Artemisia vulgaris) near specific acupuncture points on the body. The heat and energy released from the herb are believed to stimulate the flow of Qi (vital energy), improve circulation, and restore balance to the body. 🌿✨

This ancient healing method has been practiced for over 2,500 years and is still widely used today in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), Acupuncture, and Holistic Healing systems.

⸻

🌿 How Moxibustion is Done

There are two main techniques:
 1. Direct Moxibustion – Small cones of mugwort are placed directly on acupuncture points and lit until the skin feels warm (sometimes removed before burning the skin, or in older methods, left until a small scar forms).
 2. Indirect Moxibustion – The most common method today, where a moxa stick (like a cigar made of mugwort) is lit and held close to the skin to transfer soothing heat without burning.

Sometimes, moxa is also placed on acupuncture needles to enhance their effect.

⸻

✨ Benefits & Uses of Moxibustion

Practitioners believe moxibustion may help with:
 • Improving blood circulation & reducing inflammation
 • Boosting energy (Qi) and strengthening immunity
 • Relieving chronic pain (arthritis, back pain, joint stiffness)
 • Supporting digestion and metabolism
 • Regulating menstrual cycles & reducing menstrual cramps
 • Turning breech babies during pregnancy (under professional supervision)
 • Balancing cold-related conditions in the body

⸻

📚 Moxibustion for BNYS Students

For BNYS (Bachelor of Naturopathy and Yogic Sciences) students, moxibustion is an important part of Acupuncture & Traditional Medicine studies.
 • It helps connect principles of naturopathy, yoga, and TCM.
 • Useful for exam preparation, case studies, and clinical training.
 • Shows how natural therapies like heat + herbs can impact health holistically.

Moxa comes from a herb Mugwort and is being widely
used. Moxa is burned in order to warm the body during an
acupuncture treatment. This is called moxibustion.A safe therapy.
Moxa is important in the treatment of fatigue, fibromyalgia
and pain syndromes, certain musculo-skeletal injuries, arthritis,
digestive disorders, women’s menstrual issues and more.
In India this treatment is termed a s Chutta therapy.
There are varieties in this therapy like Ginger Moksha, Potatoe
and other.

At Nadipathy we treat patients by putting the heat on the appropriate reflex points or Su Jok points/
Acupuncture points.

One of the ancient therapies that we do frequently.

🌿 Experience the Warmth of Moxibustion! 🌿

Moxibustion is a gentle and effective therapy that has been cherished in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries. By warming specific acupuncture points with burning moxa (dried mugwort), it helps to enhance circulation, balance your Qi (energy), and promote natural healing. Whether you're facing digestive concerns, menstrual discomfort, chronic fatigue, or soft tissue injuries, moxibustion offers a soothing and holistic solution.

Mugwort is a staple herb in Traditional Chinese Medicine. Moxa is made by compressing dried mugwort leaves into sticks. In the video above, farm workers roll the dried leaves into sticks.

title: Dr. Saish Prabhu- Acupuncture-Moxibustion and Tuina through Shanghai University of TCM China
description:
Dr. Saish is a young enthusiastic doctor working at SARC-Goa Hospital Porvorim. He has invaluable experience gained during several years in China, where TCM is based.
First Indian to have a Bachelor’s degree in Acupuncture-Moxibustion and Tuina through Shanghai University of TCM China in Chinese language.
He has dedicated his life to researching and mastering the deep roots of acupuncture and the Chinese way of healthy life.  
From an early age, his father, Dr. M. B. Prabhu was constantly in treatment camps and Young Saish was with him trying to understand how such chronic and horrifying diseases could be cured with tiny needles. It was a fascinating experience for him that had a magnetic effect towards the science of acupuncture. From an early age, he had a strong will to become an acupuncturist. When you set your life goal to become a healer, even the universe supports you, and through the tireless efforts of him and his father, he passed the admission procedure to SHANGHAI UNIVERSITY OF TCM, the most prestigious TCM university in the world, where the seeds of his lifelong desire to become a skilled acupuncturist like his father finally sprouted and blossomed into a tall tree.

project_name_en: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
title: Traditional Japanese Acupuncture and Moxibustion, Ebramec, Brazil 2022
description:
"The pathology of Liver deficiency Yin deficiency heat pattern involves deficiency of Kidney Jin Ye and Jin Ye deficiency within the Blood. Heat is generated as a result. 
This state is called pathological Yin deficiency or Blood deficiency and the heat generated as a result is called deficient heat as it occurs due to Jin Ye deficiency."
Alan Jansson sensei presents a 3 Day deep dive into Kidney and Liver Deficiency syndrome patterns in the practice of Traditional Japanese Acupuncture and Moxibustion.
An English/Portuguese presentation from WorldAcupuncture.com.
Ebramec São Paulo, Brazil 24th - 25th - 26th September, 2022.

project_name_en: Acupuncture and moxibustion of traditional Chinese medicine
title: Why Is That Acupuncture Needle Smoking?
description:
This is usually the moment patients start asking questions.

Why is that acupuncture needle smoking?

It's called moxa (moxibustion), a traditional Chinese medicine technique that uses the heat from mugwort to gently warm specific acupuncture points.

While most people associate Chinese medicine with needles, heat has been used alongside acupuncture for centuries.

Simple. Ancient. Still used every day.

📍Plantation, Florida
🔗 www.cosmopolitanhealing.com

title: EAR #acupuncture #moxibustion #chinese #electro  ACUPUNCTURE #Chinese-therapy
description:
The ear is what we call a microcosm of the body. All areas of the body are represented and can be treated through the ear. Just like in reflexology they use the foot or hand. Korean Acupuncture for example has over 300 points just on the hand.  There are 100 commonly used acupuncture points on the ear. A lot of western research has been performed on Ear Acupuncture.  The mechanism behind Ear Acupuncture is believed to be the ear’s close proximity to the brain, the large number of nerve endings in it, and the fact that it is the first sense organ to develop on a young fetus.  In Chinese Medicine, the ear has strong connections to your Kidney energy, which is the basis of life and long term health and fertility.

Cute #Japanese #girl #massage; #oil #Japanese #massage 

Very fine needles are used to stimulate points on the ear, and little press tabs are stuck to the ear so the patient can massage the points whenever they feel stressed for example, to further benefit their overall treatment.

title: Acupuncture points for skin and weight
description:
"Acupuncture Points to Avoid for Clear Skin and Weight Maintenance"

While acupuncture and acupressure are revered for their benefits in promoting health and wellness, certain points might be too effective for those grappling with specific issues like acne and weight gain. The 'Neiting' point, located between the second and third toes, is known for its ability to reduce inflammation and clear heat from the body, potentially clearing up acne rapidly. However, for those enjoying their clear skin, tampering with this point might just resolve their issues a bit too effectively.

Similarly, the 'Zhongwan' point, situated midway between the navel and the base of the sternum, is a critical point for digestive health. It's said to boost metabolism and aid in digestion, which can inadvertently prevent weight gain after eating. This could be frustrating for gourmets who revel in their culinary indulgences and wish to maintain their weight.

These insights into acupuncture suggest that while some might seek to stimulate these points for health benefits, others might consider avoiding them to preserve their current state, showcasing the dual-edged nature of traditional Chinese medicine. Whether to stimulate or avoid these points should be a well-considered decision based on individual health goals and lifestyle preferences.

title: 喉が痛い風邪気味７歳小児はり｜Kids Acu & Moxa Care#netherlands #rotterdam #acupunctuur
description:
寒暖差の影響なのか、咲実が少し風邪気味になったので、家族で小児はりとお灸をして体調を整えてみた日の記録です。

鼻水、くしゃみ、だるさ、なんとなく元気が出ない時。
子どもの体は小さな変化にも反応しやすいので、強い刺激ではなく、やさしく触れるような小児はりと、温かいお灸で体が休みやすい状態を作っていきました。

今回は、咲実だけでなく、家族みんなで鍼とお灸に触れながら、体を整える時間になりました。
オランダ生活の中で感じる寒暖差や、子どもの体調管理、家でできる小さなケアの参考になればうれしいです。

This is a family vlog from the day Saki felt a little under the weather, possibly because of the temperature changes.
We tried gentle pediatric acupuncture and moxibustion to help support her body.

Runny nose, sneezing, tiredness, or just not feeling like herself.
Children’s bodies can react easily to small changes, so I used gentle pediatric acupuncture and warm moxibustion to help her body relax and recover more comfortably.

This time, it was not only for Saki.
The whole family spent time with acupuncture and moxa together, as a gentle way to care for the body.

I hope this video is helpful for families living in the Netherlands, parents interested in children’s health care, and anyone curious about pediatric acupuncture and moxibustion.

※体調や症状には個人差があります。強い発熱、ぐったりしている、呼吸が苦しそう、症状が長引く場合は、医療機関にご相談ください。
Please note that every child is different. If your child has a high fever, seems very weak, has difficulty breathing, or symptoms continue for a long time, please consult a medical professional.

title: Acupuncture and Moxibustion in Treating Brain Problems. # How Moxibustion Support Brain Health
description:
# The Role of Acupuncture and Moxibustion in Treating Brain Problems
# How Acupuncture and Moxibustion Support Brain Health

The brain is vital for controlling thoughts, emotions, and bodily functions. Maintaining its health is essential, but various factors can impair brain function, including nutritional deficiencies, neurological disorders, injuries, and mental health issues. Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), especially acupuncture and moxibustion, offers supportive therapies to enhance brain health and aid recovery.

## Common Causes of Brain Problems

### Nutritional Deficiencies
Key nutrients like vitamin B12, vitamin D, omega-3 fatty acids, and iron are crucial for brain function. Deficiencies can cause memory issues, fatigue, and cognitive decline.

### Neurological Disorders
Conditions such as Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, multiple sclerosis, and epilepsy directly affect brain tissues, leading to progressive symptoms.

### Mental Health Conditions
Depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder involve imbalances in brain chemistry, impacting mood and cognition.

### Brain Injury and Trauma
Traumatic brain injuries from accidents can cause lasting cognitive and emotional issues.

### Developmental and Genetic Disorders
Autism, ADHD, and genetic syndromes influence brain development and function.

### Neurodegenerative Diseases
Progressive diseases like Huntington’s and ALS deteriorate nerve cells over time.

### Substance Abuse
Chronic drug and alcohol use damage neural pathways, impairing cognition.

### Infections
Encephalitis and meningitis cause brain inflammation, leading to neurological deficits.

---

## How Acupuncture and Moxibustion Promote Brain Health

### 1. Enhancing Blood Flow
Both therapies improve cerebral circulation, delivering oxygen and nutrients vital for brain repair and function.

### 2. Modulating Neurotransmitters
Acupuncture influences serotonin, dopamine, and endorphin levels, helping manage depression, anxiety, and mood swings.

### 3. Reducing Inflammation
They decrease inflammatory cytokines and oxidative stress, protecting neurons from damage.

### 4. Supporting Neuroplasticity
Stimulation of specific acupoints fosters brain adaptability, aiding recovery after injury.

### 5. Improving Sleep and Stress
Acupuncture promotes relaxation, enhances sleep quality, and balances the nervous system.

### 6. Specific Conditions
- **Dementia and Memory Loss:** Improves cognitive function.
- **Parkinson’s Disease:** Reduces tremors and improves mobility.
- **Stroke Rehab:** Aids motor and speech recovery.
- **Mental Health:** Alleviates depression and anxiety symptoms.

---

## Practical Application

### Key Acupoints
- **GV20 (Baihui):** Calms the mind, enhances mental clarity.
- **HT7 (Shenmen):** Relieves anxiety, insomnia.
- **ST36 (Zusanli):** Boosts vitality, neurological health.
- **GV24 (Shenting):** Improves focus and memory.

### Moxibustion
Applying heat to specific points can invigorate Qi, dispel cold, and promote healing, especially in deficiency states.

### Integrative Approach
Combining acupuncture and moxibustion with conventional treatments, herbal medicine, and lifestyle changes offers comprehensive care.

---

## Precautions
Ensure treatments are performed by qualified practitioners. Always consult healthcare providers before starting new therapies, especially if you have existing health conditions or are on medication.

---

## Conclusion

Acupuncture and moxibustion are promising supportive therapies for brain health. They help improve circulation, balance neurochemicals, reduce inflammation, and support brain plasticity. When integrated into a holistic treatment plan, these therapies can enhance recovery and overall neurological well-being.

If interested, consult with certified TCM practitioners to explore how these therapies can benefit your brain health journey.

title: 2,000 Years of Healing — Traditional Chinese Medicine Explained
description:
Traditional Chinese Medicine has been reading the human body for over 2,000 years,  through energy, touch, breath, and presence.

This short walks through six of its most enduring practices:
🌿 Moxibustion — burning mugwort to warm and wake the body's energy points
🌿 Qi Gong — moving the body to move the energy
🌿 Tongue & Pulse Diagnosis — reading what the body is already saying
🌿 Acupuncture — opening blocked meridian pathways with hair-thin needles
🌿 Tui Na — the same channels as acupuncture, opened through the hands alone
🌿 Cupping (Ba Guan) — drawing stagnant qi to the surface

These weren't discovered. They were always known.

NandawiVeda 🌿 Ancestral healing for modern life.

✨Follow for more ancestral healing wisdom across traditions worldwide.

「せりえ鍼灸室」公式チャンネルです。

延べ7万人以上の女性をサポートしてきた、

Description: The unique water quality and mild climate of Jing County in Anhui Province in eastern China are two of the key ingredients in the craft of making Xuan paper that thrives there. Handmade from the tough bark of the Tara Wing-Celtis or Blue Sandalwood tree and rice straw, Xuan paper is known for its strong, smooth surface, its ability to absorb water and moisten ink, and fold repeatedly without breaking. It has been widely used in calligraphy, painting and book printing. The traditional process passed down orally over generations and still followed today proceeds strictly by hand through more than a hundred steps such as steeping, washing, fermenting, bleaching, pulping, sunning and cutting  all of which lasts more than two years. The production of the Paper of Ages or King of Papers is a major part of the economy in Jing County, where the industry directly or indirectly employs one in nine locals and the craft is taught in local schools. True mastery of the entire complicated process is won only by a lifetime of dedicated work. Xuan paper has become synonymous with the region, where a score of artisans still keep the craft alive.
