project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Living Heritage: Xuan Paper
description:
Smooth yet tensile. Versatile and durable. With these qualities, Xuan paper is known as the "Paper of Ages".

The term Xuan paper first appeared in On Famous Paintings through the Ages, a book written by Tang Dynasty (618-907) scholar Zhang Yanyuan, in which he described Xuan paper as an ideal carrier for calligraphy and painting. Handmade Xuan paper from Jingxian county, East China's Anhui province, was listed as a tribute to the imperial court for its supreme quality during the period.

The traditional #craft of making Xuan paper is extremely demanding. Sandalwood bark, a plant native to southern China, goes through 108 procedures together with rice straw over the course of three years before it can transform into a batch of fine Xuan paper. The entire procedure is so complex that even the most skilled craftsman can only master limited number of steps.

The flow of ink, both guided and resisted by water, determines the output of Chinese art, and Xuan paper outshines others with its excellent ability to give full play to ink. Different proportions of bark to straw during the papermaking process can create different canvases best suited for artistic expression in freehand ink paintings or calligraphy. 

Xuan paper has accompanied the passionate brushstrokes of the Chinese literati for thousands of years. Unlike other forms of paper, it is very resistant to damage brought by time. It is this durability that has made the preservations of many valuable works from ancient China possible.

Papermaking is a crystallization of wisdom of the ancient Chinese, and Xuan paper is the zenith of papermaking. #LivingHeritage #XuanPaper

title: Chinese Rice Paper Explained | Sheng & Shu Xuan Paper
description:
We no longer stock this product, however we will leave this video up as it is a valuable resource for anyone getting into the artform!

Xuan Paper otherwise known as Rice Paper or Shuen Paper, is renowned for being soft and finely textured appearing as a thin, semi-opaque surface. Its delicate nature (40gsm approx.) renders it wholly suitable for both Chinese calligraphy, Chinese brush painting and printmaking. The characteristic of the paper and how pigment reacts to it is largely down to its absorbency - unsized (Raw) paper is porous and absorbent whilst lightly sized paper (Ripe) is less absorbent and more forgiving.

This is also both parts 1 and 2 of our Sheng and Shu paper Shorts videos.

title: How to Make Paper from Bamboo
description:
In this video, we showcase the fascinating process of making paper from bamboo, a sustainable and eco-friendly alternative to traditional paper-making. Bamboo, known for its fast growth and strength, is an excellent material for paper production. This process begins by harvesting bamboo, which is then shredded into fibers, boiled, and pulped to create the foundation for paper. The pulp is then spread onto a screen to form sheets, which are left to dry naturally. The resulting bamboo paper is strong, durable, and has a unique texture, making it ideal for writing, drawing, and even eco-conscious packaging.

The process of making bamboo paper is an example of how ancient techniques can be adapted to modern sustainability needs. Bamboo is an incredibly versatile material, and by using it to create paper, we can help reduce the environmental impact of traditional paper production. This method not only provides a unique, high-quality product but also showcases the endless possibilities of using natural, renewable resources in everyday life.

title: Rice paper omelette✨

▪️3 Eggs▪️Salt & Pepper▪️1 Scallion, sliced▪️1 Tbsp Olive Oil▪️1 Rice Paper▪️2 tsp Chili Oil▪️
.
1️⃣ Beat eggs with salt and pepper and set aside
2️⃣ Heat a pan over medium high and spread on a layer of oil; place a dry rice paper on the pan and top with scallions, salt and pepper
3️⃣ Slowly pour on the egg (if it seeps outside the rice paper perimeter push it back towards the rice paper with a spatula) and cook until egg is almost set.. drizzle with chili oil and then fold in half
4️⃣ Cut and eat like a quesadilla with more chili oil drizzled on top - enjoy!
.
⏲10 minute total prep + cook time

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Traditional Chinese Paper Trimming with a Half-Moon Knife
description:
A Little Wisdom Helps You Become Smarter !

Cutting a Stack of Xuan Paper with a Curved Hand Blade

In the video, a skilled artisan is using a traditional half-moon-shaped paper knife to trim a large stack of Xuan paper. The paper is tightly pressed under a simple wooden frame to keep it aligned and secure. With precise and steady hands, the worker pulls the sharp curved blade along the edge of the wooden guide, cutting smoothly through the entire stack in one motion. This traditional method is used in Chinese papermaking and bookbinding workshops to neatly trim the rough edges of handmade paper. The curved knife allows long, continuous cuts without damaging the delicate paper fibers. This technique ensures every sheet is uniformly sized with clean edges, ready for calligraphy, painting, or printing. The process highlights the efficiency and elegance of handmade paper finishing. Using simple tools, experienced crafters can achieve results that are difficult to replicate even with modern machines. This video offers a glimpse into a time-honored craft that values precision, patience, and practicality. Viewers can appreciate the seamless blend of tool, material, and human skill in this delicate yet decisive act of cutting.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Chinese Arts and Crafts: An Introduction to Xuanzhi
description:
Xuanzhi is a kind of paper used by Chinese intellectuals for calligraphy and painting. The biggest Xuanzhi factory—Anhui Hongxing Xuanzhi Factory—has sent the best workers from two affiliated factories to make a giant piece of Xuanzhi, which is 11.6 meters long and 3.3 meters wide. Even these experienced workers find it hard to adjust to such a large scale.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Xuan paper production process
description:
A little tip helps you become smarter!

The production process of Xuan paper mainly includes the following aspects:
 
Raw Material Selection
The main raw materials are the bark of the Pteroceltis tatarinowii tree and Shatian straw. The bark should be taken from the phloem of Pteroceltis tatarinowii trees that have grown for 6 to 8 years. As for Shatian straw, it is preferably produced in Jingxian County and its surrounding areas in Anhui Province. The straw is required to have long, slender, uniform fibers with few impurities.
 
Soaking and Retting
Soak the bark of the Pteroceltis tatarinowii tree and the straw separately in water to make them fully absorb water and soften. The soaking times vary. Generally, the bark needs to be soaked for 1 to 2 months, and the straw for about 1 week. After that, they are retted. During retting, natural microorganisms are used to decompose and wash away the impurities in the raw materials.
 
Cooking and Bleaching
Put the soaked and retted raw materials together with alkaline substances such as lime into a steamer for cooking. The bark of the Pteroceltis tatarinowii tree is cooked for about 8 to 12 hours, and the straw for about 4 to 6 hours. After cooking, bleaching is carried out. Traditionally, sun bleaching is often used to make the raw material fibers whiter and purer.
 
Pulping and Beating
Wash the raw materials that have been cooked and bleached to remove residual alkaline substances and other impurities. Then, beat the materials to make the fibers fibrillated and separated, turning them into a pulp suitable for papermaking, laying the foundation for the subsequent paper - forming process.
 
Paper - Scooping and Forming
Put the pulp into a paper trough, add water and stir it into a uniform pulp suspension. The paper - scooping worker holds a bamboo curtain and gently scoops in the pulp suspension, making the pulp evenly adhere to the bamboo curtain to form a wet paper sheet. This step plays a crucial role in determining the thickness and evenness of the paper.
 
Pressing and Dehydration
Transfer the wet paper sheet from the bamboo curtain to a wooden board, and apply a certain pressure through a pressing device to squeeze out a large amount of water from the wet paper sheet, making the paper take an initial shape and getting it ready for the subsequent drying process.
 
Drying and Finishing
Stick the pressed paper onto a drying wall made of adobe bricks and dry it with charcoal fire or steam heating. After drying, cut the Xuan paper according to the specified requirements to remove the rough edges. Finally, check each sheet of Xuan paper one by one for any flaws. The qualified products are packaged and stored in the warehouse in a certain quantity.

title: China Culture: Xuan Paper
description:
The craft of making #Xuanpaper is a remarkable example of traditional Chinese handmade papermaking. Originating in the #Tang Dynasty, it boasts a history of over 1,500 years. Xuan paper production involves 108 intricate steps—from material selection and pulping to sheet forming and drying—a process that can take two to three years to complete.
Xuan paper is often referred to as the “paper of a thousand-year lifespan.” In 2009, it was inscribed on #UNESCO’s Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. This ancient technique not only embodies the essence of traditional Chinese calligraphy and painting but also reflects generations of artisanship, wisdom, and devotion passed down through centuries. #ChinaCulture (Video by Huayun Bilingual Cultural Communication Society of North Minzu University)

title: Why Is Xuan Paper The Best Choice For Chinese Calligraphy? - Art Across Cultures
description:
Why Is Xuan Paper The Best Choice For Chinese Calligraphy? Have you ever wondered why certain papers are preferred for traditional Chinese calligraphy? In this video, we explore the unique qualities that make Xuan paper the top choice for artists practicing this ancient art form. We’ll explain what makes Xuan paper special, including its long history dating back over a thousand years and its origins in Jing County, Anhui Province, China. You’ll learn about the traditional handmade process using fibers from the Pteroceltis tatarinowii tree and rice straw, which results in a soft yet durable surface. 

We’ll discuss how the paper’s absorbency influences brushwork and ink flow, allowing artists to create expressive strokes with controlled spreading of ink. Different types of Xuan paper, such as Shengxuan, Shuxuan, and Banshuxuan, are examined, highlighting their unique qualities suited for various artistic styles and techniques. We’ll also cover the texture and resilience of Xuan paper, making it ideal for layered ink washes and long-lasting artworks. 

Whether you’re an aspiring calligrapher or an art enthusiast, understanding the significance of Xuan paper helps appreciate the craftsmanship behind traditional Chinese art. Join us to discover why this paper remains a vital tool for serious artists and how its qualities support the beauty and precision of Chinese calligraphy. title: Beating blue sandalwood bark: Extracting fibers for handmade Xuan papermaking
description: The bark of the blue sandalwood tree, also known as the blue sandalwood, is a natural material rich in fibers. The bark is tough and elastic, commonly used in traditional handmade papermaking. During the papermaking process, the bark is beaten and soaked to extract fibers, which are then used to make high-quality Xuan paper. The fibers from the blue sandalwood bark are fine in texture, helping the paper maintain good strength and flexibility, while also offering excellent water absorption and breathability. It is an essential material in Chinese traditional culture.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Preserving a Thousand-Year Legacy: The Fascinating World of Xuan Paper Art
description:
This unique paper, more valuable than gold, can only be produced in China, and creating a single sheet takes at least two years. The reason for its high value is that paintings made on it can last for thousands of years without decay, making it the dream canvas for artists. Xuan paper, originating from Jing County in Anhui Province, has a thousand-year history and is still part of the intangible cultural heritage today.

The traditional handmade Xuan paper, also known as the "King of Papers," represents the highest grade in the world of paper. The intricate 18-step process involves local Qingtan tree bark and Shatian rice straw as raw materials. The production requires skilled artisans, as current robotic technology cannot meet the highest requirements of calligraphy and painting, and only the traditional handmade process can satisfy the subtle variations in color and shading that renowned artists seek. This long-lasting, non-decaying cultural legacy is a testament to China's rich heritage.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: The Soul of Ink: Crafting Tengchong Xuan Paper by Hand for 100+ Years 📜🌿
description:
For over a century, artisans in the border town of Tengchong have transformed humble mulberry bark into one of the most revered papers in the world of calligraphy and painting. This is the story of Tengchong Xuan paper—a craft that demands patience, precision, and a deep understanding of nature's materials. In this documentary, we step into the workshops where this ancient art is still practiced by hand, following each step from bark selection to the final sheet.

In this film, you'll witness:
✅ The journey of mulberry bark: from mountain harvest to浸泡, steaming, and pounding
✅ The delicate art of sheet formation: dipping, lifting, and layering fibers by hand
✅ Why this paper is prized by artists: its perfect balance of absorbency, texture, and longevity
✅ The传承人's dedication: stories of families who have kept this craft alive for generations
✅ The challenges ahead: preserving tradition in a modern world

This is more than paper—it's a vessel for ink, a canvas for creativity, and a testament to the enduring power of handmade heritage.

description:
One hundred and eight steps are needed for a sheet of rice paper to be made, following a method that was developed by Chinese inventors hundreds of years ago, and widely used in arts and crafts. Thanks to the procedure, over 80 percent of the work is still performed manually. Mao Shengli has been making rice paper for the past 29 years and helping keep the tradition of Chinese rice paper making alive.

title: The king of paper - the making of xuan paper纸中之王-宣纸的制作 | 碎荷残雷 SuiHeCanLei
description:
Please  subscribe to 【碎荷残雷 SuiHeCanLei】碎荷残雷 SuiHeCanLei Channel on YouTube if you like my videos: 

 #chinesetraditions #traditional #碎荷残雷 #suihecanlei #xuanpaper #ricepaper 

【中国传统手艺匠人-彭南科】
以檀树皮为主要原料的宣纸，有着近1500年的历史。它质地绵韧，纹理美观，适用于书写绘画，被誉为“纸中之王”。
小时候为了让我和姐姐学写字，爸爸特意去浙江买了宣纸回来给我们练字。我做了涂鸦，姐姐抄了新华字典好几遍。现在我的字鸡爪，她的字有笔锋见龙蛇，我性格痞癞，姐姐展凰翅。贵坚韧，砺心性。

【Chinese traditional craftsman - Peng Nanke】
Xuan paper, which uses sandalwood bark as the main raw material, has a history of nearly 1,500 years. It has a soft texture and beautiful texture, which is suitable for writing and painting, and is known as the "king of paper".
When I was young, in order to let my sister and I learn to write, my father went to Zhejiang to buy xuan paper and came back to practice calligraphy for us. I made graffiti, and my sister copied the Xinhua dictionary several times. Now my calligraphy is chicken feet, while her calligraphy has the sharpness of a dragon and a snake. You are tenacious, sharpen your heart.

【碎荷残雷 SuiHeCanLei】
#suihecanlei #碎荷残雷

title: 宣纸抄纸 how to make the xuan paper for calligraphy
description:
#calligraphy #anhui 

Xuan paper is made by lifting bamboo screens from a pulp pool. The person on the left is the main worker, also called the "master of the screen," while the person on the right assists, known as the "screen lifter." When making paper, the bamboo screen is dipped into the pulp pool twice: the first dip is deep to collect more pulp, and the second dip is shallow to spread the pulp evenly. The skill of the master lies in achieving this evenness. Paper lifting is the most difficult step in the process of making Xuan paper, and paper workers earn the highest income in the entire paper-making industry.

宣纸是用竹帘从纸浆池里抄出来的，左边的是主抄，也叫掌帘，右边那个人叫抬帘，辅助掌帘。抄纸要将竹帘在纸浆池里抄两下，第一下要深，为了获得更多纸浆；第二下要浅，为了纸浆铺得均匀。而掌帘的厉害之处，就在于这个均匀的把控度。抄纸是宣纸制作工艺中最难的一步，抄纸工人的收入在整个造纸行业中也最高。

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Xuan Paper Cutting with Special Curved Knife
description: Craftsmanship thrives on the right tools, and this video proves it beautifully. In the clip, you’ll see someone using a special curved knife to cut off the excess part of Xuan paper. The knife’s unique curved blade allows it to glide smoothly through the delicate Xuan paper, making precise and clean cuts without tearing or wrinkling the material. As the knife moves along the edge, it neatly trims the extra paper, resulting in a perfectly shaped piece ready for uses like calligraphy, painting, or traditional crafts. This matters because Xuan paper is a treasured material in Chinese art and culture, valued for its texture and ink-absorbing properties; using the proper tool to cut it ensures the paper remains intact and maintains its quality for artistic creation. It also makes us reflect on how specialized tools—even ones as seemingly simple as this curved knife—are crucial for upholding traditional techniques. These tools connect craftsmanship and artistry, showing that the design details of a tool directly influence the beauty of the final work. In the end, this video not only demonstrates the process of cutting Xuan paper with a special curved knife but also celebrates the harmony between tools and traditional art. It reminds us that every detail, from the knife’s curve to the paper’s texture, plays a part in preserving cultural heritage through skilled craftsmanship.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Handmade Xuan Paper Crafting: Preserving Tradition
description:
"Artistry is born from the hands that shape the intangible into the tangible. In this video, you can witness the intricate process of handmade Xuan paper crafting. A craftsman is seen carefully handling a curved object, which appears to be part of the papermaking equipment. He wraps a piece of cloth around it, likely to assist in the filtering or pressing process of the Xuan paper.
The method of making Xuan paper involves several steps. First, raw materials like sandalwood bark and a type of grass)are soaked, cooked, and then beaten into a pulp. The pulp is then spread evenly on a screen - like tool, and through processes like filtering and pressing, the excess water is removed, and the paper gradually takes shape. The principle is to utilize the natural fibers of the raw materials, and through proper processing, transform them into a smooth, absorbent, and durable paper.
By doing this, the result is high - quality Xuan paper, renowned for its unique texture, excellent ink - absorption properties, and long - lasting nature. This is important because Xuan paper has been an essential medium for Chinese calligraphy, painting, and traditional arts for centuries. It not only carries the artistic creations but also the cultural heritage of China.
Safety is a concern in this process. When handling tools and raw materials, the craftsman should be careful to avoid cuts from sharp objects and skin irritation from certain chemicals used in the pulping process. To improve, using more ergonomic tools can enhance the comfort and efficiency of the craftsman during long - hours of work. Additionally, better ventilation in the workshop can reduce the inhalation of potentially harmful fumes during the cooking and pulping stages.
In conclusion, the handmade crafting of Xuan paper is a time - honored tradition that combines the essence of art and craftsmanship. It stands as a testament to the rich cultural heritage of China, and the efforts to preserve and continue this traditional craft are crucial for passing down this unique art form to future generations."

title: The Art of Handmade Xuan Paper
description:
A Little Wisdom Helps You Become Smarter!
The video you've shared offers a profound insight into the traditional craft of handmade Xuan paper, an intangible cultural heritage that deserves preservation and continuation.

Introduction to Handmade Xuan Paper:
Xuan paper, known for its quality and durability, is a type of paper traditionally used in Chinese calligraphy and painting. Its production is a meticulous and labor-intensive process.

Video Overview:
The Process: The video captures the intricate process of making Xuan paper by hand. It involves multiple steps, including pounding, shaping, and drying, each requiring skill and precision.
The Equipment: Simple yet effective tools like mallets and frames are used, highlighting the reliance on manual techniques in this ancient craft.

Why This Method is Vital:

Quality Assurance: Handmade Xuan paper ensures a superior quality product, valued by artists for its texture and absorbency.

Efficiency: While more time-consuming than machine-made paper, the handcrafted method allows for greater control over the paper's characteristics.

Customization: Each batch of paper can be tailored to specific artistic needs, making handmade Xuan paper a preferred choice for many artists.

Recommended Practices for Handmade Xuan Paper Production:

Ingredient Quality: Use high-quality raw materials to ensure the durability and quality of the paper.

Process Control: Maintain strict control over each step of the production process to ensure consistency in the final product.

Safety Measures: Implement safety protocols to protect workers from repetitive strain injuries and other occupational hazards.

Conclusion: This video underscores the importance of preserving traditional crafts like Xuan paper production. It highlights the expertise and dedication required in these crafts and the cultural value they hold. By understanding and appreciating these practices, we can help ensure that the wisdom and artistry of handmade Xuan paper continue to be passed down to future generations.

title: FAUX HANDMADE RICE PAPER: A HARD LESSON!! 😳 #youtubeshorts @tiffscreatives
description:
@tiffscreatives 
HANDMADE FAUX RICE PAPER...A HARD LESSON!
When making faux rice paper it's A MUST TO USE THE RIGHT GLUE!
I have been watching YouTube on how to make faux rice paper as real rice paper is expensive to buy. There were several I liked but when it came to glue most people referred to using any PVA glue mixed with 1/3 water. So that's what I did I used a PVA glue to make paper. When I was done with the test initially all was well. 24 hours later my glue had hardened on the project and started to crack. I tried another glue and another...It wasn't until the 4th or 5th glue that I found success. 

So, I thought I'd share my experience and help you find the the best way to make faux rice paper for yourself. Faux rice paper makes a great DIY craft, Junk journal embellishment, makes a cool Junk journal page, or paper craft. It's not only fun to do but makes a wonderful element that provides texture, color, and visual interest to many crafts, cards, or journal pieces. 

Have fun experimenting &
Happy crafting!

title: Rice Paper Flowers | How to make Translucent Rice Paper Flowers 🌸| Confirmation Cake |Dried Flowers
description:
Hello YouTube fam!
Hope everyone is doing well. 
Let me first start by Thanking all my subs ❤️ 
We made it 30K! woohoo!!! 🥂🍾 I'm so grateful for your support 🥰💕 So many of you have been with us from the beginning of our journey ❤️ sending you all my love and hugs 🤗🤗🤗 and hoping we get to see another milestone soon 😘

Our video features my daughter's cake that I made for her Confirmation last month.
I didn't film the entire process of the cake but I wanted to film more of the flowers.
I tried rice paper flowers for the first time 🙈 yeah I know... For my first time they were tricky but as you go along you seem to get the hang of it.

The template was simply drawn freehand and cut out. 
For each flower I used 3 small petals and 6 big petals.
Don't leave the petal in the water for too long as it'll get too soft and soggy.
Attaching the wire to the petal can be tricky at first but the key is patience. Gently wrap with the rice paper petal using a tweezer.
Leave to dry for a couple of hours and they're ready to use.
I tied 3 small petals first and the the bigger ones. Use a white thread preferably to tie.
The edge is lightly touched with gold paint.
For the middle I stuck a craft gem sticker using royal icing.

The dried flowers were attached to a wire or even better cocktail stick to be pierced into the cake.
The pins that were used to hold the acrylic name were later taken out.


Any other questions please leave in the comments below.

Here's how we also made a Rice Paper Rose 🌹 https://youtu.be/qWAedl6k8VA


I hope you enjoyed watching this tutorial. If you decide to make these toppers please don't forget to tag me #marias4cakes or @marias4cakes on Insta. We'd love to see your creation.

Suggestions for my next tutorial video are always welcome. Please leave them in the comments below ❤️

Thank you ever so much for supporting our channel. I know a lot of you like and share our videos. Please continue to do so and please subscribe if you haven’t. It helps me to keep this channel going 😘❤️

title: Ancient Xuan Paper Making! 📜 #TraditionalCrafts
description: Ever wondered how that thin, silky Xuan paper—used for Chinese calligraphy and paintings for over a thousand years—is made the old-fashioned way? This video lets you watch a master artisan at work, using a massive wooden mallet to pound bundles of plant fibers (a mix of sandalwood straw and wingceltis bark) into a fluffy, stringy pulp. Here’s the simple logic: the repeated pounding splits the fibers into tiny, thread-like strands, which interlock tightly when spread into a thin sheet with a bamboo screen. This interlocking is why Xuan paper is so strong yet soft, able to hold ink without bleeding and last for centuries without yellowing. The result isn’t just paper—it’s a piece of living history, preserving the art of handcraft that’s been passed down for generations. But let’s talk safety: that wooden mallet weighs a ton, so the artisan stays focused on their stance, keeping hands clear of the impact zone, and wears thick gloves to protect their palms from the rough fibers. The two biggest wins here? First, this handcrafted method keeps a UNESCO-listed intangible cultural heritage alive, ensuring future generations can learn and carry on the skill. Second, Xuan paper’s unique texture—unreplicable by machine—lets artists create delicate, nuanced works that would be impossible with modern paper. If you found this peek into ancient Chinese craftsmanship as fascinating as I did, hit that like button, share it with a friend who loves history or art, and subscribe for more down-to-earth looks at the traditional skills that shape our world!

description:
Bark and straw fibers drift within the crystal-clear currents of a wooden vat, waiting for the precise moment to be captured by a bamboo screen. This silent alchemy of water and fiber showcases a breathtaking Visual ASMR as each delicate sheet is lifted and pressed with rhythmic Craftsmanship. It is a Primal transformation of forest materials into a canvas that will last a thousand years, embodying the patient Rural Wisdom and refined Traditional Skills of the scholar’s heritage. The steady, wet slap of the fibers against the mold celebrates the quiet power of the Pure Handmade spirit.

title: Making Giant Chinese Xuan Paper: How Artisans Cut 'Si Zhang Si' for Traditional Art
description: Craft is the soul of art, and making giant Xuan paper is a testament to Chinese traditional craftsmanship. In this video, you can see artisans carefully cutting a huge sheet of Xuan paper. They follow traditional methods, ensuring precision in every cut to maintain the paper’s integrity. This Xuan paper, known as ‘Si Zhang Si’, is made for traditional Chinese painting and calligraphy. The process relies on age-old techniques that preserve the paper’s quality, making it ideal for artistic creation. The result is a large, high-quality sheet of Xuan paper, ready to be used by artists to create magnificent works. This is important because Xuan paper is a cornerstone of Chinese art, and preserving its crafting methods helps pass down cultural heritage. It makes us think about how traditional crafts continue to play a vital role in modern art and culture. So, let’s appreciate the skill and dedication behind cutting giant Xuan paper, a craft that bridges the past and present in the art world.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: 宣纸 Xuan paper #springonshorts
description:
Xuan paper originates from Jing County, Anhui Province, named after ancient Xuanzhou. It is a precious traditional Chinese painting and calligraphy paper with a thousand-year history. It took shape in the Tang Dynasty, was widely used in painting and calligraphy in the Song and Yuan dynasties, and matured in the Ming and Qing dynasties as a treasure among the Four Treasures of the Study.
Made of wingceltis bark and sandy-field rice straw via over a hundred manual procedures, it features tough texture, even ink absorption, insect resistance and durability, famed as "millennium-lasting paper".
Favored by ancient artists, it bears countless classic artworks. Ideal for calligraphy, painting, ancient book reproduction and seal mounting, its craftsmanship is now listed as intangible cultural heritage, an indispensable gem of oriental ink culture.

title: Xuan Paper vs Non-Chinese Paper for Calligraphy Effects
description:
How to mix gold pigment for Chinese calligraphy 泥金製作 
http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DN4qOtBsyLs
Gold pigments ( 金粉 ) will be available from forbiddencity2008 on e-Bay after December, 2010.
http://stores.ebay.com/Big-Teapot-Brush-Jade-Dress-eStore/_i.html?_fsub=336676&_sc=1&_sop=1

Chinese characters used in this video
望 hope; behold; view
癒 healed; recover ( 痊癒 ) (The simplified version is  愈 which also means "even more.") Both 癒 and 愈 have same pronunciations.
道 the Way; Dao; Tao (pronunciation is dao4)
家 home
道家 the Daoist School in China; Taoist School
畫 (painting) compared with 謝 (thank)

1:11 Retouches ( 描 ) are not a correct and honest way to practice Chinese calligraphy in Chinese culture; 描 is not allowed in Chinese calligraphy contests. Due to the inferior absorbency of this paper, I did retouches a couple of times when writing 癒. The only advantage of this paper is that it is not absorbing water as much and fast as Xuan paper, thus allowing slower and easier writing at the sacrifice of other unique features of Chinese calligraphy such as 疾與澀 and 飛白.

Xuan Paper (mistakenly called rice paper) allows more agile and nuanced movements of Chinese brushes due to the compatible ingredients of Chinese ink sticks and Xuan paper (and other calligraphy paper in different names.)
書法疾與澀 "Fast and Slow" (this is not an appropriate translation for 疾與澀 since 疾 and 澀 have some metaphysical implications for the beauty of Chinese calligraphy) 
疾與澀 is a unique term for the study of Chinese calligraphy theories and philosophy (not for beginners.)
http://www.google.com/search?hl=en&lr=lang_en&gbv=2&tbs=lr%3Alang_1en&q=%E7%96%BE%E8%88%87%E6%BE%80&btnG=Search&aq=f&aqi=&aql=&oq=&gs_rfai=


飛白 Flying White Effects
http://www.google.com/search?hl=en&q=%E6%9B%B8%E6%B3%95%E9%A3%9B%E7%99%BD&btnG=Search&aq=f&aqi=&aql=&oq=&gs_rfai=

The above references may provide reasons why it's best to practice Chinese calligraphy without using Magic Paper (Water Writing Paper, Zen Board, Buddha Board) and the mislabeled "rice paper" (this is actually not the Xuan paper! but a substandard Cotton Paper which is more absorbent than Xuan paper) sold outside Asian countries.

title: Iris on Semi-sized Xuan Paper: Easy Chinese Flower Painting
description:
One of the most difficult adjustments for artists who have backgrounds in other forms of painting like Western watercolor is to adjust to the idea that strokes in the spontaneous or xieyi style of Chinese painting must be done decisively, and left alone. This is especially true while working on raw xuan paper.

To ease the transition students can work with xuan paper that has sizing. Sizing is an alum coating that is sprayed on the paper to help it resist moisture. Semi-sized xuan paper has a partial coating so some of the moisture / color is allowed to absorb while some stays on the top and can be manipulated afterward.

In this lesson, Ling Chi shows a combination of spontaneous style technique, wet on wet technique and fineline technique to complete an iris composition. This is excellent for students looking for an easier transition from the mindset of a western painter to the mindset of an eastern painter.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: How Xuan Paper Survived 1,000 Years! The Paper That Never Dies
description:
Xuan paper is one of China’s oldest surviving crafts — a material so durable that artists call it “the paper that never dies.”
Made from blue sandalwood bark and rice straw, each sheet is lifted by hand and polished to last for centuries.
Forgotten Techniques brings you the world’s rarest crafts, preserved through the hands that keep tradition alive.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Ancient Paper Alchemy: Watch 1,000-Year-Old Techniques Create the World's Most Prized Paper!
description:
Timeless Craftsmanship - The Sacred Art of Making Paper That Lasts a Millennium!
This mesmerizing footage reveals the painstaking process behind creating the legendary "rice paper" treasured by calligraphers worldwide.

Why This Matters:
• Cultural Heritage: UNESCO-listed intangible cultural asset
• Artistic Perfection: The only paper that truly showcases ink nuances
• Scientific Marvel: Acid-free composition ensures 1,000+ year lifespan

The Crafting Process:
• Bark Harvesting: Carefully selected sandalwood tree fibers
• Fermentation: 3-month natural fermentation in mountain springs
• Pulp Preparation: Hand-beaten to perfect consistency
• Sheet Formation: Bamboo screen dipping technique

Key Considerations:
• Water Quality: Must be pH-neutral mountain spring water
• Fiber Ratio: Precise blend of bark and rice straw
• Drying Conditions: Natural shade drying only
• Seasonal Timing: Best made in late spring

Pro Artisan Tips:
• "Listen to the Pulp": Sound indicates proper consistency
• Screen Mastery: 17-degree dipping angle
• Fiber Alignment: Creates perfect ink absorption
• Patience Pays: No shortcuts in traditional methods

The Science Behind It:
Xuan paper's magic comes from:
• Unique fiber interlocking structure
• Natural pH balance (7.2-7.8)
• Micro-porous surface texture
• Lignin-free composition

Bonus Paper Facts:
• 108 exacting steps
• 3 years to master basics
• $100/sheet for premium grades
• Used by emperors since Tang Dynasty

This living heritage proves some traditions are truly timeless - where every sheet carries the soul of Chinese civilization in its fibers!

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Four Great Inventions: Ancient Chinese Rice Paper Making Process
description: Discover the timeless craft of Xuan paper (rice paper)—one of China’s most celebrated traditional arts linked to the Four Great Inventions! This short showcases the labor-intensive process: selecting mulberry bark and rice straw, soaking, beating into pulp, spreading on bamboo screens, pressing, and air-drying. Witness the patience and skill that create the smooth, durable paper prized by artists and calligraphers for centuries. Perfect for culture enthusiasts, artists, or anyone fascinated by ancient crafts. Hit like to honor this heritage, and subscribe for more traditional Chinese craft videos!

title: Chrysanthemum on Olive Xuan Paper
description:
Ling Chi is back revisiting the iconic Chrysanthemum. Chrysanthemum is one of the essential "Four Gentlemen" subjects of Chinese brush painting (Orchid, Bamboo, Chrysanthemum, and Plum). Each represents a season and Chrysanthemum is the Gentleman of Autumn.

Along with the basic strokes for petals, leaves, and stem, Ling Chi demonstrates and discusses ways to effectively paint on colored xuan paper. Painting on our Olive Xuan paper Ling Chi shows how to use different whites and metallic/pearlescent colors to create interesting contrast on colored xuan paper.

Two reference books are mentioned. There is Su-sing Chow's book Fundamentals of Chinese Floral Painting: Chrysanthemum Vol. 4, Book of the Chrysanthemum.
https://orientalartsupply.com/products/fundamentals-of-chinese-floral-painting-chrysanthemum-v4

Also, there is a reference to the Orchid Lesson in Ning Yeh's new Chinese Brush Painting: An Instructional Guide Gold Edition as a simpler subject to practice the petal stroke used for the Chrysanthemum.
https://orientalartsupply.com/products/chinese-brush-painting-an-instructional-guide-gold-edition-by-ning-yeh

title: Conservation: Korean Hanji Paper Making
description: Come on a journey into Korean Hanji paper making, part of ongoing conservation research into oriental papers. This process is explained by master paper maker Jang Seong Woo to V&A paper conservator Susan Catcher, from the choosing of the raw material, to the cooking, cleaning and pulping that enables a paper sheet to be cast using a bamboo frame or bal. The beauty of the final hanji paper after drying on a heated wall and burnishing is seen in the stacked sheets, some of which returned to the Museum’s paper conservation studio.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Xuan City: Hometown of Chinese Calligraphy / Painting Four Treasures Part 1 (Brush making processes)
description:
The Xuan writing brush, together with the famous Xuan paper, is made in Jing County, Anhui Province. In ancient times, Jing County was under the jurisdiction of Xuanzhou Prefecture, where the product got its name.
Scholars of the Jin Dynasty (256-420) were especially fond of the Xuan brush. During the Tang (618-907) and Song (960-1279) dynasties, Xuanzhou became a writing brush manufacturing center, and the Xuan brush was listed as a tribute to emperors. At that time, folk artisans made a breakthrough in craftsmanship in selecting materials and polishing the shaft. Brushes were both sharp and neatly cut, and plump and smooth at the tip, enabling artists to write and paint freely. The Xuan brushes, made elaborately from brownish rabbit hair, are considered to be the best and come at a very high price.
Because of wars in the Song Dynasty, brush makers moved out of Xuan City toward the south as a consequence, Hu Zhou brushes also became very famous.

title: The Art of the Oil-Paper Umbrella: A Tradition of Craftsmanship
description:
The oil-paper umbrella, an intangible cultural heritage passed down for thousands of years, is one of China’s traditional handicrafts. The creation of the umbrella involves multiple steps, including material selection, making the umbrella frame, mounting the arc, and covering the umbrella surface. The umbrella surface uses high-quality Xuan paper, which is coated with copper oil multiple times to make it waterproof, sun-resistant, and durable. The frame is made from high-quality bamboo, which is roasted, bent, and treated to form a strong and durable structure.

The oil-paper umbrella is not only a practical item but also a cultural symbol. title: Xuan Paper Core Process – Pounding Bark with a Stone Hammer 📜
description:
Watch a skilled worker operate a giant wooden lever that lifts and drops a heavy stone hammer onto piles of blue sandalwood bark. Each slow, powerful thump breaks down the plant fibers, turning tough bark into soft, flexible pulp. This ancient technique, called “stuffing” (or “pounding the material”), is the secret behind Xuan paper’s legendary durability – “a thousand years without decay.” No chemicals, just brute force and centuries of tradition.

Let's Talk About This Ancient Papermaking Step
Have you ever seen Xuan paper up close – it feels soft yet incredibly strong? Do you think hand‑pounding fibers in this way gives paper a longer life than machine‑beating? How long do you think a single worker keeps this rhythmic hammering going before a break? What would be harder: aiming the falling weight perfectly, or keeping the bark pile even? Share your own experiences with handmade paper, calligraphy, or traditional crafts that rely on pure physical labor in the comments – we want to hear from artists, historians, and anyone who marvels that a sheet of paper can last a millennium.

Pound, Fluff, Shape
If this ancient fiber‑beating fascinated you, hit like and subscribe for more traditional craft processes and the hidden work behind timeless materials. Tag a friend who loves calligraphy but has no idea how Xuan paper is made 😄

title: How to dry-mount a sumie or Chinese painting done on rice paper with silicone release paper
description:
This video shows the dry-mounting process of this free style Chinese painting done on single Xuan rice paper. This method is to transfer the silicon adhesive film to the backing material first, then take the release paper off and mount the painting on.  Here are the steps:
1. Cut the Silicone Release Paper to the same size as the backing board or a little bit smaller (about 1/4" - 1/8") than the board. (No need to measure!) 

1. Place the silicone release paper filme side facing down, or mounting board, foam board etc.

2. Iron it at 180 degree fahrenheit and let it cool a little bit. 

4. Peel the release paper off and save it for use in step 6.

5. If necessary, trim the extra adhesive film along the board edge with a glue gun, knife or scissors.

6. Lay the artwork face up on the adhesive film and cover it with the release paper from step 4.

7. Now it's ready to iron it on. 

*Silicone release paper is available at http://www.BlueHeronArts.com

title: Xuanzhi Paper Museum in China is a quiet place where history, craft and nature meet in one material
description:
Here it is possible to see how ordinary plant fibers slowly turn into the delicate, strong sheets that artists and calligraphers have used for centuries.

The museum walks through the whole journey of Xuan paper: from water, bark and pulp to pressing, drying and polishing each sheet by hand. Old tools, wooden vats, bamboo screens and samples of different paper types show how carefully every step is controlled. In some halls there are demonstrations where masters scoop, shake and lift the frames, and the future paper appears right in front of visitors’ eyes.

It is not only about techniques. The museum also explains why Xuan paper is so important for Chinese culture – how it helped preserve paintings, books and calligraphy, and why it is still chosen by many modern artists. A visit here feels like entering a living workshop, where time slows down and every sheet of paper seems to breathe.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Hammer Tree Bark To Make A Special Paper !
description:
A Little Wisdom Helps You Become Smarter !

During the production process of xuan paper, the beating of raw materials (mainly the bark of wingceltis trees and paddy straw from Shantian) is a key procedure.
 
Preparation of Raw Materials
 
- Firstly, it's necessary to select high-quality bark of wingceltis trees. Usually, wingceltis trees that have grown for many years are felled in specific seasons. After undergoing preliminary treatments like soaking and peeling, the bark is collected. The paddy straw from Shantian also needs to be selected, with impurities and moldy parts removed !

- Then, the prepared bark of wingceltis trees and paddy straw are mixed in a certain proportion (this proportion varies among different varieties of xuan paper). After that, they are put into water for soaking so that they can fully absorb water and soften. This soaking process may last for several days, preparing for the subsequent beating !
 
Beating Process
 
- Tool Selection : 
A special wooden mallet will be used. This kind of wooden mallet is usually made of hard wood and has a certain weight. Besides, the shape of the mallet head is designed to better contact the raw materials and evenly distribute the force. Meanwhile, the beating operation is carried out in a specially made stone trough or wooden trough. The size and depth of the trough should be suitable for placing the raw materials and for the performance of the beating actions !

- Beating Method : 
Workers hold the wooden mallet and beat the soaked raw materials rhythmically. At the beginning, a relatively moderate force is used to break up the raw materials first. As the beating proceeds, the force is gradually increased with the aim of gradually crushing and breaking up the fiber bundles of the bark and straw. The beating actions should cover the raw materials comprehensively to ensure that every part can be beaten evenly. From the edge to the center of the raw materials, repeated beatings are needed to fully separate the fibers. This process may last for a relatively long time, such as several hours or even a whole day. The specific time depends on the quantity of raw materials and the degree of fiber separation desired !

- Quality Control : 
During the beating process, craftsmen will check the state of the raw materials from time to time. They judge whether the requirements are met by observing the dispersion of the fibers. If the fibers are still obviously in bundles and not broken up, the beating needs to continue. When the fibers present a uniform, flocculent form and can be evenly dispersed when stirred in water, it indicates that the beating is qualified and the raw materials can enter the next procedure !
 
This beating procedure is crucial to the final quality of xuan paper, as it directly affects many key properties of xuan paper, such as fiber distribution, toughness and water absorption !

title: 揭秘中国安徽传承古法宣纸制作过程
description:
Rice paper, Anhui Province Xuancheng Jing county specialty, national geographic indication products.
Jing County is located in the south of the middle latitude, according to the classification of meteorological indicators, belongs to the north subtropical and subtropical monsoon humid climate. Mild climate, abundant rainfall, abundant light resources, spring, summer, autumn, winter four distinct seasons. The average annual temperature is 15.7℃, and the maximum annual extreme temperature is 40.8℃. There are about 1000 kinds of herbaceous plants in Jingxian County, which are suitable for the materials and manufacture of rice paper. During the reign of Tianbao in the Tang Dynasty, among the tributes delivered to Chang 'an in the capital from all over the country, there were "paper and pens" and other tributes in the Xuancheng County ship, which indicates that Xuancheng County had produced paper and pens at that time. Rice paper is a unique handicrafts in China. It has the characteristics of soft texture, smooth and clean like jade, no decay and no decay, and the ink rhyme changes. It enjoys the reputation of "thousand-year life paper" and is known as "national treasure". Painting with rice paper inscriptions, ink rhyme clear, distinct layers, bones and storage, momentum overflowing show, thick but not muddy, light but not gray, its word and its painting, on the paper, flying, flying eyes bright.
In August 2002, rice paper was successfully approved as China's National Geographic Indication protection product title.

title: Traditional Chinese Handmade | The Complete Process of Making Paper-Cut Clothing
description:
The Paper-Cut Clothing is a traditional Chinese handmade masterpiece that transforms delicate rice paper into a wearable sculpture of light and shadow. It represents the perfect blend of folk-art precision and the eternal elegance of ancient Chinese civilization.

First, layers of high-quality, sun-dried red Xuan paper are selected and stiffened using a traditional natural glue made from glutinous rice and alum. The artisan meticulously traces intricate patterns of dragons, phoenixes, and blooming peonies onto the surface, each line a whisper from 1,000 years of heritage. Meanwhile, fine silk threads and bamboo strips are prepared to create the "bones" and structure that will support the delicate paper body.

Using specialized knives of various sizes, the artisan performs the arduous task of "hollow cutting," removing tiny fragments of red paper one by one. This process requires absolute focus, as a single slip could shatter the artwork. After weeks of silent cutting, the thousands of hollow shapes are brought to life. Finally, the fragile paper panels are hand-stitched onto the structured silk base, creating a shimmering red armor of negative space.

The finished Paper-Cut Clothing is a breathtaking fusion of fragility and strength, releasing a mystical scarlet glow as light filters through its intricate patterns — a true embodiment of traditional craftsmanship and the sophisticated soul of Eastern aesthetics.

title: How Ancient Chinese Make Paper from Tree Bark? #shorts #papermaking #craft
description:
Soft yet strong, mulberry paper has been used for thousands of years in writing, painting, and bookmaking. Made from the inner bark of the mulberry tree, this paper is valued for its durability and natural texture.

🇨🇳 China – Ancient Papermaking Regions
Mulberry paper is traditionally produced in provinces like Anhui, Sichuan, Yunnan, and Zhejiang, where papermaking techniques have been passed down for generations.

🏞️ Rural Papermaking Villages
In small villages, artisans still strip mulberry bark by hand, soak it, cook it with ash, and beat it into pulp before forming each sheet by hand.

🇯🇵 Japan – Washi Paper Centers
In places like Gifu and Kochi, mulberry paper (known as kozo washi) is made using similar traditional methods and prized for calligraphy and art.

🇰🇷 Korea – Hanji Villages
Korean mulberry paper, called hanji, is found in historic papermaking towns and is known for its strength and long lifespan.

🛍️ Art Supply & Specialty Paper Shops
Authentic mulberry paper can be found in art stores, calligraphy shops, museum stores, and traditional craft markets.

Mulberry paper isn’t just paper—it’s a living link to one of humanity’s greatest inventions.

title: The Paper Shield | Weaving Raw Silk & Micro-Bamboo into the Millennium Papermaking Screen 🤫🎋🧵
description:
Turn your volume UP and step into the silent, hypnotic world of ancient Chinese literary heritage. 🎧📜

True eternity is woven by hand. Today, we unfold a legendary chapter of the "Four Treasures of the Study": crafting the sacred Bamboo Screen (Zao Zhi Zhu Lian) used to scoop traditional Xuan paper. Honoring a thousand-year-old non-heritage ritual, we split resilient green bamboo into threads as thin as hair, binding each one meticulously with raw mountain silk. Through endless hours of micro-weaving, we build the crucial instrument that captures floating paper pulp from scratch. 🌾🎋🏺

No talking. No industrial machinery. No modern hurry. Just the deeply relaxing, unedited ASMR of an elite artisan's sanctuary: the high-frequency "shack-shack" of a blade refining raw fibers, the delicate rustle of thousands of silk threads locking bamboo teeth together, and the ultimate, poetic "splash" as the finished screen dips into the milky white pulp, rising to reveal a single, perfect sheet of paper that will endure for a millennium. ⏳✨

Take a deep breath, watch the liquid fibers settle, and let the quiet focus of history wash your stress away.

title: Chinese Rice Paper Maker Sticks to Traditional Hand making Methods
description:
In a time when advancements in technology have made it easier to produce paper, Mao Shengli, a traditional Chinese rice paper maker, is sticking to the traditional and complicated procedures of making the delicate material in order to better present Chinese culture. 

Great art comes with great artists. But Mao is a different kind of artist. He is a traditional Chinese rice paper maker, or to be more specific a rice paper dryer. It is a kind of profession that may only exist in Chinese culture nowadays as rice paper is irreplaceable as one of the four treasures for the finest Chinese paintings and calligraphy works. 

Throughout Mao's life, he has been preoccupied with the traditional way of making rice paper which consists of 108 procedures and 80 percent of them are operated by human hands. 

Twenty-nine years ago, Mao picked up a brush for the first time and became an apprentice in a factory. Most others left because they could not stand the hot temperature and humidity, but he stayed. Now he has become one of the best and still has not stopped.

In Jingxian County of east China's Anhui Province, lies the origin of traditional rice paper. Mao, along with more than a hundred workers, has just finished their "big paper" experiment – a piece of paper with a length of 11 meters and a width of 3.6 meters compared to the usual length of 6.6 meters for normal rice paper. 

The first key step is filtering the paper pulp through a bamboo tapestry. Forty-four workers at both sides of the tapestry work together to finish the very delicate procedure. They stick to the same pace and listen to the water in order to make the pulp evenly precipitate on the tapestry. Then a piece of wet paper is formed. The workers then press a pile of wet paper in order to squeeze out the water. After drying for one day, then it's Mao and two other workers' turn. 

In ancient times, the drying process took place outdoors. But now, it has turned indoors with a special kind of wall-like black heater. Mao soaks a piece of white coarse linen into the rice water then wraps it around a wooden lath. Next, he paints the rice water on the heater. The density of the rice water and temperature on the heater's surface all decide whether the paper can stick on it perfectly. The whole process has no numerical standards. All is based on Mao's experience. Then he stands on a three-meter high platform and starts the first brush to paste the water onto the heater. 

"It has to be accurate. Even if the top of the paper is just slightly crooked, the bottom may drag onto the ground. So it has to be accurate when you lay it on the heater," said Mao. 

Mao's work is of course perfect without any bubbles or wrinkles in between.

The two most daunting challenges for rice paper dryers are "big paper" and "thin paper." The first one is accomplished now, but the second one follows. The Shanghai Museum is currently repairing ancient paintings of the Song Dynasty (960-1279 A.D.), which requires a very special kind of Chinese rice paper called "Qixiu" (literally meaning "ingenious embroidery"), with an extremely demanding technique called "Zhahua" (literally meaning "embroidery"). 

"As thin as cicada's wings and as pure as snow, shaking like fine silk without sound. This is the description of " Zhahua." Yet, Qixiu is the most difficult in Zhahua, weighing only half of normal paper,"said Wang Shuicai, a factory manager at the China Rice Paper Co. Ltd.

Fortunately, the museum has found Mao, who has dealt with numerous daunting tasks before.

"For Zhahua, you cannot rub the paper. You have to tap very gently in order to separate the pieces. But even if you know how to tap, you are not sure if it's one or two pieces of paper that gets separated because the paper is just so thin that you can't tell. My teacher taught me how to tap for a week until my figure was swollen, " said Mao.

After studying with his co-workers, Mao decides to use a more traditional way -- to paint the rice water in circles so that it can be better pasted on the heater. Here, Qixiu comes alive again. Mao is satisfied. 

According to Mao, although the paper making industry has mostly been equipped with machines, the traditional way of making paper will never be replaced. To him, it's more than a job.http://www.cctvplus.com/news/20161004/8033584.shtml#!language=1

"Rice paper is an attachment to us. I, myself, am proud of it," said Mao.More on:Subscribe us on Youtube: https://www.youtube.com/c/CCTVPlus

title: Ancient Chinese Rice Paper Cutting: Intangible Cultural Heritage Craft ✂️
description:
Discover the traditional cutting technique for Chinese rice paper (xuan paper)—a UNESCO-worthy intangible heritage! Fold dried rice paper neatly, use a sharp traditional blade, and cut steadily with even pressure to preserve its toughness and fiber integrity.  
Dive into the fascinating world of ancient Chinese craftsmanship with this step-by-step guide to cutting rice paper (xuan paper)—a cherished intangible cultural heritage! This isn’t just a cutting tutorial—it’s a celebration of centuries-old wisdom that’s kept traditional paper-making alive.  

Here’s how the age-old technique works: First, stack sun-dried rice paper neatly, aligning the edges precisely to avoid misalignment. Grab a specialized sharp blade designed for delicate paper crafts (blunt tools risk fraying the fibers!). Hold the blade steadily with both hands, maintain balanced pressure, and cut smoothly along your marked line—no dragging, no pressing too hard. The key? Even, consistent movement to protect the paper’s natural toughness and delicate texture, a detail that makes traditional rice paper perfect for calligraphy, painting, and art.  

What makes this craft so special? It relies on hand-eye coordination and respect for the material—no rigid rulers, just the craftsman’s experience to ensure clean cuts that preserve the paper’s fiber integrity. This step is at the heart of China’s ancient papermaking heritage, a skill passed down through generations.

title: Paper Making I Wasli Making I Miniature Painting I Art Practice @ShumailsStudio - 5
description:
Most often used to accompany manuscripts and art books, Mughal miniatures were small, vividly colored, and extremely detailed paintings. Even though they are so little, they are extraordinarily exact, with some lines being painted with brushes made of only one hair. 
A brief blog post could never do justice to the amount of knowledge and abilities that the past masters of traditional Indian painting received from earlier generations. No way! The preparation of the paper, color, and detail are only a few of the many traditional skills that make up the distinctive appeal of Indian miniature painting, but I can also give some insights here into some helpful  equipment and process I have used throughout my recent workshop of material making.
Prior to applying the tiny artwork, the paper surface must be properly prepared. Wasli paper has typically been employed. This was created of numerous layers of handcrafted paper that were adhered using a paste made of starch and polished to smooth up the surface. Smooth Hot Pressed Watercolor Paper is what I use for my artwork (300 gsm).
In order to stop paint and ink from leaking through the paper surface, burnishing is required.. For polishing the surface of paper, I used a large cowrie shell, ceramic spoon and replaced agate costly burnisher.  It only takes approximately 10 to 15 minutes for the surface to feel smooth and have a delicate shine visible in the light by using slow, gentle strokes up and down, then left to right.

title: Top 20 Ancient Chinese Inventions From Xuan Paper to Gunpowder-The Innovations Shaping Today's World
description: Explore the incredible ingenuity of ancient Chinese civilization in this fascinating dive into the top 20 inventions that have shaped both their world and ours. project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Crafting Tradition: How Centuries-Old Techniques Create Timeless Paper
description:
The Art of Xuan Paper Making
Xuan paper, a renowned type of Chinese paper, has been crafted for centuries using traditional methods that combine skill, patience, and a deep understanding of materials. The process involves several intricate steps, each requiring precise control and a keen eye for detail. This video captures a craftsman in the midst of creating Xuan paper, showcasing the timeless techniques that have been passed down through generations.

Historical Background: Xuan paper has a rich history dating back to the Tang Dynasty. It was originally used for calligraphy and painting, valued for its ability to absorb ink and maintain its shape over time. The craft has been recognized as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage, highlighting its cultural significance and the need for its preservation.

Materials and Tools: The primary materials used in Xuan paper making include the bark of the blue sandalwood tree and rice straw. These materials are carefully selected and processed to create the pulp. The tools used, such as the paper mold and the press, are traditional and have been used for centuries, emphasizing the craft's connection to its historical roots.

The Crafting Process: The process begins with the preparation of the pulp, which is then poured into a paper mold. The craftsman carefully spreads the pulp evenly across the mold, ensuring a uniform thickness. The mold is then lifted out of the water, allowing the excess water to drain. The paper is then pressed and dried, resulting in a sheet of Xuan paper that is both durable and versatile.

Cultural Significance: Xuan paper is not just a material; it is a symbol of cultural heritage and artistic expression. It has been used by famous calligraphers and painters throughout history, and its unique properties make it ideal for traditional Chinese art forms. The craft of making Xuan paper is a testament to the skill and dedication of the craftsmen who have kept this tradition alive.

Preservation and Innovation: While the traditional methods of Xuan paper making are still widely used, there is also a growing interest in innovating the process to meet modern demands. Craftsmen are exploring new materials and techniques to create Xuan paper that is both authentic and adaptable to contemporary uses.

In conclusion, the craft of Xuan paper making is a beautiful blend of history, culture, and artistry. The meticulous process and the dedication of the craftsmen ensure that this timeless tradition continues to thrive, preserving the cultural heritage for future generations. The video serves as a reminder of the importance of preserving such traditions and the beauty that can be found in the art of craftsmanship.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: The Technique Of Making Handmade Paper Has Existed For Over 1500 Years #job #skills #workers
description: Hello Everyone !In this video, the artisans share with us an important part of the process of making Xuan paper – a famous traditional Chinese paper, known as the "king of papers" with a history of over 1,500 years. In the video, the artisans are performing the hand-rolling process, which is the most technically demanding step: * Paper-rolling mold: The artisans use an extremely fine bamboo screen attached to a large wooden frame. Controlling this frame requires the rhythmic coordination of the entire body to spread the pulp evenly on the screen surface. * Pulp tank: The water in the tank contains a mixture of processed sandalwood (Pteroceltis tatarinowii) bark fibers and straw. A key element is the viscous liquid (usually from the roots of elm or magnolia trees) mixed into the water to help the paper fibers float evenly, preventing them from settling to the bottom. The papermaking process: The artisan immerses the frame in water, gently shaking it in various directions to interweave the cellulose fibers, creating a strong, durable network structure. Then, the wet paper is separated from the screen and stacked into a thick pile (as you see at the end of the video).This type of paper possesses unique characteristics that machines cannot perfectly replicate: * Extremely high durability: Xuan paper is resistant to termites, mold, and does not become brittle over time. It is often said to have a "lifespan of a thousand years" (Thiên niên thọ chỉ). * Absorbency (Ink absorption): This is the most important characteristic for calligraphy and ink painting. The paper has the ability to absorb a variety of inks, creating beautiful "bleeding" (xuan) effects, helping to express the subtle black and white shades of the ink. * Natural whiteness: The paper is whitened using sunlight and natural spring water instead of harsh bleaching chemicals, helping to protect the paper's fiber structure.

title: Handmade Chinese mulberry paper
description:
These unique papers are crafted from the natural bark of the Paper Mulberry Tree, Broussonetia papyrifera. Mulberry papers are known by many names including Kozo, Rice Paper, Hanji, Xuan Paper and Unryu. They are eco-friendly and sustainable.

USES:
The long fibers of mulberry paper gives it a soft feel, yet it is more durable than traditional papers. Because mulberry paper has a neutral pH, it makes a wonderful art paper for long-lasting projects. Natural Mulberry paper is prized for use in collage, painting, scrapbooking, note cards, lamp shades, wedding invitations, fish rubbings, and calligraphy.  Tear the edges of Mulberry Paper to get a soft, feathered, deckled edge, creating a perfect accent for projects such as stationery, wedding invitations and scrapbooks. In China it's also traditionally used for sealing wine jars and as paper strips for moxibustion.
The many kinds and weights of Mulberry Papers provide a multitude of unique and distinctive qualities for any project.

title: The Most Expensive Paper in the World (Lasts 1,000 Years) 📜#paper #makingpaper#handmadepaper
description:
You are looking at the most expensive paper in the world. It’s called Liang’s Paper, and it lasts for a thousand years without fading. But making it is a nightmare.
​In this video, we reveal the grueling process:
​The Material: Harvesting young bamboo during a specific week of the year.
​The Rot: Soaking the bamboo in a mountain stream for 3 months to strip the green skin.
​The Chemistry: Using limestone powder as a chemical bleach to turn the fiber white.
​The Secret Ingredient: Mixing the pulp with a natural glue made from Kiwi fruit roots so the fibers float separately in the water.
​The Skill: Dipping the screen with perfect level precision—if the hand shakes, the sheet is ruined.
​The Drying: Plastering the wet sheets onto a heated wall and brushing them with horsehair.
​This paper is the sound of money and is used to restore ancient scrolls in the Forbidden City.

title: Pounding Paper into Existence! 🧻👊
description:
🌿 From Bark to Pulp: The Heart of Papermaking
Watch the ancient craft of transforming sandalwood bark and rice straw into the soul of Xuan paper! After soaking and steaming, the fibers are rhythmically pounded until they become a soft, cotton-like pulp.

👐 Why Hand-Pounding Makes the Difference
This isn't just hitting—it's a controlled, artisanal process. Hand-pounding gently separates fibers without cutting them (unlike machines), resulting in longer, stronger fibers that give Xuan paper its legendary durability, texture, and ink-absorbing quality.

🎨 What Makes Xuan Paper So Special
This traditional method, recognized as Intangible Cultural Heritage, produces paper that lasts over a thousand years. It's the preferred choice for classical Chinese painting and calligraphy because of its unique strength and responsiveness.

💡 What You Can Learn & Appreciate
Even if you never make paper, you'll gain a deep respect for slow craftsmanship and how patience creates lasting beauty. Perfect for artists, history lovers, or anyone who values things made with care.

💬 Could You Keep the Rhythm?
Does this process remind you of other traditional crafts? Or have you ever tried making something completely from scratch? Share your thoughts below!

title: 😱కాగితాన్ని ఇలా కూడా చేస్తారా?Amazing Chinese Paper Making! #art #trending #shots #reels #viralvideo
description:
Amazing Chinese Paper Making! 

Traditional Chinese papermaking, as seen in this video, is a meticulous craft used to create Yunlong paper, which is also known as half-cooked Xuan paper. The process begins by creating a pulp slurry from a blend of pure mulberry bark, cherry powder, and clear mountain water. A craftsman then uses a large, suspended frame to dip into the vat, skillfully lifting an even layer of pulp to form a sheet. This wet sheet is subsequently pressed to remove water, applied to a large surface to dry, and finally peeled off as a single, finished piece of handmade paper. The resulting product is valued for being both soft and strong, making it an ideal medium for calligraphy and Chinese painting as it effectively carries both ink and artistic "spirit."

స్వచ్ఛమైన మల్బరీ బెరడు, చెర్రీ పొడి, మరియు స్వచ్ఛమైన కొండ నీటిని మిళితం చేసి, వీరు 'యున్‌లాంగ్ పేపర్' (దీనిని 'హాఫ్-కుక్డ్ జువాన్ పేపర్' అని కూడా పిలుస్తారు) ను సృష్టిస్తారు.
ఆ కళాకారుడు ఫ్రేమ్‌ను గుజ్జులో ముంచి, నీటి నుండి పట్టు వస్త్రాన్ని తీస్తున్నట్లుగా దానిని సున్నితంగా పైకి లేపడం చూడండి. తరువాత, దానిని ఒత్తి, ఆరబెట్టి, చేతితో తయారు చేసిన ఒక్కో కాగితాన్ని జాగ్రత్తగా వేరు చేస్తాడు.
మృదువుగా ఉంటూనే బలంగా ఉండే ఈ కాగితం, సిరాను మరియు భావాన్ని (spirit) అద్భుతంగా నిలుపుకుంటుంది. అందుకే ఇది కాలిగ్రఫీ మరియు చైనీస్ పెయింటింగ్‌లకు అంత ఖచ్చితంగా సరిపోతుంది!

The guide will take you around the herb garden. Walk through the market gardens where local farmers work on their fields using traditional methods from 300 years ago. Take a rest with a special welcome drink.

Return to the hotel.

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Traditional Paper Making in China
description:
Han Hui Tang Xuan Paper is a Chinese workshop dedicated to keeping the centuries-old tradition of handmade Xuan paper alive.

Pulp made from tree bark and rice straw is soaked and beaten into a smooth slurry. A bamboo screen is dipped into the vat and lifted with care, forming a delicate, even sheet. This layer is then placed on a heated surface to dry slowly, creating soft, breathable paper.

Celebrated for its strength, absorbency, and archival quality, Xuan paper has long been a favorite of calligraphers and ink painters, with some creations surviving for more than a millennium.

Artist: LYF19316183920 via Douyin

title: How to Make Rice Paper Shikishi Board Yourself  for Watercolor Painting
description:
Using Silicone dry-mounting paper you can create a rice paper board for watercolor painting very easily. 

Materials: Xuan rice paper, Silicone release paper and bristol paper, iron and denim cloth. 
Steps: 
1. Cut the Silicone Release Paper to the same size as the backing board or a little bit smaller (about 1/4" - 1/8") than the board. (No need to measure!) 

1. Place the silicone release paper filme side facing down, or mounting board, foam board etc.

2. Iron it at 180 degree fahrenheit and let it cool a little bit. 

4. Peel the release paper off and save it for use in step 6.

5. If necessary, trim the extra adhesive film along the board edge with a glue gun, knife or scissors.

6. Lay the rice apper smooth side up on the adhesive film and cover it with the release paper from step 4.

7. Now it's ready to iron it on. 

*Silicone release paper is available at http://www.BlueHeronArts.com

title: 3858: The art of tradition: Capturing Anhui’s fading craftsmanship
description:
This is the touching moment that showcased the intricate processes of traditional crafts including cotton carding, Xuan paper making, blacksmithing, and the preparation of stir-fried rice, a local delicacy of Wannan.

On February 3, 2024, the video captured the meticulous methods of traditional artisans in Anhui, detailing the transformation of raw cotton into fine threads, the careful crafting of Xuan paper known for its historical significance, the intense heat and strength required in iron forging, and the culinary skill involved in making stir-fried rice.

The video showcased skilled artisans at work, with close-up shots that highlighted the detailed techniques involved in these ancient crafts.

The video concluded by providing a window into the enduring skills passed down through generations.

title: How China Turns Flowers and Gold into Paper!
description:
Chinese luxurious calligraphy paper, often known as Xuan paper, is one of the most treasured materials in traditional Chinese art. 🌿 Handmade using bark, rice straw, and natural plant fibers, it is carefully crafted to hold ink smoothly, giving every brushstroke depth and elegance.

You can find this exquisite paper mainly in Anhui Province, especially in the region of Xuancheng, which has been the heart of Xuan paper making for over a thousand years. 🏯 Today, it’s still used by artists and calligraphers worldwide who value its beauty and durability.

💬 Would you love to try writing on this luxurious paper?

title: Making a traditional Chinese hanging scroll
description:
Senior Conservator Mrs Jin Xian Qiu has spent the last 43 years mastering the traditional Chinese method of mounting a hanging scroll. Watch as she prepares and hangs two works by Chinese artist Qu Leilei – a process that takes a whole year.

The works – 'Journey' and 'Lei Feng' – are both created using ink on traditional xuan paper and have been donated to the British Museum by the artist. 

Qu Leilei (b. 1951) is a calligrapher, painter and author. He was one of the founding members of the Stars Group alongside his contemporaries Ma Desheng, Wang Keping, Huang Rui, Li Shuang, Zhong Acheng and Ai Weiwei.

The works will be on display in Room 91a at the British Museum from November to December 2015.

Qu Leilei will be discussing contemporary Chinese art with Shen Kuiyi and Ma Desheng at the British Museum on Friday 13 November 2015 at 18.30.
Information and booking: http://www.britishmuseum.org/whats_on/events_calendar/event_detail.aspx?eventId=2635

title: Does this Vietnamese fish sauce get the Vietnamese mom stamp of approval?? part 1 of 2
description:
CHRISTY COOK’S FISH SAUCE

I got this recipe from Christy Cooks on IG and TT

1.5 cups sugar
1.5 cups boiling hot water
15 thai chilis
2 TBSP garlic
1/2 cup lime juice
3/4 cup fish sauce

1. Make a simple syrup by dissolving 1.5 cups sugar with 1.5 cups boiling hot water. Set this aside to cool

2. In a food processor, blend together: 15 thai chilis, garlic, and a 1-2 TBSP of your simple syrup. Now add this mixture with your simple syrup along with lime juice and fish sauce. Taste test and adjust to preference.

title: Making Very Large Sheets of Paper in China
description: In 2007, Elaine and Sidney Koretsky traveled to Anhui Province, China, and quite serendipitously stumbled upon a workshop making very large sheets of paper, measuring nearly 10 feet by 23 feet. The feat of making such a large sheet of paper is vividly depicted in the film and is enhanced by a sequence of slow motion videography. The fibers, cooking, beating, sheet formation, pressing, and drying are all meticulously described. The large sheet observed was xuanzhi, the finest paper made in China for hundreds of years and used for fine calligraphy and ink brush painting, displayed in large public spaces.

Garnishing: Coriander fresh-30gms
White sesame- 1tsp 
Salt & pepper- to taste(if required)

Method :

Cut the clean vegetables to julienne. Pour some hot water into a wide mouth vessel to soak the rice papers, Soak the rice paper till it becomes semi-translucent or white. Using a clean muslin cloth drain out the extra water from the rice paper. Spread the lettuce leaves on the rice paper and arrange the julienne vegetables on the rice paper, roll it and cut it into 2 halves to serve it. Prepare the soy souse for dipping by adding grated ginger. Before serving the vegetable rice paper roll sprinkle some white sesame seeds to enhance the flavors garnish it with some coriander leaves. Vegetable rice paper with soy sauce dip ready to eat.

Enjoy the fresh veggies with salty, tangy, sweet sauce...

July '09

Our one-day Mekong River tour included a lunch stop where we had some of the best damned fried fish we'd ever eaten in our lives. And it was served in an interesting way: upright in swimming position on a special stand, rather than flat on a plate like the Chinese do it. Also, it was fried with many of the scales intact, which we had to scrape off before eating (I tried chewing one, it wasn't very tasty). I don't know how, but it seemed to add to the flavour while keeping the flesh tender and juicy. And putting it in the wrap made it even better still!

project_name_en: Traditional handicrafts of making Xuan paper
title: Making hanji: Korean papermaking by Shin Hyun Se
description:
Hanji, Korean paper, has a long but often overlooked tradition. This high-quality paper is made from the inner bark of the dak (mulberry) tree, in the countryside where the water supply is clean and abundant. There are many, many stages of papermaking. This video was shot on August 6, 2008 on a site visit to a traditional papermaker who supplies FIDES International with conservation-grade hanji. He has been making paper since he was a teenager, and his is the only surviving mill in a rural village where papermaking used to be plentiful.

Thanks to Bo Kyung Kim of FIDES International and the US Fulbright Program

description:
•The Mottled Flora Texture of Botanical Monoprinting: It replicates the authentic texture of botanical dye-making on Xuan paper or monoprinting actual vines and leaves. One side features a deep, calm, and elegant "Cypress Green", while the other is a soft, pristine "Dusk Grey".  Closer inspection reveals the gentle warmth of silk and paper; from afar, it possesses the aura of a classical Eastern landscape.

•The "Scholarly Temperament" of Low-Saturation Tones:When natural daylight or a warm ambient LED strip washes over the botanical shadows, the motifs alternate beautifully between light and darkness, void and substance—as if weightlessly capturing the quiet wind and shifting seasons from the wild mountain onto your cabinets and walls.

•Seamless Continuity for Advanced Door-Wall-Cabinet Architecture: The millimeter-level joints are virtually invisible, dissolving the industrial boundaries of cabinetry into a naturally draped, magnificent piece of architectural art.
