title: Mongolian art of singing: Khoomei
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00210
Description: The Mongolian art of singing: Khoomei, or Hooliin Chor (throat harmony), is a style of singing in which a single performer produces a diversified harmony of multiple voice parts, including a continued bass element produced in the throat. These singers may perform alone or in groups. Khoomei is practised today among Mongolian communities in several countries, especially in Inner Mongolia in northern China, western Mongolia and the Tuva Republic of Russia. Traditionally performed on the occasion of ritual ceremonies, songs express respect and praise for the natural world, for the ancestors of the Mongolian people and for great heroes. The form is reserved for special events and group activities such as horse races, archery and wrestling tournaments, large banquets and sacrificial rituals. The timing and order of songs is often strictly regulated. Khoomei has long been regarded as a central element representing Mongolian culture and remains a strong symbol of national or ethnic identity. As a window into the philosophy and aesthetic values of the Mongol people, it has served as a kind of cultural emissary promoting understanding and friendship among China, Mongolia and Russia, and has attracted attention around the world as a unique form of musical expression.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 IMARI

title: Mongolian Throat Singing | Khöömei | UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity
description:
Khoomei is a traditional Mongolian art of throat singing practiced by people in Mongolia, Inner Mongolia, Tuva and Siberia. The performer imitates sounds of nature, simultaneously emitting two distinct vocal sounds. Khoomei literally means pharynx, and is believed to have been learned from birds. It is inscribed in 2009 on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity of UNESCO, under the name Mongolian art of singing, Khoomei.

ICCR - Indian Council for Cultural Relations in collaboration with Heritage Bengal presented this awesome Mongolian throat singing which is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

title: The Mongolian traditional art of Khöömei
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00396
Description: Khöömei is a form of singing originating in western Mongolia, in the Altai mountains. The performer imitates sounds of nature, simultaneously emitting two distinct vocal sounds: along with a continuous drone, the singer produces a melody of harmonics. Khöömei literally means pharynx, and it is believed to have been learned from birds, whose spirits are central to shamanic practices. The multitude of Khöömei techniques in Mongolia are grouped within two main styles: the ''kharkhiraa'' (deep Khöömei) and ''isgeree'' Khöömei (whistled Khöömei). In ''kharkhiraa'' the singer sings a drone in a normal voice, while emphasizing the undertone or subharmonic one octave below. In ''isgeree'' Khöömei, it is the overtones above the fundamental note of the drone that are emphasized, creating a higher-pitched whistle. In both cases, the drone is produced with very taut vocal cords, and the melody is created by modulating the size and shape of the mouth cavity, opening and closing the lips and moving the tongue. Khöömei is performed by Mongolian nomads in a variety of social occasions, from grand state ceremonies to festive household events. Khöömei is also sung during herding, and inside the yurt to lull babies to sleep. Traditionally, Khöömei is transmitted orally from bearer to learner, or via master-to-apprentice. 
Country(ies): Mongolia
© 2009 by Mongolian National Commission for UNESCO

title: Cântico gutural Tuvan - khoomei ( Huun-Huur-Tu - Live )
description:
Khoomei:
língua Tuva : Хөөмей ; mongol : , Baopei transliteração : kögemei , cirílico : Хөөмий

A arte da Mongólia, cantando: Khoomei, ou Hooliin Chor ("harmonia da garganta"), é um estilo de cantar no qual um único cantor produz uma harmonia diversificada de vários tons de voz, incluindo um elemento de baixo contínuo produzido na garganta. 
Esses cantores podem realizar sozinhos ou em grupos. Khoomei é praticada hoje entre as comunidades da Mongólia em vários países, especialmente no interior da Mongólia, no norte da China, Mongólia ocidental e na República de Tuva da Rússia.

Tradicionalmente realizada por ocasião de cerimoniais, rituais, canções onde se expressa o respeito e louvor para o mundo natural, para os antepassados do povo mongol e para grandes heróis. O formulário é reservado para eventos especiais e atividades em grupo, como corridas de cavalos, tiro com arco e luta livre em torneios, grandes banquetes e rituais de sacrifício. O calendário e a ordem das músicas é muitas vezes estritamente regulamentado. “Khoomei” tem sido considerado como um elemento central que representa a cultura mongol e continua a ser um forte símbolo de identidade nacional ou étnica. Como uma janela para a filosofia e os valores estéticos das comunidades mongóis, tem servido como uma espécie de emissário cultural em promover a compreensão e amizade entre China, Mongólia e Rússia, e tem atraído a atenção de todo o mundo como uma forma única de expressão musical.

Há muito tempo, a Mongólia incluiu o Khoomei como tesouro nacional, e Tuva colocou o Khoomei visto como a alma nacional de seu povo. E na China, juntamente com a ecologia original das canções folclóricas desde as escavações, também foi visto como uma prática folclórica chinesa.Atualmente, a República do Governo Popular da China tem um projeto, onde a UNESCO, declara e inclui o Khoomei como "obras-primas do Patrimônio Oral e Imaterial da Humanidade".

Em 2009, entrou oficialmente para o Patrimônio Cultural Imaterial Mundial destacando o Teatro chamado “Inner Mongolia Nacional de Canto e Dança”.

Critérios para inscrição na Lista Representativa

Em arquivos de nomeação, a (s) parte que apresente Estado (s) é (são) solicitado a demonstrar que um elemento proposto para inscrição na Lista Representativa do Patrimônio Cultural Imaterial da Humanidade satisfaz todos os critérios a seguir:

R.1 O elemento constitui patrimônio cultural imaterial, tal como definido no artigo 2 da Convenção.

R.2 Inscrição do elemento contribuirá para assegurar a visibilidade e conscientização da importância do patrimônio cultural intangível e de encorajar o diálogo, o que reflete a diversidade cultural no mundo inteiro e testemunhando a criatividade humana.

R.3 medidas de salvaguarda são elaborados que podem proteger e promover o elemento.

R.4 O elemento foi nomeado após a mais ampla participação possível da comunidade, grupo ou, se for o caso, os indivíduos em causa e com seu consentimento livre, prévio e informado.

R.5 O elemento é incluído em um inventário do patrimônio cultural imaterial existente no território (s) da apresentação de Estado (s) Parte (s), conforme definido no artigo 11 e artigo 12 da Convenção

title: Khoomei Frequencies | Mongolian Throat Singing for Altered Consciousness
description:
Listen to the eternal voice of the Mongolian steppes. For over 2000 years, nomadic throat singers have channeled the sounds of their land through the ancient art of khoomei. Experience authentic Mongolian throat singing for deep focus, meditation, and spiritual practice.

🎵 WHAT IS THROAT SINGING?

Khoomei (overtone singing) is a vocal technique where singers produce multiple pitches simultaneously – a deep kargyraa bass drone with whistle-like sygyt overtones. This creates a meditative atmosphere used in shamanic rituals across Central Asia for millennia.

🎼 TRACKLIST:

0:00:00 Steppe Awakening – Dawn meditation, grassland atmosphere

🏔️ Journey through authentic Mongolian landscapes: endless steppes at golden hour, sacred Altai mountain peaks with prayer flags, traditional ger yurts with warm firelight, Gobi desert under infinite stars, and ancient shamanic ceremonies.

🎯 PERFECT FOR:
Deep focus work • Meditation practice • Yoga sessions • Study and concentration • Sleep and relaxation • Stress relief • Spiritual practice • Background ambience for creative work

📌 PLAYLISTS:

- Focus Music 2026 – Deep Work & Study: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLABA3TnZIVer3nfCaT_p8dUqUYHxgosS3

🎧 LISTENING TIPS:
Use quality headphones to hear the full harmonic overtone series. The interplay between bass drones and high overtones creates a three-dimensional sound field. Listen at moderate volume – throat singing is meant to be felt as much as heard.

🌍 CULTURAL CONTEXT:
Mongolia's nomadic people have preserved one of the world's oldest continuous cultural traditions. Throat singing (höömii in Mongolian, khoomei in Tuvan) developed as shamans learned to mimic the sounds of their environment – wolves howling, wind whistling through mountain passes, and the deep rumble of distant thunder. The morin khuur horsehead fiddle you hear throughout is Mongolia's national instrument.

title: How to Throat Sing With 3 Easy Steps (Kargyraa Style)
description:
Link to our Patreon:
https://www.patreon.com/Hachapuri69

If you have questions, you can check out this questions/answers video here:
https://youtu.be/WPlCMSGIQkc

Social Media platforms:

Official Website: https://thefrogcult.com/
 
Hello! 

Recently on Reddit, I posted a poll about which video I should make: https://www.reddit.com/r/Hordecore/
Taking the votes into account, also interests of my friends who wanted to learn Mongolian throat singing, I present you "how to throat sing in 3 easy steps!" =)

It might take some dedication to get it better, it could be a bit challenging for girls but personally, I've seen a live show of a Tuvan woman, musician, and artist Sainkho, who can do a few forms of this culture. Another person was a female student I met a few years ago. She used to learn how to play morin khuur with a few boys when she was young, the boys were learning khoomei. They teased her that she can't sing it because she is a girl, so she got irritated and practiced morin khuur with khoomei at home. After some time, it seems she was able to sing as well as any other person. There is another woman who can sing it as well, she plays in Tuvan folk-rock band Yat-Kha :)

In any case, let me know in the comments below if you have questions or ideas. Oh, one detail I forgot to mention in the video is that this type of singing is specifically called "kargyraa". The other one is called "isgeree"(or sygyt in Tuvan) is quite difficult to learn, I still haven't mastered it fully. The intro music was done originally by ElectroKaplosion - coffin dance music with Mongolian theme, a super cool dude, check out his channel :D - https://www.youtube.com/user/ElectroKaplosion

If you liked the content and if you wish to support this channel for further video, please do give this man a helping hand :D

title: The Dual Mastery: Master Otgonhuu performs the intricate "Sygyt-Borbangnadyr" Khoomei
description:
Witness the fusion of two fundamental techniques into one breathtaking sound.

This is "Sygyt-Borbangnadyr," a complex and advanced style of overtone singing performed by Master Otgonhuu. It masterfully blends the crystal-clear, high-pitched melody of **Sygyt (Whistle Khoomei)** with the rhythmic, rolling resonance of **Borbangnadyr (Reverberating Khoomei)**. It is a true testament to the depth and versatility achievable in this ancient art form.

// ABOUT THE ARTIST //
Otgonhuu is a Khoomei artist and culture bearer from the Altai Mountain region. He grew up surrounded by these ancient sounds and has dedicated his life to preserving their purest form. To him, Khoomei is the root of his culture and a bridge that connects nature with the soul.

// LEARN KHOOMEI WITH THE MASTER //
Are you captivated by this incredible sound? You can learn to create it, too.
The techniques showcased in this performance are all part of a clear, systematic learning path. Otgonhuu has distilled his 40 years of personal practice and over 20 years of teaching experience into a unique online course, designed to help enthusiasts from all over the world awaken the ancient voice sleeping within them.

title: TUVA Khoomei - Tuvan Throat Singing
description:
http://www.discoverysound.com/en/DFSD670/
KHOOMEI - A present from the magnificent ground TUVA

From the Asian republic of TUVA, the amazing style of overtone singing has finally been properly captured as a mystical and marvelous sample library, along with the traditional Tuvan stringed instruments ‹Igil› and ‹Doshpuluur›.

In the Republic of TUVA, the mountain basin is populated with lakes and rivers. The inspiring landscape is home to the powerful and beautiful voices of ‹Khoomei›.

A long-awaited ‹Khoomei› samples have finally arrived, encompassing 154 files in ACID|WAV and REX2 formats.

This collection features samples of ‹Khoomei› , male and female throat singers, and two types of stringed instruments: ‹Doshpuluur› and ‹Igil›. All sound files are presented in both 44.1 kHz/16-bit and 96 kHz/24-bit formats.


Tuvan Sounds on the disc:

KHOOMEI :
A type of throat singing with a distinct fundamental and an overtone melody, used in folk music from Tuva.

DOSHPULUUR :
A three-stringed instrument with fretless similar to a banjo, but with the warm, organic sound of wood and goat skin.

IGIL :
A two-stringed, bowed and fretless instrument. Deeply connected with the mythology of the horse, the most important animal in Tuvan culture. The Igil sounds like a cross between violin and cello, and is often used to imitate the sound and rhythms of the horse.


The Republic of TUVA:

It is a member of the Russian Federation, located in the Tannu Mountains on the Siberian border in northwestern Mongolia. The capital is Kyzyl. The population of 308,000 lives in the area of 170,500 square kilometers. The area is a mountain basin with numerous lakes and rivers. Many of Tuvans are still cattle-breeding nomads.

Sound Files
44.1kHz/16-bit | 96kHz/24-bit formats
ACID WAV [115 files]

Doshpuluur:
Loops [32 files]
Oneshot Phrases [3 files]

title: Khoomei + Ezengileer Throat Singing Explained
description:
This real-time MRI video features Khoomei + Ezengileer throat singing performed by Giovanni Bortoluzzi.
Khoomei sustains a steady low fundamental note, while Ezengileer uses rapid tongue and vocal-tract movements to isolate higher harmonics.
The MRI and spectrogram together reveal how precise shaping of the tongue, lips, and throat allows a single voice to produce two distinct notes simultaneously.

title: Why Can a Khoomei Master Sing Without Moving a Candle Flame? The Science of Breath Control Explained
description:
Have you seen the candle test in vocal training?

An experienced singer produces sound — 
the flame barely moves.
An untrained singer opens their mouth — 
the flame goes out.

This is not magic. It's breath control.

And the science behind it is exactly what 
Master Otgonhuu teaches in Khoomei:

→ Breath support stays LOW — not rushing into the throat
→ Vocal folds close steadily — no air leaking out
→ Higher pitch = breath sinks DEEPER, not upward

True Khoomei is not blowing air outward.
It is forming a resonance space inside the body,
where sound grows from deep within the chest.

If you practice Khoomei correctly,
not blowing out a candle is the natural result.

title: Khoomei: Mongolian Throat Singing – Overtone Magic of the Steppe
description:
🎶 Welcome to Classical Crescendo! Today, we explore khoomei—Mongolian throat singing, a vocal art that allows one singer to produce multiple notes at once. With its hypnotic overtones, khoomei echoes the wind, rivers, and vast landscapes of the steppe, bridging the human voice with the natural world.

In this episode, you’ll discover:
✨ The ancient origins of khoomei among Mongolian and Tuvan nomads, imitating wind, water, birds, and animals
🎼 The main styles of throat singing: khoomei (airy overtones), sygyt (whistle-like harmonics), kargyraa (deep growling bass), and related Tuva techniques like ezengileer and borbangnadyr
📖 Khoomei’s role in shamanic ritual, Buddhist meditation, herding life, and legends of learning from wind and streams
🌍 Its global journey through groups like Huun-Huur-Tu, Alash Ensemble, Egschiglen, and The Hu, and its presence in film soundtracks and world fusion projects
🏅 UNESCO’s recognition of throat singing as Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2010, ensuring its preservation and revival for future generations

Khoomei is more than music—it is a living spiritual tradition, a vocal art form that unites humanity with nature and carries the timeless spirit of the Mongolian steppe.

title: Why You CAN'T Find Chest Resonance (It's Not Your Throat) | Khoomei Breathing Masterclass
description:
Struggling to find your chest resonance? Throat getting tight and painful 
every time you try to sing?

It's not your talent. It's not your anatomy.
It's your order of operations.

─────────────────────────────────
▶ THE TRUTH NO ONE TELLS YOU
─────────────────────────────────

Until your breath truly sinks into your core, the door to chest resonance 
stays locked — no matter how hard you try.

Here's what's fascinating: every elite vocal tradition on earth figured 
this out thousands of years ago.

• Bel Canto (Western Classical) → calls it Appoggio
  Expand the lower ribs like an opening umbrella. Hold that expansion 
  as you sing.

• Indian Classical (Raga) → calls it Pranayama
  Abdominal control + steady air output. Notes that never tremble.

• Modern Pop & Rock → calls it "core support"
  Every high note, every long phrase — same principle underneath.

Different names. Same universal truth:
Low inhalation → Expanded support → Steady exhalation → Relaxed throat

This is the exact foundation Master Otgonhuu teaches in authentic 
Mongolian Khoomei.

─────────────────────────────────
▶ THE TEETER-TOTTER EFFECT
─────────────────────────────────

Shallow breath → body lacks air pressure → brain locks the throat to 
compensate → throat tension cuts off the path to your chest.

Deep, sinking breath → core takes all the pressure → throat is completely 
liberated → it stops being a "valve" and becomes a wide-open "pipeline" 
→ sound drops naturally into chest resonance.

─────────────────────────────────
▶ TWO EXERCISES TO TRY RIGHT NOW
─────────────────────────────────

EXERCISE 1: The Sibilant Sustain (Test Your Foundation)
→ Place hands on your lower ribs
→ Inhale deeply — feel your ribs push your hands outward
→ Hiss a steady "Ssss" — keep ribs expanded the entire time
→ Goal: hold steadily for 30 seconds
   If you can, your foundation is ready.

EXERCISE 2: The Deep Sigh (Unlock the Chest)
→ Take a deep breath
→ Imagine you're exhausted after a long day
→ Let out a deep, lazy sigh: "Haaaa—"
→ Don't push from the throat — feel the air vibrate against your breastbone
→ That vibration is the seed of chest resonance.

✅ You feel vibration in your chest = correct
❌ Your throat gets tight or tired = breath hasn't sunk deep enough yet

─────────────────────────────────
▶ READY TO GO DEEPER?
─────────────────────────────────

Join Master Otgonhuu's Authentic Mongolian Khoomei Masterclass — a 
step-by-step system built on the same universal breathing foundation, 
taking you from first breath to full overtone resonance.

👉 Enroll here: [otgonhuu.art]

I'm Tianbing — drop your progress in the comments below.
See you in the course.

title: Echoes of nature: Kazakh khoomei lives on
description:
The captivating sound of khoomei, or throat singing, is a vocal art form rooted in the nomadic lifestyle where people feel at one with nature. Singers imitate sounds from the natural world and can produce two or more pitches simultaneously, creating a unique and mesmerizing auditory experience.

In Kazakh culture, khoomei is more than just a musical technique – it symbolizes respect for nature and the pursuit of spiritual coexistence with nature. Kazakh khoomei inheritor Dumanbek Butikhan and his band have been reinterpreting ancient Kazakh lyrics and blending them with traditional instruments. Their enchanting performances are breathing new life into this rich intangible cultural heritage. #Xinjiang #SCO2025 #ChinaTravel

Diese einzigartige Kunstform ermöglicht es Sängern, mehrere Töne gleichzeitig zu erzeugen, indem sie ihre Stimmbänder und den Rachen auf spezielle Weise nutzen. Besonders beeindruckend ist der Bass-Khoomei, der durch tiefe, resonante Töne besticht. Es gibt auch Varianten, bei denen der Gesang das Blöken von Schafen oder das Heulen des Windes imitiert – eine Hommage an die Natur und ihre Klänge. Findet ihr, dass die Imitationen gelungen sind? title: The 3 Types of Mongolian Khoomei (Kharkhiraa, Isgeree, Xiaha) & The Secret of Body Resonance
description:
In this rare archival footage, Master Otgonhuu breaks down the three fundamental pillars of Mongolian Throat Singing (Khoomei):

Kharkhiraa (Deep/Undertone)

Isgeree (Whistle/Overtone, often called Sygyt)

Xiaha (The Base Tone)

He points out a critical issue in modern practice: many singers today rely solely on the throat ("Single Line"), losing the rich, multi-layered "Double Line" resonance that defines the authentic ancient style.

Master Otgonhuu believed that Khoomei is not just a vocal trick, but a full-body connection.

👇 Learn the authentic "Double Line" method from Master Otgonhuu: Start your journey here: https://otgonhuu.art/

title: Katajjaq meets Khoomei, Orchid Ensemble & Oktoecho CAPACOA 2025 Snapshot 5min showcase
description:
Katajjaq meets Khoomei (Inuit & Mongolian throat singing), featuring Oktoecho (Montreal) & Orchid Ensemble (Vancouver) 

This project brings together two ensembles from across Canada to present a unique blend of two throat singing styles: katajjaq (Inuit) and khoomei (Mongolian), as well as a hybrid that crosses between Inuit, Mongolian, Chinese, Japanese, European, and Arabic music.

Katajjaq and khoomei are ancestral throat singing techniques that reproduce sounds heard in nature –  water flowing, the wind blowing, echoes in mountains, the calls of birds… Coincidentally, the nature is also a major theme in Chinese music. The Juno nominated Orchid Ensemble (Vancouver) is made up of composer/artistic director Lan Tung on vocals & erhu/Chinese violin, Dailin Hsieh on the zheng/Chinese zither, and Jonathan Bernard on percussion, with special guest Anand Avirmed on khoomei and morin khuur (Mongolian horsehead fiddle).  https://www.orchidensemble.com/katajjaq-meets-khoomei/

Oktoecho (Montreal) is a collective of musicians from diverse cultures. Oktoecho musicians in this project include composer/co-artistic director Katia Makdissi-Warren, Inuit katajjaq performers Lydia Etok (co-artistic director) and Caroline Novalinga, multi-instrumentalist Michel Dubeau (flutes, shakuhachi, duduk, Irish whistle, clarinet, bagpipe), percussionist Bertil Schulrabe, Turkish kanun player Didem Basar, and Moroccan oud player Khalil Moqadem. (Oktoecho may have 4 to 7 players to join in the performance) https://www.oktoecho.com/katajjaq-khoomei

Traditionally, Inuit throat singing was exclusively performed by women, while Khoomei was performed by men. Katajjaq is a game practiced by two women facing each other. The game ends when one of them is out of breath or laughs. Khoomei was primarily used in shamanic ceremonies, and today also used at festive events. They are both fascinating vocal skills of long traditions.

Katajjaq meets Khoomei has been co-created by Katia Makdissi-Warren (Oktoecho) & Lan Tung (Orchid Ensemble).

title: A Crystal Flute Made of Breath: The Tuvan "Sygyt" Whistle Khoomei | Performed by Otgonhuu
description:
Close your eyes and listen. This is the sound of a high mountain stream, a crystal flute made not of wood or metal, but of pure human breath.

This is "Sygyt," the iconic whistle Khoomei of the Tuva region, performed by Master Otgonhuu. It is a breathtaking display of overtone singing, where a clear, piercing melody floats ethereally above a steady root note. It is the art of separating a single voice into two, creating a sound that is both ancient and otherworldly.

// ABOUT THE ARTIST //
Otgonhuu comes from the Uvs province of Mongolia, a land bordering the Tuva Republic. He grew up listening to Khoomei, and from the age of ten, began to learn this art form that was his birthright. To him, Khoomei is the root of his culture and a bridge that connects nature with the soul.

// LEARN KHOOMEI WITH THE MASTER //
Are you captivated by this incredible sound? You can learn to create it, too.
The techniques showcased in this performance are all part of a clear, systematic learning path. Otgonhuu has distilled his 40 years of personal practice and over 20 years of teaching experience into a unique online course, designed to help enthusiasts from all over the world awaken the ancient voice sleeping within them.

➡️ Start Learning the Full Method Here: https://otgonhuu.art/ (Get the best price & complete curriculum on our official site)

Subscribe to our channel for more stunning Khoomei performances, glimpses into the artist's life, and exclusive practice tips.

Thank you for listening.

title: Khoomei  The "Forgotten Sound" of Humanity | Master Otgonhuu
description:
What is the true nature of Khoomei (Throat Singing)?

In this profound lesson, Master Otgonhuu explains why Khoomei is more than just a vocal technique—it is a reawakening of humanity's "Forgotten Sound."

👇 Start Your Full Training Journey Here: https://www.otgonhuu.art

💡 Key Insights from this video:

The "Outer Voice": Since learning to speak, humans have relied on the throat and vocal cords, neglecting our internal resonance.

The "Inner Voice": Khoomei taps into the same internal organs that mammals use to produce sound—a primal skill we have forgotten.

Ancestral Memory: This is why hearing Khoomei often feels so grounding and familiar; it connects us to our ancient past.

Master Otgonhuu is the authorized bearer of this intangible cultural heritage. This channel is dedicated to passing down the authentic tradition of Mongolian Throat Singing.

title: Ethnomusicologist Mark van Tongeren Gives Lesson in Khöömei [Live at Folklife Festival 2002]
description:
For those who think the human voice can produce only one note at a time, the resonant harmonies of throat-singing are surprising. In throat-singing, a singer can produce two or more notes simultaneously through a specialized vocalization technique taking advantage of the throat's resonance characteristics. Singers use a form of circular breathing which allows them to sustain multiple notes for long periods of time. Young Tuvan singers are trained from childhood through a sort of apprentice system to use the folds of the throat as reverberation chambers. Mark van Tongeren, an ethnomusicologist specializing in Khöömei (the Turco-Mongolian art of throat-singing), teaches the technique during the 2002 Smithsonian Folklife Festival.

For more throat-singing, listen to the 1990 Smithsonian Folkways album 'Tuva: Voices from the Center of Asia,' available on CD and digital.

Stream/download/purchase:
Smithsonian Folkways: https://folkways.si.edu/tuva-voices-from-the-center-of-asia/world/music/album/smithsonian
Spotify: https://open.spotify.com/album/5x57y0xlgA965zIeuAXhv3?si=kNva1YizQFiG3mFPvLdVYw

Recorded in 1987, this highly-acclaimed release makes available to the world a miraculous and remarkable ancient musical tradition, including examples of the rare multiphonic "throat-singing" technique. Sponsored by the National Geographic Society, the Smithsonian Institution's Office of Folklife Programs, and the U.S.S.R. Union of Composers, it is an astonishing achievement. "Primal and forthright...a beguiling earful." — The New York Times

title: The Rhythm of the Ride: Master Otgonhuu performs "Ezenggileer" (Stirrup Khoomei)
description:
Close your eyes and let the sound transport you to the vast plains of Mongolia, where horse and rider move as one.

This is "Ezenggileer" Khoomei (often translated as "Stirrup Khoomei"), a fascinating and rhythmic style of overtone singing performed by Master Otgonhuu. This technique uniquely mimics the sounds of galloping horses, the creaking of saddles, and the rhythmic jingle of stirrups—a direct reflection of the nomadic life and deep connection between the Mongolian people and their horses.

// ABOUT THE ARTIST //
Otgonhuu is a Khoomei artist and culture bearer from the Altai Mountain region. He grew up surrounded by these ancient sounds and has dedicated his life to preserving their purest form. To him, Khoomei is the root of his culture and a bridge that connects nature with the soul.

// LEARN KHOOMEI WITH THE MASTER //
Are you captivated by this incredible sound? You can learn to create it, too.
The techniques showcased in this performance are all part of a clear, systematic learning path. Otgonhuu has distilled his 40 years of personal practice and over 20 years of teaching experience into a unique online course, designed to help enthusiasts from all over the world awaken the ancient voice sleeping within them.

description:
Delehei:
  a Mongolian musician, is a pioneer of a natural singing and song writing style that focuses on using traditional Mongolian techniques. Over the years, he has committed to attaining Mongolian Throat singing techniques, learn to play lost and forgotten traditional instruments and continuously experiments with native traditional music and its hybrid integration into other music genres. He is able to composite many instruments together to create his own unique style of music and play with loop-station, such as; Morinhuur, Tovtsuur, Amanhuur, Homhuur, Modentsoor, Bumber(Mongolian drum)and digital music. He has also mastered the rare technique of Mongolian Throat singing and Tuva Throat singing, which in itself is its own unique instrument. Delehei has introduced his new style of music to over 10 countries.

title: The True Origin of an Epic Khoomei Masterpiece: Tes Gol | Master Otgonhuu (1998 Original)
description:
Experience the raw, untamed power of the mother river. 🎧 (Headphones highly recommended for the deep overtone resonance)

You may have heard many covers of this legendary throat-singing track, but this is the true origin. In 1998, Master Otgonhuu adapted the traditional Tuvan folk song "Alash River" into the majestic Oirat anthem, "Tes River" (Tes Gol).

Born to an Oirat father and a Tuvan mother, Master Otgonhuu perfectly fused his dual heritage into this magnificent piece. With his unparalleled Khoomei techniques, he captures the heroic spirit of the rushing river and a nomad's deep, spiritual reverence for the motherland.

This is not just traditional music; it is the authentic source of a global Khoomei phenomenon.

title: Mongolian  (﹙ ‍🎧 ﹚)  Throat Singing  - KHOOMEI
description:
Mongolian Khoomei — Throat Singing & Morin Khuur  KHOOMEI — 

THE IMPOSSIBLE SOUND Khoomei (or höömii) is the Mongolian art of producing two or more notes simultaneously with the human voice 
— a bass drone sustained in the chest while a flute-like overtone melody is articulated in the oral cavity. It is perhaps the single most astonishing vocal technique in human musical history: one person creating what sounds like two separate instruments.  
THE PHYSICS OF OVERTONE SINGING When the bass drone is sustained at a stable pitch, the upper vocal tract (pharynx, mouth, lips) can be shaped to selectively amplify specific harmonics of that fundamental frequency. By adjusting tongue position, lip shape, and jaw opening, the singer causes different harmonics to ring out as discrete, audible melodic tones — while the fundamental continues beneath.  
SYGYT STYLE This recording uses the sygyt ("whistling") style of khoomei — the clearest and most melodically developed form, in which the overtone melody sings in a pure, flute-like register against the sustained bass drone.  
THE MORIN KHUUR The morin khuur (horse head fiddle) is the national instrument of Mongolia. Its two strings are made of horse hair, its bow is strung with horse hair, and its scroll is carved in the form of a horse's head. The morin khuur carries the spirit of the horse — and in Mongolian culture, the horse is the vehicle of the soul.  
THE STEPPE The vast open landscape of the Mongolian steppe — where there are no walls, no ceilings, and no horizons — is the natural reverb of this music. Its empty, resonant openness is built into every tone.

title: Chant Diphonique, Khoomei
description: Le chant diphonique est une technique vocale permettant à une personne de produire un timbre vocal caractérisé par deux notes de fréquences différentes. Il s'agit donc de faire du chant polyphonique (à plusieurs voix) au moyen d'un seul organe vocal combinant d'une part divers types de voix (de poitrine, de tête…) et d'autre part divers positionnements de la langue ou des lèvres. La seconde voix, ou harmonique, est dans un rapport exact de fréquences avec celle de la voix de base, bourdon ou encore fondamental. Elle peut être égale à deux fois la fréquence du bourdon

title: 세상에서 가장 신비로운 찬양, 몽골 '흐미(Khoomei)'와 CCM의 만남 / 창조의 울림 (The Echo of Creation)
description:
'흐미(Khoomei)' 장르 소개 
🌍 장르 소개: 흐미(Khoomei) '흐미'는 몽골과 투바 지역에서 전해 내려오는 
유네스코 인류무형문화유산으로, 한 가수가 **낮은 저음(드론)과 
높은 배음(오버톤)**을 동시에 내는 독특한 이중창 창법입니다. 
낮은 저음은 광활한 대지를, 그 위로 흐르는 맑은 고음은 바람과 새소리, 
즉 만물의 영혼을 상징합니다. 사람이 내는 소리 중 가장 자연과 닮아 있어 
'우주의 소리'라고도 불립니다. 이번 CCM에서는 이 신비로운 공명을 통해 
창조주 하나님의 위엄과 태초의 정적을 표현했습니다.

🙏 **"깊은 바다가 깊은 바다를 부르며..." (시편 42:7)**

태초에 하나님이 천지를 창조하실 때, 그 광활한 공간에 울려 퍼졌을 
'첫 소리'는 어떤 느낌이었을까요? 
이번에 선보이는 **[창조의 울림 (The Echo of Creation)]**은 
몽골의 신비로운 창법 '흐미(Khoomei)'를 통해 그 압도적인 
경외감을 담아낸 곡입니다.

낮게 깔리는 저음의 공명은 변치 않는 하나님의 신실하심을, 
그 위를 유영하는 맑은 배음은 만물 속에 깃든 주의 숨결을 상징합니다. 

전문 음악인이 아니더라도 AI라는 도구를 통해 우리 마음속 
깊은 영성을 이토록 이국적이고 웅장한 선율로 담아낼 수 있다는 것이 
참으로 감사한 고백이 됩니다.

🎵 **Credits**
* Lyrics & Concept: [Bless You]

title: Celestia Nomad – Khoomei Above the Server Farm (Mongolian × Tribal Techno)
description:
The khoomei rises with the dual-tone harmonic precision that Tuvan and Mongolian throat singers have been refining across the steppes for at least a thousand years, splitting the fundamental into overtones the way the eagle-bone whistle splits the air, and the tribal heartbeat that locks beneath it belongs to a server farm built atop the abandoned Soviet collective ranch where the singer's grandfather once tended a thousand head of cashmere goat. A morin khuur bows a slow melody that follows the contour of the foothills behind the data centre, and a frame drum drives the rhythm forward while a jaw harp buzzes a counter-line with the clockwork precision of cooling fans cycling in their racks. The throat singing continues after the kick fades, because the steppe was here first and the servers will eventually rust.

#CelestiaNomad #Mongolian #TribalTechno #Khoomei #MorinKhuur #DataSteppe

title: 👆 Listen to it here: Mongolian (﹙ ‍🎧 ﹚) Throat Singing - KHOOMEI    #music #song
description:
Mongolian Khoomei — Throat Singing & Morin Khuur  KHOOMEI — 

THE IMPOSSIBLE SOUND Khoomei (or höömii) is the Mongolian art of producing two or more notes simultaneously with the human voice 
— a bass drone sustained in the chest while a flute-like overtone melody is articulated in the oral cavity. It is perhaps the single most astonishing vocal technique in human musical history: one person creating what sounds like two separate instruments.  
THE PHYSICS OF OVERTONE SINGING When the bass drone is sustained at a stable pitch, the upper vocal tract (pharynx, mouth, lips) can be shaped to selectively amplify specific harmonics of that fundamental frequency. By adjusting tongue position, lip shape, and jaw opening, the singer causes different harmonics to ring out as discrete, audible melodic tones — while the fundamental continues beneath.  
SYGYT STYLE This recording uses the sygyt ("whistling") style of khoomei — the clearest and most melodically developed form, in which the overtone melody sings in a pure, flute-like register against the sustained bass drone.  
THE MORIN KHUUR The morin khuur (horse head fiddle) is the national instrument of Mongolia. Its two strings are made of horse hair, its bow is strung with horse hair, and its scroll is carved in the form of a horse's head. The morin khuur carries the spirit of the horse — and in Mongolian culture, the horse is the vehicle of the soul.  
THE STEPPE The vast open landscape of the Mongolian steppe — where there are no walls, no ceilings, and no horizons — is the natural reverb of this music. Its empty, resonant openness is built into every tone. 

#MongolianThroatSinging #Khoomei #MorinKhuur #OvertoneSinging #MongolianMusic #SteppeSound

title: Khoomei trifft Blockbuster: Epische Musik in „Ne Zha 2“! | DRM News | AA15
description:
Der Animationsblockbuster Ne Zha 2 setzt auf mongolischen Khoomei-Gesang, um eine faszinierende Klanglandschaft zu schaffen. Dieses traditionelle Kehlkopfgesangsstil wurde von Halamuji, einem 26-jährigen Musiker, in die Filmmusik integriert und untermalt zentrale Szenen wie das Herabsteigen des „Himmlischen Kessels“. Neben Khoomei kombiniert der Film weitere ethnische Musiktraditionen Chinas. Ne Zha 2, der bereits über 10 Milliarden Yuan (1,4 Milliarden USD) eingespielt hat, läuft nun auch international und soll die kulturelle Vielfalt Chinas einem weltweiten Publikum näherbringen.

title: KHOOMEI BEAT-“Dorugdayym” (Tuvan throat singing, world music)
description:
Для тувинцев конь - это верный друг, символ преданности и свободы. В тувинской народной песне «Доругдайым» хөөмейжи воспевают своего быстрого скакуна. 
Доруг - с тув.языка означает гнедой темно-рыжей шерсти или масти, с черным хвостом и гривой.

For tuvans a horse a faithful friend, symbol of devotion and freedom. In the Tuvan folk song "Dorugdayym” homegji sings his fast horse. 
Dorug means of the color of the horse.

title: Genghis Khan's Ancestry 🔮 Mongolian Throat Singing Jazz | Shamans of the Eternal Steppe
description:
Genghis Khan's Ancestry — a journey into the ancient soul of Mongolia.
The shamans of the eternal blue sky, the spirit of the great Khan,
and the throat singers who carry his memory across the endless steppe.

From the sacred fires of the nomadic camps to the vast horizons
of the Mongolian plains — this is the music of conquerors,
ancestors and eternal spirits.

🎧 Khoomei throat singing | Morin Khuur | Mongolian Jazz

🎵 LYRICS | ҮГҮҮД:

[Verse]
Хөөмий дуун талын салхинд
Морин хуурын уйлах дуун
Өргөн талын нар жаргахад
Монгол хүний зүрхний дуун

[Chorus]
Уу уу уу... хөөмий...
Тал нутгийн эрх чөлөө
Уу уу уу... хөөмий...
Монгол минь мөнхөд байна

[Verse]
Чингис хааны нутаг дэвсгэр
Морьдын туурайн дуун
Гал голомтын дэргэд суун
Өвгөдийн дуун эргэн ирнэ

[Outro]
Уу уу уу...
Монгол... Монгол...

The throat singing echoes in the steppe wind
The morin khuur weeps its ancient song
As the sun sets over the vast plains
The heart of the Mongolian people sings

Freedom of the homeland
Mongolia lives forever

The land of Genghis Khan
The sound of horses hooves

title: Cantos espirituales (sygyt, khoomei, kargyraa y Gyü-ke [canto tibetano gutural]).
description:
Descubre la fuerza ancestral de la voz.
En este video te llevo a un viaje sonoro por cuatro técnicas vocales profundas y espirituales: Sygyt, Khoomei, Kargyraa y Gyü-ke (canto tibetano). Desde la resonancia brillante y silbante del Sygyt, pasando por las armonías del Khoomei, hasta la vibración grave del Kargyraa y la potencia meditativa del Gyü-ke, cada estilo conecta con tradiciones antiguas que utilizan la voz como puente entre cuerpo, energía y conciencia.

Un recorrido para quienes aman la música tradicional, la espiritualidad, la exploración vocal y los sonidos que transforman el espacio interior.
Siéntelo, respíralo y déjate llevar por la vibración.

title: TURAN — Yeni Çağın Şafağı | Türk Şamanik Epik | Bozkır Destanı | Khoomei & Dombra 🐺🦅
description:
🐺🦅 TURAN — Yeni Çağın Şafağı 🦅🐺
Türk Şamanik Epik | Kazak Ethno-Folk | Erkek & Kadın Düet
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

𝗧𝗨𝗥𝗔𝗡 — Binlerce yıllık bir rüyanın, yeni çağda yeniden doğuş hikâyesi.

Bu şarkı, Altay dağlarından Tuna boylarına uzanan kadim Türk ruhunun modern dünyada yankılanan sesidir. Ergenekon'dan çıkışın ateşi, Göktürk yazıtlarının taşa kazınan vasiyeti, Oğuz Han'ın altın yayı ve Asena'nın karanlığı yırtan koşusu — hepsi bu destanda birleşiyor.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🐺 TURAN HİKÂYESİ — Neden Şimdi?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Turan, sadece bir coğrafya değil — bir bilinç, bir uyanış, bir ruh hâlidir.

Binlerce yıl önce, Orta Asya'nın uçsuz bucaksız bozkırlarında bir medeniyet doğdu. Göktürkler, Hunlar, Oğuzlar, Kıpçaklar, Kazaklar, Kırgızlar, Özbekler, Türkmenler, Azeriler ve Anadolu Türkleri — hepsi aynı kökten, aynı gök kubbenin altından, aynı Tengri'nin nefesinden.

Tarih boyunca dağıldılar, ayrı devletler kurdular, ayrı diller konuştular. Ama kanlarında akan kod hep aynı kaldı: Bozkurt'un uluması, kartalın süzülüşü, dombranın telleri ve kobyzın ağıtı.

Şimdi dünya yeni bir düzene evriliyor. Eski güç dengeleri çatırdıyor, yeni ittifaklar kuruluyor. Batı'nın yüzyıllık hegemonyası sorgulanırken, Doğu'dan yeni sesler yükseliyor. İşte tam bu kavşakta, Türk dünyası yeniden birbirine bakıyor.

Türk Devletleri Teşkilatı, Oğuz Birliği vizyonu, İpek Yolu'nun dijital çağda yeniden canlanması — bunlar artık hayal değil, adım adım gerçeğe dönüşen bir irade. TURAN, bu iradenin müzikal manifestosudur.

Bu şarkıda Bozkurt 🐺 — Türk'ün cesaretini, bağımsızlığını ve savaşçı ruhunu temsil eder. Kartal 🦅 — gökyüzüne hükmeden vizyonu, özgürlüğü ve Tengri ile bağı simgeler. İkisi birlikte: Turan'ın iki kanadı.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🎵 MÜZİKAL YOLCULUK
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🎻 Enstrümanlar: Dombra, Kobyz, Shankobyz (Ağız Kopuzu), Dongira (Şaman Davulu)
🗣️ Vokal: Khoomei (Gırtlak Şarkısı), Erkek & Kadın Düet, Savaş Çığlığı, Tribal Vokal
🥁 Ritim: 100 BPM, Dörtnala At Ritmi, Şamanik Transa Hipnotik Vuruş
🌍 Tür: Kazak Ethno-Folk, Türk Şamanik Müzik, Sinematik Bozkır Epik

Şarkı, bir şamanik ritüel olarak başlar — rüzgârın fısıltısı, kobyzın iniltisi, dongira davulunun kalp atışı. Kadim bir dua yükselir karanlıktan. Sonra dombra dörtnala koşmaya başlar, khoomei gırtlaktan yükselen doğaüstü bir güç olarak katılır. Erkek savaşçının sesi ile kadın şamanın kehaneti iç içe geçer — tıpkı Oğuz Han ile Umay Ana'nın birlikteliği gibi.

Koro, bir savaş çığlığıdır: "TURAN! TURAN!" — yedi boyun birleştiği an, göğün altında tek yürek olan bir milletin manifestosu.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📜 ŞARKI SÖZLERİ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

[Intro]
Ey Kök Tengri... Ey Yer-Sub...
Uyan dedelerimizin ruhu...
Bozkurt ulumaya başladı... Vakit geldi...

[Verse 1 — Erkek / Khoomei]
Ergenekon'dan çıkan ateş sönmedi
Demir dağı eriten yürek dönmedi
Altay'dan Tuna'ya bir nefes taşır rüzgâr
Atalarım fısıldar: "Korkma, uyan artık çağlar"

Göktürk yazıtları taşlarda susmadı
Bilge Kağan'ın sözü asla unutulmadı
"Türk milleti yok olma, var ol!" dedi taşa kazıdı
Bu söz bugün de yarına köprü, ateşle yazıldı

[Pre-Chorus]
Dört bir yandan yükseliyor soyun sesi
Turan'ın kalbinde atıyor bin yılın nefesi
Eski dünya çatırdıyor, devler sallanıyor
Bozkurt gözünü açtı — yeni çağ başlıyor!

[Chorus — Erkek & Kadın Düet]
Turan! Turan! Yükselen güneş doğudan!
Bir millet, bir nefes, bir yürek, bir ferman!
Göğün altında birleşir yedi boyun kanı
Turan uyanıyor — bu yeni dünyanın sultanı!

Turan! Turan! Kurtlar semaya ulur!
Yeni dünya düzeni bozulur, yeniden kurulur!
Step rüzgârı taşır bu sesi her diyara
Tengri şahit — Turan yürüyor zafere, nura!

[Verse 2 — Kadın]
Anadolu'dan Sibirya'ya bir ana ağladı
Her çocuğuna ayrı dilde ninni bağladı
Ama hepsi aynı südü emdi aynı kandan
Özbek, Kazak, Kırgız, Türkmen — hepsi aynı ocaktan

Yeni dünya tahtalar döşüyor kendi sarayına
Ama hesap sormadı bozkırın tek varisine
Uyuyan aslan sandılar, kurdun kılığına girdik
Şimdi gözümüz açık — kendi tarihimizi yazdık!

[Bridge — Ritüel Doruk]
Oğuz Han'ın altın yayı gerildi!
Üç ok, Boz ok — cihan yeniden bölündü!
Umay Ana koruyor beşiği
Asena koşuyor önde, yırtarak geceyi

[Outro]
Tengri biz menen... Türk'ün ateşi sönmez... Turan... ebediyen...

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

title: 몽골전통악기 마두금과 노래 Khoomei #music
description:
몽골 마두금
마두금(馬頭琴)은 몽골의 대표적인 전통 현악기로, 악기 끝부분이 말머리 모양으로 장식되어 있어 붙여진 이름입니다. 몽골어로는 '모린 후르(Morin khuur)'라고 부르며, 그 특유의 깊고 부드러운 음색 덕분에 '초원의 첼로'라고도 불립니다.마두금에 대한 핵심적인 정보는 다음과 같습니다.구조와 특징생김새: 사다리꼴 모양의 울림통 위에 긴 목이 있고, 맨 위에는 조각된 말머리가 달려 있습니다.재질: 울림통은 나무나 동물의 가죽으로 만들며, 두 줄의 현과 활은 주로 말의 꼬리털(마총)을 엮어 만듭니다.소리: 활로 줄을 문질러 소리를 내며, 말이 달리는 발굽 소리나 구슬픈 말의 울음소리를 섬세하게 표현할 수 있습니다.유래와 문화적 의미전설: 뻐꾸기로 변한 연인을 그리워하며 주인의 핍박 속에서 날아다니는 말을 타고 만났다는 애틋한 사랑 설화를 품고 있습니다.유네스코 유산: 몽골 유목민의 일상과 전통 제례에서 빠지지 않는 악기이며, 마두금으로 연주한 전통 음악은 2008년 유네스코 인류무형문화유산에 등재되었습니다.연주 방식: 주로 몽골의 전통 배음 창법인 흐미(Khoomei)와 함께 어우러져 신비롭고 웅장한 분위기를 자아냅니다.

The captivating sound of Khoomei, or throat singing, is a vocal art form rooted in the nomadic lifestyle where people feel at one with nature. Singers imitate sounds from the natural world and can produce two or more pitches simultaneously, creating a unique and mesmerizing auditory experience.
In Kazakh culture, Khoomei is more than just a musical technique — it symbolizes respect for nature and seeks spiritual coexistence with the environment. Kazakh Khoomei inheritor Dumanbek Butikhan and his band have been reinterpreting ancient Kazakh lyrics, while blending traditional instruments, with their enchanting performances breathing new life into this rich intangible cultural heritage.

title: 몽골 Khoomei Style Music 반야심경(般若心經)  Ver 2.0 | 10번
description:
몽골어로된 음악 반야심경 Ver 2.0 으로.
흐미(Khoomei) 스타일을 접목한 음악입니다.

[반야심경(般若心經)]은 불교 경전 중 가장 널리 알려진 짧고도 깊은 경전으로, 대승불교의 지혜(般若, 반야)를 핵심적으로 담고 있습니다. "심경"이라는 이름은 이 경전이 반야바라밀다의 핵심을 설명하고 있음을 의미합니다. 반야심경은 공(空)의 이치를 깨닫게 해주고, 수행자가 무상(無常)과 무아(無我)의 진리를 바르게 인식하도록 도와줍니다.



Маха Пражна Парамита зүрхний судар!
마하반야바라밀다심경

Авалокитесвара Бодьсадва гүн гүнзгий Пражна Парамита бясалгах үед.
관자재보살이 깊은 반야바라밀다를 행할 때.

Охоны хоосон орон зайг эргэцүүлэн бодох нь.
오온이 공한것을 비추어보고.

Бүх зовлон зүдгүүрийг даван туулах.
온갖 고통에서 건너느니라.

Сарижаёо! Өнгө нь бөмбөгнөөс ялгаатай биш юм.
사리자여! 색이 공과 다르지 않고.

Бөмбөг нь өнгөөрөө ялгаатай биш юм.
공이 색과 다르지 않으며.

Өнгө нь хоосон чанар, хоосон нь өнгө юм.
색이 곧 공이요 공이 곧 색이니.

Шагнал гардуулах ёслолын хувьд ч мөн адил.
수상행식도 그러하니라.

Бүх хууль олон нийтэд нээлттэй. Төрсөн ч үгүй, устгагдаагүй ч.
모든 법은 공하여. 나지도 멸하지도 않으며.

Бохир ч биш, цэвэр ч биш.
더럽지도 깨끗하지도 않으며.

Энэ нь нэмэгдэхгүй, буурахгүй.
늘지도 줄지도 않느니라.

Тэгэхээр тэнгэрийн дунд ямар ч өнгө, дүрс байхгүй
그러므로 공 가운데는 색이 없고 수상행식도 없으며

Нүд, хамар, чих, хамар, нүд, нүд, хүрэлт байхгүй
안이비설신의도 없고 색성향미촉법도 없으며

Нүд ба ухамсар хоёрын хооронд ямар ч хил хязгаар байхгүй.
눈의 경계도 의식의 경계까지도 없고.

Нэрээ нууцлах, бүр нэрээ нууцлах төгсгөл гэж байдаггүй.
무명도 무명이 다함까지도 없으며.

Өтлөх, үхэх гэж байдаггүй, хөгшрөлт, үхлийн төгсгөл гэж байдаггүй.
늙고 죽음도 늙고 죽음이 다함까지도 없고.

Зөрүүд, мөхөх гэж байдаггүй, мэргэн ухаан, олз гэж байдаггүй.
고집멸도도 없으며, 지혜도 얻음도 없느니라.

Хожих зүйл байхгүй тул Бодьсадва Пражна Парамитад тулгуурладаг.
얻을 것이 없는 까닭에 보살은 반야바라밀다를 의지하므로.

Надад санаа зовох зүйл, айдас байхгүй
마음에 걸림이 없고 두려움도 없어서

Би дэмий хоосон бодлуудаа орхиод бүрэн нирваан руу орлоо.
뒤바뀐 헛된 생각을 멀리 떠나 완전한 열반에 들어가며

Гурван үеийн Будда нар мөн Пражна Парамита, Хумм~ дээр тулгуурладаг
삼세의 부처님들도 반야바라밀다를 의지하니

Хамгийн дээд гэгээрэлд хүр.
최상의 깨달음을 얻느니라.

Пражна Парамита бол хамгийн нууцлаг бөгөөд тод шившлэг
반야바라밀다는 가장 신비하고 밝은 주문이며

Гайхалтай захиалга байна. Хумм~ Энэ бол зүйрлэшгүй захиалга
위대한 주문이며. 무엇과도 견줄 수 없는 주문이니

Энэ нь бүх зовлон зүдгүүрийг арилгах бөгөөд үнэн бөгөөд дэмий хоосон биш
온갖 괴로움을 없애고 진실하여 허망하지 않느니라

title: Ne Zha 2’s Hidden Gem: Throat Singing (Khoomei) — The Soulful Voice Behind the Tianyuan Ding
description:
The Chinese animated film Ne Zha 2 has become a global box office phenomenon, captivating audiences not only in China but around the world. Today, let's explore one of the film's hidden cultural treasures: throat singing, or khoomei, which serves as the powerful background voice of the Tianyuan Ding.
 
In Ne Zha 2, the Tianyuan Ding is the Yuxu Palace’s greatest treasure, a mystical artifact capable of transforming creatures into elixirs. Its appearance is accompanied by an intense and haunting soundtrack composed using throat singing, adding to the sense of mystery surrounding the artifact.
 
Throat singing, an ancient vocal tradition from Mongolian culture, is a UNESCO-recognized Intangible Cultural Heritage. The technique involves producing multiple harmonic tones simultaneously. It is a form of singing that embodies mystery, strength, and a vivid connection to the natural world.
 
The emotional range of throat singing is vast. From intense war chants to lyrical melodies about the grasslands, love and life with herds, its music is both energizing and serene, making it a versatile tool in storytelling.
 
Wan Pin Chu, one of the original music producers for the film, explained how the Tianyuan Ding represents an evil force in Ne Zha 2. Throat singing was the perfect way to convey its solemn and religious aura.
 
Inner Mongolian throat singer Halamuji, who performed in the film, revealed how the technique is not just limited to the sound behind Tianyuan Ding. It is also woven throughout the villainous background music.
 
By incorporating this fascinating and captivating vocal tradition, Ne Zha 2 not only enriches its storytelling but also serves as a vibrant tribute to Chinese culture.
 
#NeZha2 #ThroatSinging #K­­­hoomei #InnerMongolia

title: The Resonant Journey: Master Otgonhuu performs "Kargyraa-Borbangnadyr" Khoomei
description:
Immerse yourself in a soundscape where deep resonance meets rhythmic movement.

This is "Kargyraa-Borbangnadyr," a complex and evocative style of overtone singing performed by Master Otgonhuu. It uniquely combines the profound, rumbling tones of **Kargyraa (Deep Khoomei)** with the rolling, reverberating patterns of **Borbangnadyr (Reverberating Khoomei)**. This fusion creates a rich, textured vocal performance that is both grounding and mesmerizing, transporting the listener to ancient lands.

// ABOUT THE ARTIST //
Otgonhuu is a Khoomei artist and culture bearer from the Altai Mountain region. He grew up surrounded by these ancient sounds and has dedicated his life to preserving their purest form. To him, Khoomei is the root of his culture and a bridge that connects nature with the soul.

// LEARN KHOOMEI WITH THE MASTER //
Are you captivated by this incredible sound? You can learn to create it, too.
The techniques showcased in this performance are all part of a clear, systematic learning path. Otgonhuu has distilled his 40 years of personal practice and over 20 years of teaching experience into a unique online course, designed to help enthusiasts from all over the world awaken the ancient voice sleeping within them.

🐎🌿 Mongolian Instrumental Music | Soft, Catchy & Cinematic Steppe Ambience 🌿🐎
Close your eyes and feel the wind sweep across the vast golden steppes of Mongolia. This soft yet rhythmically alive instrumental blends the soulful cry of the morin khuur, the playful pulse of the tovshuur, and the deep earthly groove of traditional hand drums — weaving together an unforgettable sound that feels both ancient and refreshingly alive.
Subtle throat singing (khoomei) hums beneath the melody like a distant echo across open plains, adding a layer of mystique and depth that pulls you into a world of nomadic spirit and endless horizons.

title: Mongolian art of singing ,Khoomei——music in northern China
description: Khoomei is a unique singing art created by the Mongolians,which means that one singer sings two voice parts at the same time barele by this own vocal organ , listening carefully, you would find that the singer sends out both a continued thick bass, and a transparent and clear high pich,The bass of khoomei is a continuous one,the piches of which sometimes change,and the high part is a lumpy strain with no lyrics, While playing the Khoomei,singers should firstly loosen the vocal cords, and with the help of breath in the mouth, vibreat it to prouduce acoustical resonances to utter the bass notes, and then subtly adjust the apex gap and produce high notes by blowing a deep breath . A competent Khoomei player can play various types of musics with the method of Khoomei,in the last ,welcome to china for listening Mongolia music! and welcome to mobee bulid，ShangHai,China!

title: Mongolian art of singing: Khoomei
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00210
Description: The Mongolian art of singing: Khoomei, or Hooliin Chor (throat harmony), is a style of singing in which a single performer produces a diversified harmony of multiple voice parts, including a continued bass element produced in the throat. These singers may perform alone or in groups. Khoomei is practised today among Mongolian communities in several countries, especially in Inner Mongolia in northern China, western Mongolia and the Tuva Republic of Russia. Traditionally performed on the occasion of ritual ceremonies, songs express respect and praise for the natural world, for the ancestors of the Mongolian people and for great heroes. The form is reserved for special events and group activities such as horse races, archery and wrestling tournaments, large banquets and sacrificial rituals. The timing and order of songs is often strictly regulated. Khoomei has long been regarded as a central element representing Mongolian culture and remains a strong symbol of national or ethnic identity. As a window into the philosophy and aesthetic values of the Mongol people, it has served as a kind of cultural emissary promoting understanding and friendship among China, Mongolia and Russia, and has attracted attention around the world as a unique form of musical expression.
Country(ies): China
© 2008 IMARI

title: The stirring art of Mongolian throat singing
description:
The capabilities of a human body are sometimes beyond a brain’s imagination. For example, it’s hard for most people to believe the sound in the video above came from a human being rather than an instrument. That is because the singer is able to produce a continuous bass and simultaneously produce one or more pitches through his/her throat. That unique way of singing is known as khoomei, or hooliin chor (throat singing), an art of singing practiced by Mongolian communities in Inner Mongolia in northern China, Mongolia and the Russian republic of Tuva. It is also known as Tuvan throat singing in other cultures. This singing art was officially inscribed on the List of Intangible Cultural Heritage by the UNESCO in 2009.

title: Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum | Standard vs. Tibetan #mantra #chant #spiritual
description:
Throat Singing OM Mani Padme Hum in two different ways.

Om Mani Padme Hum (Sanskrit-based transliteration):
This version uses Sanskrit or classical Romanized spelling.
Common in academic texts or Pan-Asian Buddhist contexts.
In actual Tibetan speech, it wouldn’t be pronounced exactly this way.

Om Mani Peme Hung (Tibetan phonetic spelling):
Reflects Tibetan pronunciation. Common in Tibetan-speaking areas and among practitioners aiming for more authentic spoken form.
Pronunciation:
Padme → Peme (དཔལ་མེད་)
Hum → Hung (ཧཱུྃ)

This ancient chant is said to hold infinite benefits—used for protection, purification, and higher manifestation.

“Om Mani Padme Hum” is recited to neutralize negative karma and dissolve spiritual obstacles.

Just listening to this mantra can shift your energy, raise your vibration, align you with the true essence of reality, attract higher experiences, and unlock hidden aspects of the self.

It is the most important mantra in Tibetan Buddhism. Each syllable purifies one of the six realms of existence and aligns body, speech, and mind with the path to enlightenment.

Sound, when repeated with intention, transforms.

Share to multiply the energy of this chant

title: Throat Singing Around The World
description:
throat-singing around the world 🌎 throat-singing originated amongst turko-mongol tribes of the altai and sayan mountains of southern siberia and western mongolia, but is also a practice in northern canada and south africa where it has taken on its own form.

to make this unique sound, a singer will produce two or more notes simultaneously by using precise movements in their lips, tongue, jaw, valum and larynx. this produces a distinct set of harmonies through the throat’s resonant qualities.

title: Ne Zha 2’s Hidden Gem: Throat Singing (Khoomei) — The Soulful Voice Behind the Tianyuan Ding
description:
The Chinese animated film Ne Zha 2 has become a global box office phenomenon, captivating audiences not only in China but around the world. Today, let's explore one of the film's hidden cultural treasures: throat singing, or khoomei, which serves as the powerful background voice of the Tianyuan Ding.
 
In Ne Zha 2, the Tianyuan Ding is the Yuxu Palace’s greatest treasure, a mystical artifact capable of transforming creatures into elixirs. Its appearance is accompanied by an intense and haunting soundtrack composed using throat singing, adding to the sense of mystery surrounding the artifact.
 
Throat singing, an ancient vocal tradition from Mongolian culture, is a UNESCO-recognized Intangible Cultural Heritage. The technique involves producing multiple harmonic tones simultaneously. It is a form of singing that embodies mystery, strength, and a vivid connection to the natural world.
 
The emotional range of throat singing is vast. From intense war chants to lyrical melodies about the grasslands, love and life with herds, its music is both energizing and serene, making it a versatile tool in storytelling.
 
Wan Pin Chu, one of the original music producers for the film, explained how the Tianyuan Ding represents an evil force in Ne Zha 2. Throat singing was the perfect way to convey its solemn and religious aura.
 
Inner Mongolian throat singer Halamuji, who performed in the film, revealed how the technique is not just limited to the sound behind Tianyuan Ding. It is also woven throughout the villainous background music.
 
By incorporating this fascinating and captivating vocal tradition, Ne Zha 2 not only enriches its storytelling but also serves as a vibrant tribute to Chinese culture.
 
#NeZha2 #ThroatSinging #K­­­hoomei #InnerMongolia

title: Cântico gutural Tuvan - khoomei ( Huun-Huur-Tu - Live )
description:
Khoomei:
língua Tuva : Хөөмей ; mongol : , Baopei transliteração : kögemei , cirílico : Хөөмий

A arte da Mongólia, cantando: Khoomei, ou Hooliin Chor ("harmonia da garganta"), é um estilo de cantar no qual um único cantor produz uma harmonia diversificada de vários tons de voz, incluindo um elemento de baixo contínuo produzido na garganta. 
Esses cantores podem realizar sozinhos ou em grupos. Khoomei é praticada hoje entre as comunidades da Mongólia em vários países, especialmente no interior da Mongólia, no norte da China, Mongólia ocidental e na República de Tuva da Rússia.

Tradicionalmente realizada por ocasião de cerimoniais, rituais, canções onde se expressa o respeito e louvor para o mundo natural, para os antepassados do povo mongol e para grandes heróis. O formulário é reservado para eventos especiais e atividades em grupo, como corridas de cavalos, tiro com arco e luta livre em torneios, grandes banquetes e rituais de sacrifício. O calendário e a ordem das músicas é muitas vezes estritamente regulamentado. “Khoomei” tem sido considerado como um elemento central que representa a cultura mongol e continua a ser um forte símbolo de identidade nacional ou étnica. Como uma janela para a filosofia e os valores estéticos das comunidades mongóis, tem servido como uma espécie de emissário cultural em promover a compreensão e amizade entre China, Mongólia e Rússia, e tem atraído a atenção de todo o mundo como uma forma única de expressão musical.

Há muito tempo, a Mongólia incluiu o Khoomei como tesouro nacional, e Tuva colocou o Khoomei visto como a alma nacional de seu povo. E na China, juntamente com a ecologia original das canções folclóricas desde as escavações, também foi visto como uma prática folclórica chinesa.Atualmente, a República do Governo Popular da China tem um projeto, onde a UNESCO, declara e inclui o Khoomei como "obras-primas do Patrimônio Oral e Imaterial da Humanidade".

Em 2009, entrou oficialmente para o Patrimônio Cultural Imaterial Mundial destacando o Teatro chamado “Inner Mongolia Nacional de Canto e Dança”.

Critérios para inscrição na Lista Representativa

Em arquivos de nomeação, a (s) parte que apresente Estado (s) é (são) solicitado a demonstrar que um elemento proposto para inscrição na Lista Representativa do Patrimônio Cultural Imaterial da Humanidade satisfaz todos os critérios a seguir:

R.1 O elemento constitui patrimônio cultural imaterial, tal como definido no artigo 2 da Convenção.

R.2 Inscrição do elemento contribuirá para assegurar a visibilidade e conscientização da importância do patrimônio cultural intangível e de encorajar o diálogo, o que reflete a diversidade cultural no mundo inteiro e testemunhando a criatividade humana.

R.3 medidas de salvaguarda são elaborados que podem proteger e promover o elemento.

R.4 O elemento foi nomeado após a mais ampla participação possível da comunidade, grupo ou, se for o caso, os indivíduos em causa e com seu consentimento livre, prévio e informado.

R.5 O elemento é incluído em um inventário do patrimônio cultural imaterial existente no território (s) da apresentação de Estado (s) Parte (s), conforme definido no artigo 11 e artigo 12 da Convenção

title: Throat Singing Guan Yin Mantra for Compassion, Mercy, Protection & Ending Suffering #kuanyin #chant
description:
Throat singing a mantra for compassion, mercy, ending suffering, and protection from danger.

This chant is dedicated to Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of Compassion, known as the one who hears the cries of the world. Repeating “Námó Guānshìyīn Púsà” is traditionally believed to bring protection from harm and danger, ease suffering, and strengthen compassion within oneself.

The phrase comes from Chinese Mahayana Buddhism. “Námó” means “homage” or “I take refuge in,” and “Guānshìyīn Púsà” means “Bodhisattva who perceives the sounds of the world.” This invocation has been used for centuries in East Asian Buddhist practice as a way of calling on Guanyin’s compassion and support.

Hello! 

Recently on Reddit, I posted a poll about which video I should make: https://www.reddit.com/r/Hordecore/
Taking the votes into account, also interests of my friends who wanted to learn Mongolian throat singing, I present you "how to throat sing in 3 easy steps!" =)

It might take some dedication to get it better, it could be a bit challenging for girls but personally, I've seen a live show of a Tuvan woman, musician, and artist Sainkho, who can do a few forms of this culture. Another person was a female student I met a few years ago. She used to learn how to play morin khuur with a few boys when she was young, the boys were learning khoomei. They teased her that she can't sing it because she is a girl, so she got irritated and practiced morin khuur with khoomei at home. After some time, it seems she was able to sing as well as any other person. There is another woman who can sing it as well, she plays in Tuvan folk-rock band Yat-Kha :)

In any case, let me know in the comments below if you have questions or ideas. Oh, one detail I forgot to mention in the video is that this type of singing is specifically called "kargyraa". The other one is called "isgeree"(or sygyt in Tuvan) is quite difficult to learn, I still haven't mastered it fully. The intro music was done originally by ElectroKaplosion - coffin dance music with Mongolian theme, a super cool dude, check out his channel :D - https://www.youtube.com/user/ElectroKaplosion

title: Throat Singing RARE ANCIENT EGYPTIAN for Power, Life-force, Awakening & Protection #mantra #newage
description:
Throat Singing this Ancient Egyptian Chant for Power, Life Force, Protection and Awakening 

Sa Sekhem Sahu awakens your inner power, ignites your energy body, and aligns your soul with divine order. This ancient Egyptian formula merges life-force, divine strength, and the immortal light-body. 

Sa (sꜣ) 𓋴𓅱
Literal Meaning: Protection, life-force, amulet, or magical safeguard.
Esoteric Meaning: The spiritual shield or flow of vital energy surrounding the soul. In esoteric terms, it is the radiant field or aura encoded into the body.


Sekhem (sḫm) 𓋴𓐍𓅓
Literal Meaning: Power, strength, authority, inner might.
Esoteric Meaning: The divine force or pranic fire of a being — similar to chi, kundalini, or shakti. It also represents the energetic weapon wielded by deities and the spiritually awakened.


Sahu (sꜣḥw) 𓋴𓄿𓎛𓅱
Literal Meaning: Spiritual body or transfigured soul — the immortal self post-death.
Esoteric Interpretation: The divinized form of the soul after uniting ka (vitality) and ba (spirit), often emerging through rituals and funerary ascent. In living practice, Sahu refers to the awakened body of light. 

This mantra was carved on sarcophagi, temple walls, and pyramid texts, especially in Old Kingdom funerary spells used to guide the deceased into the afterlife — but its power was also understood to elevate the living. “Sahu” appears as the highest form the soul could attain after divine unification. 

Found in the Pyramid Texts of Unas (circa 2350 BCE), these words weren’t just symbolic — they were considered living formulas, infused with real cosmic force. 

Use of this chant bridges the body, ka (vitality), ba (spirit), and sahu (immortal essence). Its vibrational force is ideal for throat singing, as it activates deep resonance across the spine and energy centers.

title: khoomei, mongolian throat singing...
description:
Mongolian throat singing, also known as Khöömei, is a remarkable traditional singing technique that originates from Mongolia and is practiced by various Central Asian ethnic groups. It involves producing multiple harmonic tones simultaneously, creating a unique and mesmerizing sound.

Here's a more detailed explanation of Mongolian throat singing:

Throat Singing Techniques: There are different styles of throat singing within Mongolian culture, and each has its distinct sound. Some of the popular techniques include "Khargyraa," which produces a deep, guttural growl, "Khoomei," which creates a harmonic overtone, and "Sygyt," which produces a high-pitched, whistling tone.

Producing Harmonics: The throat singer manipulates their vocal cords, throat, and mouth to produce two distinct pitches at the same time. The base note is sung in a low register, while the overtone, or harmonic, is created by shaping the mouth and throat in specific ways to amplify and isolate specific frequencies.

Cultural Significance: Throat singing has deep cultural roots in Mongolia and is considered an important aspect of their musical heritage. It was historically practiced by nomadic herders during long periods of isolation and serves as a form of entertainment, communication, and cultural expression.

Nomadic Tradition: The nomadic lifestyle of the Mongolian people influenced their musical traditions. Throat singing was often performed during herding tasks or around the campfire, creating a sense of community and connection.

Modern Recognition: Mongolian throat singing gained international recognition in the 20th century, and some skilled throat singers have performed on global stages and in various world music festivals.

UNESCO Cultural Heritage: In 2010, UNESCO inscribed Mongolian throat singing into its list of endangered cultural practices, recognizing its significance and the need to preserve this traditional art form.

Inner and Outer Mongolian Styles: Throat singing is practiced not only in Mongolia but also among ethnic Mongolian communities in neighboring regions such as Inner Mongolia (China), Tuva (Russia), and parts of Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan. Each region has its distinct style and variation of throat singing.

Spiritual and Shamanic Connection: Throat singing is sometimes associated with spiritual and shamanic practices. Some Mongolian throat singers believe that they can evoke natural elements and connect with ancestral spirits through their singing.

International Collaboration: Throat singers have collaborated with musicians from various genres, blending traditional sounds with modern music. These collaborations have led to innovative fusions that appeal to a broader audience.

Mongolian throat singing continues to captivate audiences worldwide with its unique and hauntingly beautiful sound. It remains an essential part of Mongolian culture, reflecting the deep connection between the nomadic lifestyle, the natural environment, and the spiritual beliefs of the Mongolian people.

title: Tuvan Throat Overtone Singing (Sygyt Style)  Beginner
description:
**Please use headphone to hear better & focus in the whistle /angelic kind of sound, not the monotonous high pitch sound 
(Turkish: Lutfen daha iyi duymak icin kulaklik takin ve arkadan gelen islik seslerine odaklanin (monoton sese degil). Yontemin Turkce aciklamasi ingilizcenin altinda) 
--------------------------------
Tuvan Throat (Overtone) Singing, Sygyt Style where singer can have 2 notes at the same time . Overtone singing—also known as overtone chanting or throat singing—is a type of singing in which the singer manipulates the resonances. The harmonics of a sound wave made by the human voice can be selectively amplified by changing the shape of the resonant cavities of the mouth, larynx and pharynx. This resonant tuning allows singers to create apparently more than one pitch at the same time (the fundamental and a selected overtone), while actually generating only a single fundamental frequency with their vocal cords. 
After 2 months of practice finally began to get some Overtones. Tuvan Turk & Mongols use this technique for thousand and thousands of years. sygyt is the angelic/heavenly whistle sound there is also khoomei which is dark voice coming from throat . I just start getting Sygyty & will soon start practicing Khoomei so my high pitch monotonous sound can replace with low pitch & my overtones can be more clear. So first sygyts although sometimes I try 10-20 times and maynot get overtones, on my newbie level it shows variability as I cannot control it yet . lots of patience and practice needed 
----------------------------

Buna Sygyt metodu deniyor; Khöömei denen kalin girtlak sesi de baska yontem ve hatta khöömei monoton sesi ile sygyt islik sesi daha da belirgin oluyor ama ben daha khöömei cikaramiyorum, 2 aylik calisma sonrasi ancak az az sygyt; insallah sabirla cok calisarak yakinda.

title: Throat Singing Ancient Sumerian Chant for Power & Victory
description:
Throat singing an Ancient Sumerian chant for power & victory.

This ancient Sumerian chant is an invocation to Ninurta, the warrior god of storms, strength, and restoration. The phrase “Ninurta kalamma šu du₃-a” translates to “Ninurta, who raises the hand of the land.” In Sumerian, the “hand” (šu) symbolizes power and action, while “raising the hand” means to empower, elevate strength or invoke divine might and victory. The “land” (kalam) represents both the physical world and the human body, so the chant calls upon Ninurta to awaken inner vitality and authority.

Ninurta was revered as a divine force of disciplined power — a protector who fought chaos and restored order after battle. This chant channels that same energy, helping the practitioner align with focus, endurance, and controlled intensity.

This chant can be used to restore personal power, overcome inner conflict, and invoke resilience through sound and vibration.

title: Throat Singing Filipino Tree Chant for Success & Growth #meditation #mantra #manifestation
description:
Throat Singing this chant that I created for growth and success. It is in Tagalog.

Breakdown & Meaning:
This chant is based on the Bamboo tree analogy. When Chinese bamboo is planted, it is to be watered and fertilized regularly for 5 years and for the first 5 years absolutely nothing happens. No growth is seen; it is as if all this time, energy and effort is wasted. However, sometime in the fifth year, in a matter of just 6 weeks, the bamboo trees grow from nothing to 90 feet tall. If at any point the seeds weren't watered or fertilized in the first 5 years, the trees would not have grown.

This is an analogy for growth and success. It may seem at times that our efforts towards our goals are going nowhere but like the bamboo, they will sprout and grow for as long as you stay resilient, believe and keep going despite the lack of tangible results.

This chant "Tumubo na ang mga puno dito sa mundo" is intended to have all the trees (people's dreams) in the world sprout and grow. And as it is common knowledge in the spiritual field, that what you wish onto others and the energy you put out, comes back to you multiplied, this chant which evokes the growth of all people's goals, dreams and ambitions will come back to you multiplied. May this accelerate the growth of your trees.

The Mongols

The Mongols are a ethnic group native to Mongolia and to China's Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region. By the late 13th century the Mongol Empire spanned from the Pacific Ocean in the east to the Danube River and the shores of the Persian Gulf in the west.

The Huns

The Huns were a nomadic tribe prominent in the 4th and 5th century CE whose origin is unknown but, most likely originated in nowadays Northern China or Mongolia. In the Han Dynasty the Huns were called Xiongnu and invaded China before being driven out and let to exile. Over many years the Huns migrated from East Asia to Europe were they invaded the split-apart Roman Empire.

Throat Singing

Throat-singing, a guttural style of singing or chanting, is one of the world's oldest forms of music. Throat singing is found in 3 cultures: The Tuvans living in the rural region of Russia located northwest of Mongolia, the Inuit and the Xhosa people of Bantu. Throat singing is most often linked to the Mongolian Tuvan style which is also featured in this album. All samples are from real Tuvan Throat singers.

title: Lofty Spirits on the Grasslands - 2018 Shen Yun Symphony Orchestra #shenyun #music
description:
D.F. / arr. JING XIAN
An erhu melody evokes the expansive landscape of northern Asia. Joined by a cello solo in imitation of khoomei throat singing, the two instruments blend to symbolize a Mongolian call of welcome across the plains. In that part of the world, guests are treated to a ceremonial dance of hospitality, using tableware as a prop. Here, we can imagine the utensil of choice to be: chopsticks. Vigorous melodies portray the hosts’ enthusiasm, dancing with a cluster of chopsticks in each hand, rapping them against their limbs accompanied by crisp, staccato beats. The tempo slows to elongate their movements, expressing their connection to the vast grasslands. Sudden ricocheted bowing from the strings, reminiscent of galloping horses, sets the stage for a lively trumpet solo that segues into a spirited finale.

💎Watch the full performance: https://ept.ms/3ZuaudI

Sedaa means "voice" in Persian and connects traditional Mongolian music with the Orient which makes an extraordinary and fascinating sound. Master singer Nasaa Nasanjargal and Naraa Naranbaatar who studied in their Mongolian homeland, abduct you together with the virtuosic Dulcimer player Ganzorig Davaakhuu and the Iranian Multi- instrumentalist Omid Bahadori into one genuinely exotic world between Orient and Mongolian steppe.

title: Beijing International Film Festival meets the winds of Siberia
description:
From Tuva in Russia, Tyva Kyzy – the world's only all-female throat singing group – brings an ancient sound to a modern stage.
Led by Choduraa Tumat, the first woman honored as the "People's Khoomei Musician of Tuva," they broke centuries of tradition to redefine what khoomei, or throat singing, can be.
When CGTN's Ali showed Tumat the legendary bamboo forest scene from "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon," a Beijing International Film Festival favorite, the result was pure magic.
What does it sound like when Siberian throat singing meets Chinese martial arts cinema?
Watch this to find out.

title: The Beauty of Mongolian Throat Singing
description:
One of the world’s oldest musical forms, throat singing is a stunning physical feat in which performers use precise movements of the mouth, larynx and jaw to create multiple notes simultaneously. For Mendbayar, throat singing is more than a technically impressive performance style; it’s a source of profound happiness and a way to share the beauty of his native Mongolia. When he’s not performing for audiences across Europe and Asia, he’s training the next generation at his International Throat Singing School in Ulaanbaatar.

title: Қорқыт “Желмая”  The igil. Tuvan musical instrument
description: The igil  is a two-stringed Tuvan musical instrument, played by bowing the strings. (It is called "ikili" in Western Mongolia.) The neck and lute-shaped sound box are usually made of a solid piece of pine or larch. The top of the sound box may be covered with skin or a thin wooden plate. The strings, and those of the bow, are traditionally made of hair from a horse's tail (strung parallel), but may also be made of nylon. Like the morin khuur of Mongolia, the igil typically features a carved horse's head at the top of the neck above the tuning pegs, and both instruments are known as the horsehead fiddle.

title: Throat Singing Chinese Fire Horse Chant for Power, Forward Movement, Drive, Action & Destiny
description:
Throat singing a chant for power, forward movement, courage, drive, action, destiny, breakthrough & transformation

This chant is used to activate momentum, courage, passion, and decisive forward movement. It is especially powerful during periods of transition, when you are breaking out of stagnation or reclaiming personal power. The Fire Horse symbolizes unstoppable energy, charisma, independence, and the willingness to move boldly toward destiny. Chanting these words is believed to awaken inner drive, strengthen confidence, and ignite the life force needed to pursue goals, leadership, and creative expression without hesitation.

In esoteric practice, Fire Horse energy is associated with transformation through action — not waiting, but moving. The imagery of flames opening the path represents obstacles dissolving as determination increases. This can be applied to career movement, relationships, financial growth, and personal reinvention. Rather than suppressing fear, the vibration of the chant helps bring awareness to it while simultaneously amplifying courage and motivation, allowing energy that was previously blocked to flow again.

Fire Horse symbolism comes from traditional East Asian elemental astrology, where the combination of the Fire element and Horse animal represents intensity, magnetism, and powerful life direction. Historically, it has been viewed as both volatile and extraordinarily potent — capable of great success when consciously directed.

Happy Year of the Fire Horse!

Chant:
火馬奔天，赤焰開路，
陽魂覺醒，命力歸吾。
Huǒ mǎ bēn tiān, chì yàn kāi lù,
yáng hún jué xǐng, mìng lì guī wú.

Translation:
“The Fire Horse gallops through heaven, crimson flames open the way.
My solar spirit awakens — Life-Force / the power of destiny returns to me.”

Share to multiply the energy
