title: Can you sing two notes at once? Puedes cantar dos notas a la vez? #overtonesinging #cantodifonico
description:
Can you sing two notes at once? 
This is called overtone singing / throat singing/ polyphonic singing/ canto difonico/ canto armónico /
It’s a technique where one is able to isolate and amplify the overtone notes that exist in the fundamental singing note /
Enjoy:)

title: Altai Kai ( Tuva Throat Singing ) - Koionok
description: The Tuvans or Tuvinians (Turkish: Tuvalar; Mongolian: Tuva Uriankhai) are a Turkic ethnic group living in southern Siberia. Tuvan throat singing is one particular variant of overtone singing practiced by the Tuva people of southern Siberia. In Turkic and Mongolic languages, the name, Altai, means the "Golden Mountain. This mountain is holy for Mongols and Turkics people. Altai (also Altay) is a language of the Turkic group of languages. It is an official language of Altai Republic, Russia. The Tuvan people - along with the Yellow Uyghur Turks in China and Turkey - are one of the only two Turkic groups who are mainly adherents to Buddhism, combined with native shamanism.

title: Sad Mongol Lament- 𝙁𝙡𝙮 𝙁𝙡𝙮 𝙈𝙮 𝙎𝙖𝙙𝙣𝙚𝙨𝙨 by Hun Huur Tu #mongol
description:
🎨 Art: Mongol Warrior, Yuan Dynasty by Jack Huang (OC)
🎨 Dragon's Armory: https://dragonsarmory.blogspot.com/
🎨 Art blog: https://jackhuangartworks.blogspot.com/

🎵 Music by: Hun Huur Tu
🟢 Spotify: https://open.spotify.com/artist/1bK0K2tuAcvMScEWIHt54t

🟠 PATREON: https://www.patreon.com/dragonsarmory/overview

Fly Fly My Sadness by the Tuvan Band Hun Huur Tu with the Bulgarian Angelites. One of the art pieces I have done was that of a forlorn Mongol warrior riding in the wintry barrens, and the music that I was listening while I digitally painted and photobashed the image was Hun Huur Tu's soul wrenching vocals in Fly Fly My Sadness. It's so effortless in taking you to a barren headspace and feel that icy loneliness which bred the hardy people that conquered the vast expanse of the world. 

Though in abstraction, Mongol, Bulgarian, and Chinese (this channel) might seem incongruous and would have little to do with each other. In the context of the Mongol Empire which spanned most of Eurasia- all 3 were stitched in one. In this matter, the Mongol realm was the bridge and conduit where voices of Bulgaria, of Mongols, can still be heard as far as some great Chinese cities- such as Khanbaliq/ Dadu 大都  or modern Beijing or by a warrior born in what was Yuan China. Much (of these distinct voices) was at the disposal of a master that was literally the bridge of these worlds. 

In its lyrics Fly Fly My Sadness paints a soul crushing lament of one's immense pain in missing his mother: both internally and externally both against the elements and the tumulus nostalgia- laced emotions that are threatening to spill over.

title: Throat Singing Mantra with Infinite Benefits | Om Mani Padme Hum (8 different language forms)
description:
Throat singing a mantra with infinite benefits, but especially for compassion, emotional healing, inner peace, spiritual protection, and opening the heart.

This mantra is traditionally associated with compassion, mercy, emotional purification, and transforming suffering into wisdom. In this version, I explored Om Mani Padme Hum across 8 different traditional language forms and pronunciations that developed across cultures. Although the wording changes between regions, the intention remains connected to compassion, awareness, and the refinement of the heart.

This recording explores how one mantra can travel across languages while carrying different textures, sounds, and cultural expressions.

Om Mani Padme Hum originated in Sanskrit and became one of the most recognized Buddhist mantras, especially through traditions connected to Avalokiteśvara (Chenrezig / Guanyin), spreading across Tibet, Mongolia, China, Japan, Korea, Vietnam, Nepal, and beyond.

Share to multiply the energy

#OmManiPadmeHum #Mantra #Buddhism #spiritualawakening #Meditation

The site also features the unique Botamaoyin rock paintings, which are at least 3,000 years old. Keketuohai, China is one of eight new UNESCO Global Geopark designated this year.

title: Chinese Mongolian Musical Instrument - Morinhuur
description:
Matouqin is a two-string stringed instrument with a trapezoidal body and a handle carved into the shape of a horse's head. It is a favorite musical instrument of the Mongolian people.
Matouqin is a Mongolian folk stringed musical instrument. Mongolian called "Chao'er". The body is made of wood, about one meter long, and has two strings. The resonance box is trapezoid-shaped, and the sound is round, with a low back and low volume.

title: ホーミーと喉ベース組み合わせると...
description:
ホーミーとは
モンゴルの唱法の一つ。一人の人間が二つの声を出して歌う。男性による歌唱法であるが，歌詞をもたず，むしろ器楽的である。一つの声は喉の奥から絞り出される一定した持続低音，もう一つの声は口腔内の形を変えることによって得られる倍音である。聴き手は主として倍音の動きを旋律として追っていく。似たような唱法がユーラシア大陸の各地に存在し，シベリアのトゥバではホーメイと呼ばれ，こちらは歌詞の内容も重視する。ハカスではハイkhai，アルタイではカウィkay，バシキールではウズリャウuzlyauと呼ばれる。
(コトバンクより)

overtone singing is a voice technique where it seems like one person sings two notes at the same time. You can sing the overtone scale on one fundamental. Another fundamental has its own overtone scale, so in order to have more overtones to sing nice melodies, you can use different fundamentals and change them while singing.

this video is live, and every human being is capable of learning overtone singing ;)

title: 01.游览3026年，银河系蒙古族风格星际巨星杨雁雁演唱会：鸿雁“鸿雁，天空上，对对排成行……”
description:
3026年，银河系蒙古族风格星际巨星杨雁雁演唱会：鸿雁
“鸿雁，天空上，对对排成行……”
当这首苍凉、辽阔、深情的《鸿雁》，在3026年的银河系响起，那将是一场跨越光年的“星际思乡曲”！
蒙古族文化中的**“游牧”、“马背”、“长调”、“苍穹”，与科幻的“星际航行”、“反重力”、“戴森球”**简直是天作之合！
1️ 蒙古风格 = 视觉与世界观 (Visual & Worldview)
这是整个视频的 “美术指导”！
服装：液态金属蒙古袍、全息绿松石头冠、反重力战靴。
场景：赛博草原、悬浮蒙古包、星际游牧营地。
气质：苍凉、辽阔、狂野、神圣。
AI指令：Cyber-Mongol style, Nomadic sci-fi, Holographic steppe.
2️ 《鸿雁》 = 听觉与情感内核 (Audio & Emotion)
这是整个视频的 “灵魂BGM”！
旋律：苍凉的马头琴、悠扬的长调。
情感：思乡、辽阔、对故乡的眷恋。
AI指令（针对音乐）：Mongolian long song, Morin khuur (horse-head fiddle), Melancholic and epic.

《苍穹牧歌·AI马背回响》
—— 当长生天的风遇见数字算法，唤醒草原千年的自由之魂。

穿越千里草原，AI重构蒙古族千年音律。马头琴低吟如诉，呼麦震颤穿透灵魂，长调悠远直击苍穹。这不是简单的音符堆砌，而是一场数字时代的草原朝圣。闭上眼，在算法编织的敖包前，听见风与马的对话。让游牧文明的浪漫与苍凉，唤醒都市人久违的自然共鸣。

是一张融合蒙古族传统音乐元素与现代中文流行音乐的跨界专辑。专辑以广袤的内蒙古草原为背景，通过马头琴的悠扬、呼麦的神秘、长调的辽阔，结合现代编曲技术，讲述草原儿女的乡愁、爱情、自由与传承。三首歌曲分别代表"故乡"、"爱情"、"自由"三个情感维度，带领听众穿越时空，感受草原文化的深厚底蕴与现代魅力。

《山音记》是一个以保护、维維、传承与创新为核心的音乐频道，许诚打造一个赏听中国各地小体、小群体、小文化的音乐空间。《山音记》基于深度研究和音乐创作，将中国多次元小体文化与环境音效，画面美学和轻享之音融为一体，有效地进行传统与环境之间的跨时代对话。

《山音记》的创作方向包括：

1. 民族乐器的光荣重现：这里没有虚假的电子效果，也没有充满市场化的便便音，有的是商朝雄鸿的笠箫、藏族厚重的唐鼓、壮族美妙的竹笛、蒙古勇敢的马头琴……每一种乐器，都被让保持他自然的声色和环境特征，不经过分分合合的弄造，保留了大自然舍时有时的动态精神。

2. 小体文化与Lo-fi混合类：美术的连续性，体环与时间的融合，为Lo-fi的音乐本身起到了新的规范作用。《山音记》成功地把小体民族乐器成功融入到Lo-fi音色中，使之不再是一种仅仅热感情的民族环境演奏，而是作为有节奏的国风时代声音，拉远了中国传统音乐与环境音乐之间的距离。

《山音记》是一个以保护、维維、传承与创新为核心的音乐频道，许诚打造一个赏听中国各地小体、小群体、小文化的音乐空间。《山音记》基于深度研究和音乐创作，将中国多次元小体文化与环境音效，画面美学和轻享之音融为一体，有效地进行传统与环境之间的跨时代对话。

《山音记》的创作方向包括：

1. 民族乐器的光荣重现：这里没有虚假的电子效果，也没有充满市场化的便便音，有的是商朝雄鸿的笠箫、藏族厚重的唐鼓、壮族美妙的竹笛、蒙古勇敢的马头琴……每一种乐器，都被让保持他自然的声色和环境特征，不经过分分合合的弄造，保留了大自然舍时有时的动态精神。

2. 小体文化与Lo-fi混合类：美术的连续性，体环与时间的融合，为Lo-fi的音乐本身起到了新的规范作用。《山音记》成功地把小体民族乐器成功融入到Lo-fi音色中，使之不再是一种仅仅热感情的民族环境演奏，而是作为有节奏的国风时代声音，拉远了中国传统音乐与环境音乐之间的距离。

Credited with single-handedly introducing the outside world to the boundless wealth of Tuvan traditions, Huun Huur Tu has spent decades honing overtone singing, instrumental approaches, and the vibrant songs of Central Siberia’s Altai Mountains. We deeply recommend the fotobook: TERRA INCOGNITO TUVA a book with the history of Tuva, interviews and a CD and DVD from a Germany TV production:


Are profoundly mysterious: the traditional, ritual laryngeal (throat) chants of the Central Asian land of Tuva. Their unique song technique creates an enthralling soundscape rich in undertones and overtones. Something that is no longer a mystery, however, is the world-wide success of the Tuvan quartet HUUN-HUUR-TU, now an established name in international world music, thanks to their modern presentation of traditional laryngeal (throat) singing.
In 2020, a book about Tuva “Terra Incognita Tuva” was published by Ulrich Balss at Jaro Media (ISBN 98-3-9813509-6-8 / JARO  4351-2), it’s a book about Tuva background on the music, shamans, nature, culture and interviews with Radik Tylysh from band as well an exclusive new Live CD recorded in Irland in 2019.

穿越千里草原，AI重构蒙古族千年音律。马头琴低吟如诉，呼麦震颤穿透灵魂，长调悠远直击苍穹。这不是简单的音符堆砌，而是一场数字时代的草原朝圣。闭上眼，在算法编织的敖包前，听见风与马的对话。让游牧文明的浪漫与苍凉，唤醒都市人久违的自然共鸣。

是一张融合蒙古族传统音乐元素与现代中文流行音乐的跨界专辑。专辑以广袤的内蒙古草原为背景，通过马头琴的悠扬、呼麦的神秘、长调的辽阔，结合现代编曲技术，讲述草原儿女的乡愁、爱情、自由与传承。三首歌曲分别代表"故乡"、"爱情"、"自由"三个情感维度，带领听众穿越时空，感受草原文化的深厚底蕴与现代魅力。

《山音记》是一个以保护、维維、传承与创新为核心的音乐频道，许诚打造一个赏听中国各地小体、小群体、小文化的音乐空间。《山音记》基于深度研究和音乐创作，将中国多次元小体文化与环境音效，画面美学和轻享之音融为一体，有效地进行传统与环境之间的跨时代对话。

《山音记》的创作方向包括：

1. 民族乐器的光荣重现：这里没有虚假的电子效果，也没有充满市场化的便便音，有的是商朝雄鸿的笠箫、藏族厚重的唐鼓、壮族美妙的竹笛、蒙古勇敢的马头琴……每一种乐器，都被让保持他自然的声色和环境特征，不经过分分合合的弄造，保留了大自然舍时有时的动态精神。

2. 小体文化与Lo-fi混合类：美术的连续性，体环与时间的融合，为Lo-fi的音乐本身起到了新的规范作用。《山音记》成功地把小体民族乐器成功融入到Lo-fi音色中，使之不再是一种仅仅热感情的民族环境演奏，而是作为有节奏的国风时代声音，拉远了中国传统音乐与环境音乐之间的距离。

title: Concerto HUUN-HUUR-TU, musiche tradizionali della Tuva - Catania Jazz, 31 luglio 2017
description:
Formazione:
Kaigall-Ool Khovalyg - voce (khöömei, sygyt, kargyraa), igylRadik Tyulyush - voce (barbang-nadyr), byzaanchi, khomuz (jew’s harp)Alexey Saryglar - voce, tungur, igylSayan Bapa - voce, toschpulur, chitarra, igyl

La musica degli Huun-Huur-Tu può venire descritta solo come profondamente misteriosa. Ciò come diretta conseguenza del loro stile di canto armonico, figlio di una tradizione secolare che discende dalla repubblica di Tuva, nell’Asia centrale.Una tecnica nella quale il cantante, sfruttando le risonanze che si creano nel tratto tra le corde vocali e la bocca, emette contemporaneamente la nota e l’armonico relativo (detto anche ipertono). Questa tecnica (in inglese throat singing) permette lo sviluppo di un universo del suono unico e coinvolgente, ricco di armonici sopra e sotto la frequenza fondamentale. Un altro elemento peculiare del gruppo è l’utilizzo di strumenti tradizionali come l’igil, il byzaanchi, il khommuz, il doshpuluur e il tuyug.
I membri di Huun-Huur-Tu si sono dedicati all’apprendimento di canzoni e di melodie antiche, ma allo stesso tempo le loro performance trovano risonanza anche nel mondo contemporaneo. Il percuotere ripetuto di una corda contro legno e pelle si trasforma in un disegno meditativo che sembra venire direttamente dall’avanguardia.I discendenti di isolati pastori siberiani estraggono una musica stranamente universale da alcuni dei fenomeni acustici più singolari di questo pianeta.A partire dai loro primi tour in Occidente, gli Huun-Huur-Tu sono stati quasi gli unici a introdurre al mondo esterno la ricchezza sconfinata delle tradizioni di Tuva, in particolare grazie alle loro superiori abilità musicali.
Provenienti dai pascoli dei monti Altai nel sud della Siberia centrale, sono musicisti che hanno trascorso decenni a perfezionare il canto armonico, gli approcci strumentali e le canzoni vibranti della loro madre patria.
Vestito in abiti tradizionali, l’ensemble si accompagna con strumenti a corda e percussioni, i cui ritmi sembrano evocare cavalcate nella steppa siberiana. I loro pezzi strettamente strutturati spesso imitano suoni naturali, di modo che una canzone può costituire una rappresentazione letterale di un paesaggio di Tuva.
Accompagnandosi con strumenti a corda pizzicati e ad arco, percussioni, e scacciapensieri, emulano i ritmi biologici nelle canzoni: il battito cardiaco, la respirazione, una mente alla deriva nel mondo dei sogni, e non meno importante (per una popolazione nomade), il trotto di un cavallo.Le loro canzoni parlano di amore romantico, dell’amore per la propria terra, e (non da ultimo) dei cavalli, con stati d’animo che vanno dal lirico e riflessivo al gioioso al divertente e ballabile“.
Gli Huun-Huur- Tu sono stati fondati nel 1992 da Sasha Bapa, da suo fratello Sayan, e altri due musicisti, Kaigal-Ool Khovalyg e Albert Kuvezin, con l’intento di concentrarsi su “canzoni vecchie e dimenticate”, come amava dire Sasha. Sasha, Sayan e Kaigal-ool erano fuoriusciti da uno dei più grandi gruppi di canto e danza statali che erano diventati un’istituzione fissa della vita culturale pubblica durante l’era sovietica. Per decenni questi ensemble con le loro prestazioni scintillanti di musica folk o pseudo musica popolare avevano offerto l’unica possibilità per dei giovani musicisti di suonare musica indigena per potersi mantenere. Dopo la privatizzazione degli affari musicali nell’ex Unione Sovietica, molti musicisti decisero di abbandonare questi ensemble statali e di formare i propri gruppi, con risultati musicali decisamente misti.
In un’intervista al produttore e critico americano Ted Levin, Sasha Il canto armonico della band consiste nel produrre un suono profondo in modo tale da creare una o due armoniche sostanziali. La prima armonica è un tono cantato nelle gamme medie,sovrastato da un suono come di fischio forte che il cantante alza o abbassa per creare una sorta di strana melodia, modificando l’apertura della bocca. Sotto questa luce ‘Huun-Huur- Tu possono effettivamente essere visti come una forza trainante nella divulgazione del canto armonico (o khoomei) negli ultimi decenni.
Tuttavia sarebbe un errore attribuire loro la nomea di solamente un gruppo folcloristico. Dal loro esordio per l’etichetta americana Shanachie nel 1993, hanno coperto molteplici mondi musicali. Nel 2002 un remix del loro brano “Eki Attar” è arrivato addirittura primo in classifica in Grecia, e nello stesso anno, il cd “Spirits of Tuva” per la tedesca Jaro li vide remixati da dj di varie nazionalità. Hanno inciso con Kronos Quartet, il percussionista indiano Trilok Gurtu, il compositore classico russo Vladimir Martynov, il coro The Bulgarian Voices Angelite, il trio jazz Moscow Art Trio,la cantante di Tuva Sainkho, e con Ry Cooder hanno composto sei brani per la colonna sonora di “Geronimo” di Walter Hill. Si sono esibiti inoltre assieme a Frank Zappa, The Chieftains, Johnny “Guitar” Watson e L. Shankar tra gli altri.

title: Huun-Huur-Tu with 'Lament from an Orphan' (live in 1995)
description:
When the group Huun-Huur-Tu played in our studio in 19…throat-singing was quite new to us. For sure, with this 'Lament from an Orphan' (traditional) this mysterious trio hypnotised us. In an interview Sasha Bapa, one of the founders, explained the meaning of ‘Huun-Huur-Tu’ as the vertical separation of light rays that one often see out on the grasslands just after sunrise or just before sunset. It seems to be a metaphor for the band’s key element– throat-singing that “consists of producing a deep tone in such a way as to create one or two substantial harmonics. 

The first harmonic is a humming sound in the mid-range, and on top is a loud whistling tone that the singer raises and lowers to create a weird sort of melody by varying the embouchure” However rooted in Tuvan traditions, it would be a mistake to attribute Huun-Huur-Tu to a folk ensemble. For the first time, Huun-Huur-Tu laced in the pop charts with a remix of the title “Eki Attar”. It became Greece’s No.1 hit in the summer of 2002. The ensemble then went on to release a studio project entitled ‘Spirits of Tuva’ with Djs of various nationalities. They have performed with Ry Cooder, Frank Zappa, The Chieftains, Johnny “Guitar” Watson, Kronos Quartet and L. Shankar among the others.

title: Dragon Boat Races Celebrate China's Ancient Past | National Geographic
description:
Near the summer solstice, communities across China gather to race boats, many adorned with painted carvings of dragons.
➡ Subscribe: https://on.natgeo.com/4p5A0D6

It’s clear to see how Duanwu Jie, China’s summer solstice holiday, got its English-language name—the Dragon Boat Festival. Around the fifth day of the fifth month on the traditional Chinese calendar, people gather to race boats adorned with brightly-painted carvings of dragons. Designs and types of contest vary widely. Some races are intense athletic challenges, while other events are more of a community get-together. Stories link the festival with the poet Qu Yuan, born in the late fourth century B.C. Said to be an able minister of the Chu state who was banished from the court, Qu Yuan despaired at his rivals’ corruption, and at last drowned himself in a river. By legend, an ensuing search for Qu by boat inspired the day’s races.

title: Festive China: Dragon Boat Festival
description:
The #DragonBoatFestival is the fifth day of the fifth month on the Chinese lunar calendar.

There are many legends about the 2,000-year-old festival, but the most famous one is commemorating the death of #QuYuan, a patriotic poet from the State of Chu during the Warring States Period (475-221 BC).

Qu Yuan is said to have been loyal and patriotic his whole life. When he realized the decline of Chu was beyond recovery, his remorse knowing he could no longer save it grew stronger and stronger. On the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, he threw himself into the river and died for his beloved homeland.   
 
Watch this video to learn more about the legend, as well as traditions and festive foods related to the festival. 

Festive China is a series of short clips focusing on traditional Chinese festivals and festivities, the cultural connotations of traditional holidays, their development and changes, and how they manifest in today's China.

From zongzi wrapped in leaves to mugwort hung on doors, from five-color threads for children to the story of poet Qu Yuan, the festival carries layers of memory, protection, grief, family and survival.

In this episode of Unboxing China, we open the box and unpack what Dragon Boat Festival actually means – and why it is still here.

title: We Talk: American vlogger dives into Dragon Boat Festival traditions
description:
📺 Subscribe to our YouTube channel and stay updated with our latest analysis and interviews:👇
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCgrNz-aDmcr2uuto8_DL2jg 👈

The Dragon Boat Festival is one of China's four major traditional festivals and the first Chinese festival to be inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. Believed to have originated in commemoration of Qu Yuan, a poet in ancient China known for his patriotism, the festival has been celebrated for more than 2,000 years. As the holiday approaches, a festive spirit fills Hubei Province, the birthplace of Qu Yuan and a region deeply rooted in Dragon Boat Festival traditions.

Marissa, an American vlogger who lives in Wuhan, Hubei Province, visited a local celebration to experience this centuries-old tradition firsthand. From the dragon boat races and delicious sticky rice dumplings to the vibrant riverside fair, she explored the cultural meaning behind the festivities. Along the way, she learned about the historical origins of dragon boat racing, discovered the enduring legacy of Qu Yuan, and witnessed how traditional Chinese culture continues to thrive and resonate in contemporary life.

Reflecting on her experience, Marissa said, "Whether it's racing dragon boats, sharing zongzi with family, or simply spending time together, these customs have been passed down through generations and remain an important part of Chinese culture today."

title: What’s a Zongzi? Dragon Boat Festival Explained
description:
Dragon Boat Festival is a summer holiday usually celebrated in early June. Zongzi, typically translated as rice dumplings, are a staple on the festival table. In Taiwan, these savory sticky rice snacks are stuffed with goodies like braised pork, mushrooms, chestnuts, or salted egg yolk. But there’s quite the debate when it comes to cooking. Northern style zongzi are stir-fried before being wrapped, then steamed. They're firmer, drier, and packed like a savory rice bento.
 
Meanwhile, down south, Southern style skip the stir-fry. The raw rice and filling go straight into the leaf wrapper, and then get boiled all together, creating a softer, almost gooey texture. What’s your favorite kind of zongzi? Sound off in the comments below. Remember to reply at your own risk!

The Dragon Boat Festival arrives with fragrant zongzi – steamed rice mixed with meat or dates, wrapped in bamboo or reed leaves – and racing boats. title: The Dragon Boat festival
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00225
Description: Beginning on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, people of several ethnic groups throughout China and the world celebrate the Dragon Boat festival, especially in the middle and lower reaches of the Yangtze River. The festivities vary from region to region, but they usually share several features. A memorial ceremony offering sacrifices to a local hero is combined with sporting events such as dragon races, dragon boating and willow shooting; feasts of rice dumplings, eggs and ruby sulphur wine; and folk entertainments including opera, song and unicorn dances. The hero who is celebrated varies by region: the romantic poet Qu Yuan is venerated in Hubei and Hunan Provinces, Wu Zixu (an old man said to have died while slaying a dragon in Guizhou Province) in South China, and Yan Hongwo in Yunnan Province among the Dai community. Participants also ward off evil during the festival by bathing in flower-scented water, wearing five-colour silk, hanging plants such as moxa and calamus over their doors, and pasting paper cut-outs in their windows. The Dragon Boat festival strengthens bonds within families and establishes a harmonious relationship between humanity and nature. It also encourages the expression of imagination and creativity, contributing to a vivid sense of cultural identity. 
Country(ies): China

title: Dragon Boat Festival Feast: Use natural containers like leaves and bamboo to create delicious dishes
description:
※Click "cc" on the lower right menu to choose your subtitle language.

In Yunnan cuisine, there is a category of dishes that need to be wrapped in natural containers. Zongzi leaves and bamboo shoot husks are used to wrap zongzi, banana leaves are used for roasted dishes, lotus leaves can be used for lotus leaf chicken and sticky rice, and bamboo tubes can be used to steam and roast all kinds of food. The amazing thing is that whatever is used to wrap the food imparts its flavor to it, enhancing the richness and deliciousness of the dishes!

The day we had our meal, we were really lucky because there were flying ants everywhere. Actually, our family had postponed the meal for two days, but it was quite unfortunate that during the Dragon Boat Festival, there were flying ants every day. These insects are attracted to light and are quite common in the village during this season. Usually, when there are flying ants at home, we turn off all the lights. However, for filming, we couldn’t do that. I included a text warning in the video where the flying ants appear, so viewers can watch with caution. For us, flying ants are a good omen, indicating a bountiful harvest of wild mushrooms and plentiful rain. They are also edible insects. My family seemed quite happy about it. We're used to seeing them, so we don't find it uncomfortable. I also advised viewers in the video to skip this part if they don’t like it.

Finally, I wish everyone happiness and safety every day!

Hello, everyone! I am Dianxi Xiaoge, a Yunnan native. If you enjoy my videos, please keep subscribing to my channel. I'll continue to share my life and Yunnan-styled food in my uploads. Wish you have a happy life and enjoy tasty food every day

title: Qu Yuan and the Dragon Boat Festival Story | Twinkl USA
description:
The Dragon Boat Festival, also known as the Duanwu Festival, is a special annual event celebrated worldwide. 

To instantly download an unlimited library of high-quality teaching resources to go with this story, sign up for Twinkl today: https://www.twinkl.com/r/6aucl

In this video, we’ll explore the story behind the Dragon Boat Festival and what makes this ancient celebration, which began in China, so unique.

We’ll be introduced to Qu Yuan, a famous and loyal poet who was banished by King Huai of Chu. We’ll then learn about the sad events that took place, leading Qu Yuan to take his life. And people reacted when they heard the news about Qu Yuan.

There’s also an explanation why people race dragon boats, eat rice dumplings, and drink a certain wine during this event!

01:01 - Who is Qu Yuan?
01:42 - Why was Qu Yuan banished by King Huai of Chu?
02:19 - How did Qu Yuan feel about being banished?
02:39 - Tragedy for the State of Chu and Qu Yuan’s sad decision.

03:40 - The Dragon Boat Festival is an important traditional Chinese event.
03:59 - Why dragon boats, rice dumplings, and realgar wine are associated with the Dragon Boat Festival.

The Dragon Boat Festival is one of the oldest festivals, spanning a history of more than 2,000 years. It is an annual celebration to commemorate the life and death of Qu Yuan, as well as the attempt at saving him. In 2009, it was added to the UNESCO World Intangible Cultural Heritage List.

title: Living Heritage: Duanwu - Dragon Boat Festival
description:
Beating drums, synchronous strokes and an exhilarated crowd. Such spectacles of excitement are no rare scene across #China on #Duanwu, or #DragonBoatFestival, which falls on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month.

Although tales of its origin differ, the two-millennia-old festival is widely celebrated in memory of Qu Yuan, a patriotic poet during the Warring States Period (475-221 BC). When Qu realized his country's decline was beyond recovery, his remorse grew so strong that he committed suicide, on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month. Legend has it that locals rowed boats to save the poet and threw zongzi, or sticky rice dumplings, into the river to keep fish from devouring his body. This tradition was passed down in China, even travelling across borders, carrying China’s respect for a patriot to all parts of the world.

A host of other practices are also associated with Duanwu. The fifth day of the fifth month is considered unlucky and poisonous. To rid themselves of the misfortune, many people, especially those in South China, take herbal baths, hang moxa and calamus plants over their doors and wear sachets stuffed with Chinese herbs, all with strong scents, to do away with venomous insects. Realgar wine, a common antidote against venom, is often enjoyed along with meals. Five-colored thread bracelets are also worn to obviate evil spirits. 

Duanwu began as a day to remember a poet steeped with love for his country. His patriotism is still celebrated today, with rich and fun customs that unite hearts across the globe, in methods that hail to the harmony between nature and man.

《The Story of Dragon Boat Festival》端午节的故事#Chinese Traditional Stories#Children's education #Enlightenment #Parent-child reading

Do you know why people eat zongzi and race dragon boats during the Dragon Boat Festival? What magical secrets are hidden inside the tiny zongzi? And why do even little birds stop to watch the dragon boat races? Come travel through time with Lele to meet a great poet named Qu Yuan and discover many beautiful stories behind the Dragon Boat Festival! The sweet smell of zongzi, the booming drumbeats, and the colorful little sachets are all full of love and good wishes! Come listen to the full story — it will make you smile and touch your heart!

The background music of this video is sourced from the "YouTube Music Library" and "Love Giving Network - Commercially Available (including CC0/Public Copyright)"

📌 Summary
The Dragon Boat Festival (端午节) is here — but what does it really mean?
In this video, we explore the history behind the festival, the story of the poet Qu Yuan, and of course, the delicious 粽子 (zòngzi) — the sticky rice dumplings that make this festival so special.
We’ll also take a look at the vibrant dragon boat races and why they’re so important in Chinese culture.

Why watch❓ 
If this is your first view—hi, I’m Jerry! I spent six years helping students from all over the world to start using Mandarin, regardless of their level. Today a lot of people focus on "what to learn": words, grammar; but never learn "how to learn." Language isn’t a subject to study but a tool to use. This channel shows you how to start using Mandarin confidently. For anyone who wants to stop studying and start using it.

I hope this video helps you connect with the rich history of the Dragon Boat Festival and sparks your interest in Chinese traditions.🛶

title: Epic Dragon Boat Festival Stunt
description:
Experience the adrenaline-pumping tradition of pole grabbing and drifting during the Dragon Boat Festival! As the longboat charges full speed toward a massive wooden pole in the river, a fearless team member lunges forward to grip the pole while the boat executes a heart-stopping drift. This daring maneuver showcases not only cultural heritage but also incredible teamwork, strength, and precision.

🎥 Witness this rare spectacle that blends ancient tradition with the thrill of extreme sports.
🛶 Learn the story behind this high-risk festival highlight, and why it's one of the most anticipated events of the Dragon Boat Festival!

情境導言 (Hook & Intro)
一年一度的端午節到了！快跟著 Bunny 兔和小夥伴們一起划龍舟、吃粽子、學英文！這首專為幼兒設計的端午節主題字母歌，結合了 26 個有趣的節慶單字與 3D 動畫，讓孩子在歡樂的節奏中輕鬆啟蒙。
The annual Dragon Boat Festival is here! Join Bunny and friends for a rhythmic adventure of rowing dragon boats and eating Zongzi! This special ABC song combines 26 festive words with vibrant 3D animation, making English learning fun and immersive for toddlers.

學習重點 (Learning Highlights & Timeline)
在本集影片中，小朋友將會學到：

🐲 端午習俗：龍舟競賽 (Dragon Boat)、立蛋 (Egg Balancing) 與掛艾草。
📝 26 個字母單詞：從 A (Amulet) 到 Z (Zongzi) 的完整節慶詞彙。

title: Experiencing China’s Dragon Boat Festival for the First Time 🇨🇳
description:
We travelled to China just in time for the Dragon Boat Festival – and what we experienced was completely unexpected. From intense boat races to traditional food like zongzi, and full-blown street celebrations, this is one of the wildest festivals we’ve ever witnessed. With help from some incredible locals, we got a front-row seat to the chaos, culture, and energy of one of China’s biggest holidays.

This was our first time attending the Dragon Boat Festival, and let’s just say… we had no idea what we were in for 🇨🇳

title: Let's make zongzi, the traditional food of Dragon Boat Festival
description:
Duanwujie or Dragon Boat Festival – a traditional celebration first observed over 2,000 years ago in China – falls on the 5th day of the the 5th month of the Chinese Lunar Calendar, which is June 25 this year, and that means you need to eat the delicacy: zongzi.

You may recognize zongzi by their pyramid shape and bamboo leaf wrapping. They seem like a pretty simple food, with only a few ingredients and relatively simple preparation, but just how easy are they to make? And why do Chinese people eat zongzi during the festival?

Andy Boreham, a New Zealander living in Shanghai, headed out to one of Shanghai's ancient towns and sat down with a local zongzi maker to try for himself.

Check out Andy's vlog as he tries his best to make a pork zongzi, just in time for the Dragon Boat Festival.

title: The Tragic Hero Behind the Dragon Boat Festival | The Story of Qu Yuan
description:
Every year, millions celebrate the Dragon Boat Festival with races and rice dumplings—but how many truly know the heartbreaking legend behind it?

Over 2,000 years ago, during China's Warring States period, a loyal minister and gifted poet named Qu Yuan stood alone against corruption and decay. Exiled for speaking the truth, he wandered the land in sorrow, pouring his grief and ideals into poetry that would echo through history.

When his homeland fell, Qu Yuan chose to die with honor rather than live in disgrace. His final act—drowning in the Miluo River—sparked generations of remembrance.

This video tells the story of a man whose words still stir the soul:
"Though I may die a thousand times, I will never regret my choice."
and
"The road ahead is long and has no ending; yet high and low I’ll search with my will unbending."

Discover the true meaning of the Dragon Boat Festival—not just a tradition, but a tribute to loyalty, sacrifice, and the unyielding spirit of a nation. #DragonBoatFestival #QuYuan #ChineseHistory #AncientChina #WarringStates #TraditionalFestivals #EastAsianCulture #PoetryAndPatriotism #CulturalHeritage #LegendsOfChina #MiluoRiver #Zongzi #DragonBoatRace #HistoryExplained #UnsungHeroes

title: Making Zongzi for Dragon Boat Festival! (Full Recipe Video is on my Channel) 🐲 #shorts
description:
Dragon boat festival is almost here! If you’re looking for a zongzi / joong recipe that’s loaded with the goods, check out my full recipe video on my channel😋

Making sticky rice dumplings can be labor intensive but here are some tips to make the process smoother: 
2. To prevent the leaves from ripping, make sure the leaves are soaked 30 mins or more before boiling. This allows you to bend the leaves without tearing when placing them in the pot. Also buy high quality leaves, the one featured in my video is a good tried & true brand. 
3. Save time by cooking your peanuts in the pressure cooker vs stove top. It cuts the time from 45 mins to 15mins.
4. Wrap with the shiny side of the leaves facing in and matte side outwards. This prevents the leaves from sticking after it’s boiled, which makes it harder to peel. 
5. In the last step of wrapping (before the string) make sure to give it a few taps all around for the ingredients to fill in the empty spaces. 
6. When it’s time to cook your zongzi, fill your pot with cold water not hot and let it come to a boil all together. This allows it to cook thoroughly resulting in a better texture. 

Happy Zongzi Wrapping!

description:
Guangdong came alive with colour and tradition as the Dragon Boat Festival was celebrated with high-energy boat races and festive zongzi feasts. The festival, rooted in Chinese heritage, blends sport, food, and community spirit, making it one of the most vibrant annual celebrations in the region. The event showcased both cultural pride and festive unity.

title: Dragon Boat Race 2022 |Aberdeen Hong Kong
description:
As per the legend, Tuen Ng Festival or also popularly known as the dragon boat festival originated from the death of a Chinese patriotic poet Qu Yuan,  in the Warring States period

He waded into a river to drown himself and died soon after his beloved homeland’s sovereignty on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month when the Dragon Boat Festival is now held. Qu’s body could not be found, so local people raced out in their boats and threw sticky rice balls into the river to keep fish and evil spirits away from the poet’s body. 

To commemorate the Chinese poet, this day is now turned into a dragon boat festival day.

Due to Covid in 2022, some of the races in the city were postponed from June (when the festival was actually held), so this race was shot on 2nd October 2022 in Aberdeen near the promenade.

title: Bamboo Tube Zongzi Tradition ✨ Dragon Boat Festival Flavors Wrapped in Heritage
description:
✦ Title:
🎋 Bamboo Tube Zongzi Tradition ✨ Dragon Boat Festival Flavors Wrapped in Heritage

✦ Description:
🌿 As the Dragon Boat Festival draws near, the refreshing scent of bamboo fills the air, blending with the rich aroma of freshly prepared zongzi. This cherished seasonal tradition brings together family, culture, and generations of culinary craftsmanship.

🎋 Bamboo tube zongzi is a unique variation of this beloved festival food, where fragrant sticky rice is carefully prepared and cooked within natural bamboo. The result is a distinctive flavor infused with the essence of nature and traditional cooking wisdom.

🥢 Rooted in centuries of heritage, the art of making zongzi reflects patience, skill, and the enduring spirit of rural food culture. From selecting ingredients to the final steaming process, every step celebrates the beauty of seasonal traditions and handmade cuisine.

✨ Whether you're passionate about traditional foods, cultural festivals, countryside life, or authentic cooking techniques, this video offers a delightful journey into one of Asia's most treasured culinary customs.

📌 Follow for more traditional recipes, rural food stories, seasonal festivals, and heritage cooking techniques from around the world.

title: ONCE UPON A DRAGON BOAT: 50 Years, 3 Generations in Hong Kong | 龍舟三代🐲半世紀熱血傳承
description:
It all began 50 years ago at the first-ever Hong Kong International Dragon Boat Races at Shau Kei Wan Typhoon Shelter🚣‍♀️. What started as a fishermen’s pastime has since grown into a world-class event, including a vibrant festival packed with food, drinks and fun. 

What was the very first race like? How intense was it to compete against international teams? And what moments have stood the test of time? Hear it firsthand from three generations of paddlers – 👴🏼Wong Kan-chai, paddler at the first Hong Kong International Dragon Boat Races; 👨🏻Chan Kim-ming, who has taken part in the races for 19 consecutive years; and 👧🏻Christy Tai, a younger racer who discovered the sport just three years ago.

Sun Life Hong Kong International Dragon Boat Races
🗓️ 27–28 June 
⏰8am – 6:15pm (Saturday); 8am – 5pm (Sunday)
*Event times are subject to change without prior notice
📍Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade, Kowloon
🎟️Free admission
👀Don’t miss the 50th Anniversary Fishermen Invitational Cup, where six local fishermen teams race in traditional wooden dragon boats!

More than just races, the Sun Life Hong Kong International Dragon Boat Festival runs from 19 June to 1 July with even more exciting events. Find out more: https://discoverhongkong.link/dragon-boat-2026

description:
💡Do You Know?
1. 你知道華人三大重要節日是什麼嗎?沒錯！就是農曆新年Chinese New Year、中秋節 Moon Festival 和今天介紹的端午節 Dragon-boat Festival。
2. 龍舟比賽不只在端午節才看的到囉！划龍舟已經是亞洲運動會的比賽項目。
　另外，在澳洲、加拿大、日本也舉辦過世界龍舟錦標賽。
3. 端午節一定會吃的粽子 rice dumpling，是用糯米 sticky rice 做成的。
　吃太多很容易會消化不良，小朋友要多注意囉！
4. 在防蚊液發明以前，人們會用香包 sachet來驅趕蚊蟲、避免蚊子叮咬。

title: Dragon boat festival brings summer excitement in Luxembourg
description:
Paddles up, spirits high!

The Moselle River came alive with excitement on June 7 as thousands of spectators gathered in Remich for the fourth Luxembourg International Dragon Boat Festival. Teams powered across the water to the beat of drums, while cheering crowds turned the riverfront into a festival of energy and color.

The celebration extended well beyond the races. Chinese folk music, martial arts performances, international bands, and DJs kept the atmosphere buzzing throughout the day. Visitors also explored cultural exhibitions, hands-on experiences, tourism showcases, and a tempting mix of Asian and local cuisine.

Blending tradition, sport, and cultural exchange, the festival offered a vibrant taste of the Dragon Boat Festival in the heart of Europe.

(Video source: Chinese Cultural Center in Luxembourg)

title: Making Zongzi on Dragon Boat Festival
description:
Hi~This is Huamuz’s only channel on YouTube. The videos are all self-produced,and all other accounts are for moving.Thank you for your support.
Wearing beautiful clothes to make traditional cuisines and handicrafts, and living my dream rural life, has always been something I love and pursue.
Each task is quiet complex,so it may take some time to complete.Thank you for your patience.
If you like my channel, don't forget to subscribe ～^_^
Thank you for your attention. I look forward to  continue to bring you peace and surprises～💐🩷😘🎊💜
——————————————————
🦋 Description：

The annual Dragon Boat Festival has arrived again. On this special occasion, we pick wormwood and prepare all kinds of ingredients: glutinous rice, termite mushrooms, meat, lotus seeds, pine nut kernels, salt and seasonings. We wrap them up neatly and steam them to make delicious zongzi.

Zongzi is a traditional folk delicacy. Every Dragon Boat Festival, people hang wormwood at the door for good luck. Relatives and friends gather to enjoy zongzi, drink realgar wine, exchange sachets as gifts, and watch dragon boat races together. These age-old customs have been passed down for thousands of years. Back in my hometown, this festival remains one of the most important days of the year.

title: Dragon Boat Festival draws hundreds to The Market Common for race and community fun
description:
On Saturday, the 17th annual Ground Zero Dragon Boat Festival at The Market Common took place.

Dozens of boats with 20 paddlers and one drummer each raced in front of a crowd today seeing who could cross the finish line the fastest.

Hundreds of people watched and cheered on the teams alongside food, vendors and live music.

This Dragon Boat race raises money for Ground Zero, an organization providing faith, life changing experiences and positive relationships to youth.

title: DIY Clay Art Zongzi Tutorial | CALIBAO Dragon Boat Festival Kit
description:
Welcome to your CALIBAO DIY Clay Art Zongzi tutorial!

In this step-by-step video, we'll show you one way to create your clay art zongzi using the materials included in your Dragon Boat Festival Kit.

This tutorial is meant to be a guide and source of inspiration. The best part of this activity is that there is no right or wrong way to do it — let your creativity take the lead! Feel free to experiment with different colors, shapes, patterns, and expressions to create a zongzi that is uniquely yours.

As you build, you'll also discover one of the Dragon Boat Festival's most beloved traditions through hands-on cultural play.

Pause anytime and follow along at your own pace.

title: Keeping Dragon Boat Festival's traditions alive
description:
This year's Dragon Boat Festival is celebrated on the fifth day of the fifth month of the Chinese lunar calendar. People across China mark the occasion by eating the traditional snack known as Zongzi and drinking a type of Chinese wine. But most noticeably, dragon boat races are held across the country and even abroad, adding to the festive atmosphere. The Dragon Boat Festival has long been a time for Chinese to practice many ancient traditions, and it is fast becoming a cultural icon for people to commemorate the poet Qu Yuan and his poetry. 

But can this festival stay true to its roots as the athletic dragon boat races rise in prominence on the world stage and are viewed by some as just another sporting endeavor? We turn to our panel to find out: Han Hua, media and communication consultant; and David Moser, academic director at the Capital Normal University.

title: Market for zongzi picks up as Dragon Boat Festival approaches｜Taiwan News
description:
Bilingual text here: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2026604W07EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description
雙語文字版: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2026604W07EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description

Our official website: ➔ https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/

Follow us on
Facebook: ➔ https://www.facebook.com/ftvformosanews
X (Twitter): ➔ https://x.com/FTV_Taiwan_News
Dragon Boat Festival is still a few weeks away, but zongzi are already selling fast. These sticky rice dumplings, usually filled with meat, are the festival’s signature food. One traditional zongzi store in Changhua’s Lukang Township already received an order of 400, while the Changhua Fisherman’s Association’s eel zongzi are also rising to the challenge.   

There is so many flavors! Which one to pick? You gotta buy ’em all.

- It’s delicious. The ingredients are fresh. 

- There ae lots of thin bamboo shoots, and lots of mushrooms too. 

- I bought 30. I’ve had these ones for over 40 years. My hair has turned from black to white. 

This sticky rice dumpling is packed with filling, including bamboo shoots, pork, mushrooms, and egg yolk. These famous zongzi bring smiles to people’s faces after one bite. 

- The owner is really thoughtful, she never raises the price.  

This zongzi stand in Changhua’s Lukang Township has been running for 54 years. Even as the cost of ingredients has risen these past few years, its zongzi have remained the same price for the last three. This year, one company has already ordered 400. 

Ms. Hsiu-chen
Zongzi stand owner
I haven’t raised my prices in three years. I make about NT$5 on each zongzi, earning slowly but benefitting the customers. We usually sell over 30,000 leading up to the Dragon Boat Festival, it’s the same every year. 

The Changhua Fisherman’s Association is selling their very own braised eel zongzi, which is popular among people who like seafood. Its price also remains unchanged.  

Chen Wei-ku
Changhua Fisherman’s Association director
This year the Changhua Fisherman’s Association froze the price, giving it cheaply to our customers. 

With both traditional stands and the fisherman’s association freezing the price of their zongzi, this year’s market is sure to be competitive leading up to the Dragon Boat Festival.

title: Dragon Boat Festival’s Hidden Dish: Hundred‑Herb‑Root Chicken Stew – A Healing Tradition 🌿🍲 #food
description:
Beyond zongzi, there's another Dragon Boat Festival tradition in Yunnan's Kas Town: chicken stewed with a hundred wild herbal roots. Following the philosophy of "food as medicine", families gather over twenty types of mountain herbs – Yangtan grass, peony root, chicken leg root, and more – and slow‑simmer them with free‑range chicken and Lashan ham. The result is a golden, fragrant broth that's savory, slightly bitter at first, then sweet. It's said to detoxify, boost qi, and warm the body – a true taste of home and heritage. In this short film, we follow the process from market to table, and explore why locals wait all year for this seasonal delicacy.

In this video, you'll see:
✅ How to select and prepare over 20 wild medicinal herbs
✅ The traditional cooking method – ham first, then chicken, then herbs
✅ The philosophy of "food as medicine" passed down through generations
✅ The unique flavor: savory, bitter, sweet, and deeply comforting

👇 Would you try a soup made with wild herbs and chicken? What's your favorite Dragon Boat Festival food?

🔔 Love food traditions with deep cultural roots? Subscribe for more seasonal stories.

The Amazon Dragons Paddling Club raced two dragon boats in the Seattle Dragon Boat Festival, an event hosted by the Seattle Flying Dragons. This race took place at Lake Union Park in Seattle, Washington, on June 13th, 2026. Amazon Warriors took 2nd place in Women's B Division, while Amazon Rebels took 2nd place in Women's A Division.

The Amazon Dragons Paddling Club is an organization which provides lesbians and queer women with camaraderie, fitness, and physical strength through competitive dragon boat racing. Dragon boat racing has been a traditional Chinese paddled watercraft activity for over 2000 years and began as a modern international sport in Hong Kong in 1976.

Dragon boats are racing across China as communities prepare for the Dragon Boat Festival on June 19. From the rivers of Chongqing in Southwest China to Guangzhou in South China, hundreds of paddlers and cheering spectators are keeping alive a tradition that dates back more than 2,000 years.

Recognized by UNESCO as part of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, the Dragon Boat Festival is celebrated with thrilling races, zongzi feasts, and customs honoring the ancient poet Qu Yuan.

title: 世界大小知识 World Fun Facts #48 | 端午节 Dragon Boat Festival
description:
The Dragon Boat Festival is one of the most important traditional festivals celebrated by Chinese communities around the world. Held on the fifth day of the fifth month of the Chinese lunar calendar, it is known for dragon boat races, delicious zongzi, and the story of the ancient poet Qu Yuan.
In this episode of World Fun Facts, discover the origins of the Dragon Boat Festival, the meaning behind its traditions, and how this ancient celebration continues to bring people together today.

端午节是华人社会最重要的传统节日之一。它在农历五月初五举行，以龙舟竞赛、美味粽子以及屈原的故事闻名。
在这一集《世界大小知识》中，一起了解端午节的由来、各种传统习俗背后的意义，以及这个古老节日如何流传至今并凝聚人们的情感。

The Dragon Boat Festival is a centuries-old celebration known for dragon boat races and zongzi.
It is closely connected to the story of Qu Yuan and the values of loyalty, remembrance, and community spirit.
Today, people around the world continue to celebrate this vibrant cultural tradition.

时至今日，世界各地的人们依然庆祝这一充满文化魅力的节日。

铭记前人的贡献，有助于传承历史与珍贵价值观。

The Singapore Dragon Boat Festival (SDBF) at Bedok Reservoir is Singapore's premier dragon boat racing event, serving as the national championship and a significant highlight in the local sporting calendar. Organized by the Singapore Dragon Boat Association (SDBA), it's a thrilling display of athleticism, teamwork, and tradition.

Most of the races at SDBF are conducted over a challenging 500-meter distance, requiring both explosive power and endurance.

The SDBF also hosts prestigious challenge championships, including the Prime Minister's Cups, adding an extra layer of prestige and competition.

Breast Cancer Survivors' Race: A special and inspiring race promoting inclusivity and support.

The SDBF serves as a crucial pre-requisite event for the IDBF Club Crew World Championship (CCWC). For 2026, the CCWC will take place in Hualien, Taiwan.

Venue: PAssion Wave @ Bedok Reservoir
Date: June 21 & 22, 2025

title: China Window, will organize the country's first-ever Dragon Boat Festival on June 14
description:
#news #pakistan #peshawar #dailykasoti #newspaper #entertainment #ptv #ISPR #governmentofpakistan 

Preparations for Dragon Boat Festival Enter Final Stage

Sardaryab, Charsadda: As part of the celebrations marking the 75th anniversary of Pak-China friendship, the Chinese cultural center China Window, based in Peshawar, will organize the country's first-ever Dragon Boat Festival on June 14 at Sardaryab on the River Kabul.
The district administration has finalized arrangements to ensure the success of the festival and the safety of all participants. Additional Deputy Commissioner Reena Abbasi visited the event venue and reviewed the preparations. She directed the concerned authorities to provide all possible facilities and support to the participants taking part in the boat race.
More than 100 paddlers and boat racers are expected to participate in this historic competition, making it a landmark event in promoting cultural exchange and friendship between Pakistan and China.

title: 龍舟競渡:醒氣提神的端午樂章 | Dragon Boat Festival: Rhythms of Spirit and Tradition
description:
The Dragon Boat Festival, also known as Duanwu, is one of China’s most important traditional festivals, with a history spanning over two thousand years. It honors the patriotic poet Qu Yuan and serves as a day to ward off evil spirits and disease. Dragon boat racing, the festival’s signature event, originated from ancient water rituals meant to drive away evil, and has evolved into a fierce and powerful competition symbolizing unity and courage.

This composition features Erhu, Pipa, Guzheng, Xiao, and drums, blending traditional Chinese instruments with dynamic rhythms to capture the spirit of dragon boat racing and the cultural essence of the festival. Let the rhythm awaken your senses — a melody inspired by a festival with over two millennia of tradition, uplifting both body and spirit.

端午節是中國傳統的重要節日，已有超過兩千年的歷史。這一天人們紀念詩人屈原的愛國精神，也祈願避邪驅疫。龍舟競渡是端午節最具代表性的習俗，源於古代水域的祭祀和趕走惡靈的儀式，後來演變為如今激烈而充滿力量的龍舟賽，象徵團結與勇氣。

本曲以二胡、琵琶、古箏、簫及龍船鼓等中國傳統樂器演繹，揉合節奏強勁的動感元素，展現龍舟競渡的氣勢與端午節的文化底蘊。讓節奏喚醒你的感官——這首曲子靈感源於擁有兩千多年傳承的節日，振奮身心靈。

I pickled Xue Cai and Meigan Cai in the spring. The method is not particularly complicated to make preserved vegetables (steamed three times and dried three time). Traditional delicacies are just like this, everything depends on time. 
Life goes leisurely, then summer is coming, bamboo shoots are growing, and the Dragon Boat Festival is here now. 
Everything is just right: Zongzi (Rice dumpling) with Meigan Cai and fresh meat, bamboo shoots, and Xue Cai, which are all eaten in one go.

title: 中国民俗--广东盐步龙舟Chinese Folk Custom - Saltbu Dragon Boat Festival in Guangdong
description:
#china #travel #trip #match#custom #guangdong 
盐步隶属佛山南海大沥，是岭南老牌龙舟圣地，盐步老龙为全国现存最古老古龙舟，2012 年「盐步老龙礼俗」列入广东省非物质文化遗产名录，被誉为珠三角龙舟界 “龙中长者”。
盐步老龙基本概况
始建于明宣德七年（1432 年），距今近 600 年历史，坤甸木整料打造，总长 36.8 米、68 个船位、船重约 4 吨，常年沉在河涌淤泥 “龙床” 保养，仅端午前后出水亮相。
外形独有标识：龙头长雪白龙须，全广东极少白须古龙船；船配五顶罗伞，供奉五显华光大帝；船上悬慈禧年间赐制百足大旗（长 16.2 米，缀雉鸡尾）。
规矩：百年不参赛竞渡，只做巡游游龙，各地龙舟碰面都要鸣炮行礼，尊称 “契爷老龙”。
传世典故：广佛龙船结契（契爷 & 契仔）
明万历二十五年（1597 年）珠江赛龙，盐步老龙和广州泮塘龙船队结下水族契亲：盐步老龙 = 契爷（干爹）、广州泮塘龙船 = 契仔（干儿子），传承 400 余年从未中断。
端午固定习俗：每年五月初五，盐步老龙沿水路横渡珠江，专程去广州荔湾泮塘探访契仔；泮塘全村龙舟列队河边，簪花挂红、鞭炮醒狮隆重接契爷，是广佛最经典跨界民俗。
全套非遗传统礼俗（端午完整流程）
起龙（农历四月中下旬择吉日）：全村摆烧猪、果品、祝文，非遗传承人主持祭龙仪式，村民下河舀水拉船，从淤泥中起出老龙，醒狮锣鼓助兴。
拜华光：老龙上岸祭拜祖庙华光大帝，点睛簪红，开光祈福。
游三河：巡游盐步河东、河中、河西三河，各村龙舟结伴随行。
探契仔（核心仪式）：顺水路南下广州泮塘探亲，泮塘簪花挂红赠礼。
迎契回访：节后泮塘龙船回访盐步。
龙船饭：仪式收尾全村同吃龙船饭，是盐步水乡团圆民俗。
Yanbu belongs to Dali, Nanhai District, Foshan City. It is an old and renowned dragon boat site in the Lingnan region. The old dragon boat in Yanbu is the oldest existing dragon boat in China. In 2012, "The Rituals of Yanbu Old Dragon Boat" was listed as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of Guangdong Province. It is hailed as the "elderly dragon" in the dragon boat world of the Pearl River Delta
General Overview of the Yanbu Old Dragon Boat
It was built in the seventh year of the reign of Emperor Xuande of the Ming Dynasty (1432), with a history of nearly 600 years. It is made of whole pieces of teak wood, with a total length of 36.8 meters, 68 boat positions, and a weight of approximately 4 tons. It is always submerged in the riverbed silt "dragon bed" for maintenance, and is only brought out for display during the Dragon Boat Festival.
Unique appearance features: The dragon head has long, snow-white whiskers, and there are very few white-haired ancient dragon boats in Guangdong Province; The boat is equipped with five parasols, dedicated to worshipping the Five Manifestations of Huaguang Great Emperor; There is a banner with a hundred-foot flag (16.2 meters long, with peacock tails) bestowed by Empress Chai during the Qianlong period.
Rules: It has not participated in competitive races for a hundred years and only performs a procession of the dragon. When dragons from different places meet, they will sound cannons and pay respects, respectfully calling it "Yi Zui Old Dragon".
Legendary stories: Guang-Fo Dragon Boat Alliance (Yi Zui & Yi Zai)
In 1597 of the Ming Dynasty, during the dragon boat race on the Pearl River, the Yanbu Old Dragon Boat and the Guangzhou Pan Tang Dragon Boat Team formed an aquatic alliance: Yanbu Old Dragon Boat = Yi Zui (Godfather), Guangzhou Pan Tang Dragon Boat = Yi Zai (Godson). This alliance has lasted for over 400 years without interruption.
Fixed customs on the Dragon Boat Festival: Every year on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, the Yanbu Old Dragon Boat crosses the Pearl River and specially visits the Pan Tang area in Liwan, Guangzhou to visit Yi Zai. The entire village of dragon boats lines up on the riverbank, wearing flowers and red clothes, with firecrackers, lion dances, and a grand ceremony to welcome Yi Zui. This is the most classic cross-border folk custom in Guang-Fo.
Complete traditional ritual for the Dragon Boat Festival (the complete process)
Lifting the Dragon (in the middle or late April of the lunar calendar): The entire village prepares a pig, fruits, and congratulatory texts. The非遗传承人 presides over the dragon worship ceremony， villagers scoop water and pull the boat from the mud， and the old dragon is lifted out of the mud. Lion drums and music add to the celebration.
Worshiping Huaguang： The old dragon is brought ashore to worship at the ancestral temple of Huaguang Great Emperor， has its eyes painted red， and is enlightened and prayed for.
Touring Three Rivers： It tours the east， middle， and west rivers of Yanbu. The dragon boats from each village follow along.
Visiting Yi Zai (core ceremony)： Traveling south along the river to visit Yi Zai in Pan Tang. Pan Tang lines up with flowers and red decorations， and gifts are given.
Receiving Yi Zui's Return： The Pan Tang dragon boats return to Yanbu after the festival.
Dragon Boat Feast： The ceremony concludes with the entire village sharing the dragon boat feast， which is a family reunion custom in the water village of Yanbu..

title: Sylvan Lake’s Ice Dragon Boat Festival 2023 | Ice Slide & Family Skate
description:
An Ice Dragon Boat Festival happened in Sylvan Lake this weekend. 20 teams of local people race large ice boats.  The dragon boats) long slender boats that are propped up on skates and they're propelled forward by paddles with spikes on the end. There are three different divisions of racing including women’s, mixed, and individual.  

This year’s Dragon Boat Festival also run in conjunction with the Family Skate and Sled that was postponed from it’s original date in January to Saturday, February 11. Both events happened around Sylvan Lake Park.  The festival also kicked—off the Pass-the-Poutine contest which will continue into next weekend’s Winterfest events.  #sylvanlakealberta #Sylvanlake #winter #dragonboat #draogonboatrace #icedragon

Thanks to #visitsylvanlake for some additional race footage, which was recorded by other Sylvan Lake residents, to give other perspectives , and the close up footage from the Ontario Ice dragon boat races.

title: China's Frozen Festival: Harbin Unveils Dragon Boat Festival Ice Spectacle | Vantage Shots
description:
Ahead of the Dragon Boat Festival, Harbin Ice and Snow World in northeast China has unveiled a vibrant new ice sculpture celebrating one of the country's most cherished traditions. Using brightly coloured natural ice, artists have recreated iconic festival scenes, including dragon boat races and the making of zongzi, or sticky rice dumplings. The dazzling display offers a unique blend of culture, craftsmanship and ice artistry, earning Harbin's latest attraction the nickname - China's Frozen Festival.

title: 《節慶小學堂 Festival Learning Fun：端午節 Dragon Boat Festival 》 雙語教學｜節慶認識｜故事分享｜趣味知識
description:
今天我們要來玩一個特別的單元，叫做《節慶小學堂：Festival Learning Fun》我們會用一些簡單的問題，帶大家一起來認識有趣的節日哦！

今天要教的節慶為《端午節 Dragon Boat Festival 》

在開始前，爸爸媽媽們可以先截圖有字卡那一頁，然後把字卡印出來，和小朋友一起學習哦！這些字卡可以收集起來，玩釣魚記憶遊戲以鍛鍊記憶力並增強孩子對單字的記憶能力！遊戲規則很簡單，如果不確定怎麼玩，爸爸媽媽可以參考以下說明：

準備：列印兩份字卡，並將字卡朝下隨機排列成矩陣（如4x4）。
規則：玩家輪流翻兩張字卡。如果翻出的字卡相同，拿走並繼續翻牌；如果不同，將其翻回去並記住位置。
結束：當所有字卡配對完成後，遊戲結束，擁有最多配對的玩家獲勝。

每次的《節慶小學堂：Festival Learning Fun》都會提供不同的字卡，收集越多，小朋友能學到的單字就越多！快來和我們一起邊玩邊快樂了的學習中英文吧！
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

title: Dragon Boat Festival of Miao people
description: #Guizhou Dragon is the symbol of auspicious and strength in MIao people’s heart.From May 24 to May 27 on the lunar calendar is the Drgon Boat Festival of Miao people which is the one of the most traditional festival both in Guizhou and China with hundres of years history.The dragon boat is very chic, generally dugged in one whole big fir or phoenix tree.The Miao people also preserve a unique,and excellent tradition.At the end of the competition,organisers usually let out a live duck and sailors rush to chase after the duck.It is very busy and interesting.During the Dragon Boat Festival,both sides of Qingshui River are full of people. In addition to the racing boat, people step on drums, blowing sheng and other folk activities,which is also a good date for young men and women.Dragon Boat Festival shows early Miao people's park in the faith and recounts the Miao people's early civilization of the development of traditional culture at the same time which was approved by the state council listed in the first batch of state-level non-material cultural heritage list

title: Dragon Boat Festival in Rural China｜端午節｜包粽子，掛艾草，感受中國傳統節日的慢生活
description:
Hanging mugwort: exorcise evil spirits and avoid poison, pray for good health
The special aroma of mugwort can repel mosquitoes, and hanging it on the eaves of the door symbolizes blocking the entry of diseases
Tie colorful ropes: ward off evil spirits and bring good luck, and live a long life
Also known as "longevity thread" or "five-color dragon", it means tying health and happiness.
Eating five yellows: strengthen physical fitness and replenish depleted vitality
Sprinkling realgar: disinfection and insect repellent, sprinkled in the corners can prevent snakes and ants
Five-color silk threads are wrapped for good luck, and the fragrance of zongzi fills the courtyard
Dragon Boat Festival, may your life be as colorful as this festival!

掛艾草：驅邪避毒，祈福安康
艾草特殊的香氣可以驅趕蚊蟲，掛在門簷上象徵阻擋病疫入門
繫五彩繩：辟邪納吉，長命百歲
又稱為“長命縷”或“五彩龍”，寓意繫住安康與幸福。
吃五黃：增強體質，補充消耗的元氣
撒雄黃：消毒防蟲，撒在牆角可以防蛇蟻
五色絲線纏吉祥，粽香四溢滿庭院
端午佳節，願你的生活如這節日一般豐富多彩！

title: 2026端午節前夕廣東南海鹽步舉行老龍起船出水儀式！ 全國現存最老龍舟，現場人山人海，熱鬧非凡！
description:
全國最老龍舟出水！ 2026端午前夕，看廣東南海鹽步老龍“喚醒”名場面！   🐉✨

端午將至，看嶺南硬核傳統！ 今天帶大家直擊廣東佛山南海鹽步的“老龍起船”儀式。

作為全國現存最古老的龍舟（擁有594年歷史的“鹽步老龍”），它每年只在端午前夕出水，重見天日！ 現場人山人海，鑼鼓喧天，鞭炮齊鳴。 村民們齊心協力挖開河泥，當沉睡一年的老龍破水而出的那一刻，全場沸騰，直接把端午過節的氣氛拉滿！
快來跟隨鏡頭，感受這場震撼視覺與心靈的非遺民俗盛宴，看中國傳統文化的生命力有多硬核！

Welcome to another high-octane installment of the Festival Fix live on the Jerome Forestwood channel! 🍿 Pull up a chair, grab a cold drink, and jump into the live chat as we bring you a front-row seat to the Vessi 500 Championship, broadcasting live from the sun-drenched Olympic Village seawall on False Creek! With over 130 elite teams and 3,000 powerhouse athletes hitting the water, the atmospheric energy out here is off the charts. We are tracking a relentless schedule of high-stakes race completions and synchronized paddle splashes crossing the finish line every single 15 minutes. Smash that like button, subscribe to join the Forestwood Family, and drop your predictions in the chat right now as we dive into the action together! 🏆🔥 

This specific regatta holds an irreplaceable piece of the cultural puzzle for the people of Vancouver and the broader Metro Vancouver region. Dragon boat racing is far more than just a competitive weekend sport here—it is a living, breathing celebration of multicultural heritage, historical resilience, and unbreakable team spirit that has defined False Creek for generations. It brings people together from every corner of the Lower Mainland, from Richmond to Burnaby, to share in a unified celebration of community strength. Because our downtown stadium district is adjusting its footprint to accommodate massive international sports spotlights later this summer, our traditional late-June paddling mega-festival is taking a brief pause. That means the Vessi 500 has officially stepped up as the absolute crown jewel and pinnacle of dragon boat racing for the entire year, making this the true, definitive kickoff to our West Coast summer season. 🌊🍁

We are taking you on an immersive tour of the entire festival footprint today to ensure you get your complete community fix. Our main focus will be tracking the final stretches of the races as boats rocket past the Canoe Bridge toward the finish line. Thanks to a major organizational change this year, the elite Women's and Mixed divisions are fully integrated, running back-to-back all day long, ensuring non-stop variety in the live chat. But we aren't just staying by the water's edge; we are getting properly nosy and taking the camera straight into the sprawling Team Village over at the South Plaza. We will be tracking down the real characters of the festival, interviewing the athletes face-to-face right by the giant plaza bird statues, checking out their custom team jerseys, and uncovering the most hilarious pre-race rituals keeping them loose. 🙌✨

You already know a true festival celebration is never complete without a deep dive into the local culinary landscape! We are making a major pit stop at the bustling Food Truck Alley along Athletes Way to check out how local independent chefs are keeping the massive crowds fed. Get ready for a live, mouth-watering taste test of iconic local staples that define the Vancouver street food scene, including Japanese-fusion hot dogs from Japadog, legendary crispy chicken from The Frying Pan, sweet treats from the Lee's Donuts outpost, and a premium caffeine injection from Mukasi Coffee. We will be chatting with folks waiting in the massive lines, swapping local stories, and soaking in the incredible aromas of the best outdoor kitchen setup in the province today. 🌭🍩☕

To wrap up this ultimate festival broadcast, we are exploring the brilliant independent business showcase at Milton Wong Plaza North, right next to Tap and Barrel. Coordinated by the team at Got Craft, this lively artisan market features 18 curated local makers, creators, and small entrepreneurs displaying beautiful hand-crafted West Coast goods, apparel, and stunning local art. Plus, we will be swinging by the massive title sponsor activation center to check out the latest waterproof gear and custom festival shoe charms from Vessi. From the rhythmic thumping of the dragon boat drums vibrating through the boardwalk to the spectacular cultural performances lighting up the main stage, this is a true sensory explosion of sport, food, art, and local pride. Thank you for being the best live chat community on the internet, and let's enjoy the festival! 🔔❤️

title: 端午节 忆屈原 | Dragon Boat Festival: For Qu Yuan, For Ever
description:
端午节是中国传统节日，主要为纪念战国时期爱国诗人屈原。他忠诚爱国却遭陷害流放，虽身处逆境仍心系家国，写下诸多不朽诗篇。楚国都城被攻破后，屈原悲痛万分，于农历五月初五投汨罗江自尽。百姓感念其忠义，纷纷驾船打捞，投粽子喂鱼以防其遗体被啃食，还挂艾草驱虫。久而久之，这些举动演变成端午龙舟竞渡、吃粽子、挂艾草等习俗。如今，端午节不仅是缅怀屈原的日子，更是中国重要传统节日，已被列入世界非物质文化遗产。

The Dragon Boat Festival is a traditional Chinese holiday, mainly to honor Qu Yuan, a patriotic poet from ancient China. He loved his country deeply but was wrongfully exiled. Though unlucky, he always cared about his people and wrote many great poems. When his country’s capital fell, Qu Yuan was heartbroken and drowned himself in the Miluo River on the 5th day of the 5th lunar month. To honor him, people rowed boats to search for him, threw rice dumplings (zongzi) into the river to keep fish away from his body, and hung mugwort to drive away insects. These acts became traditions: dragon boat racing, eating zongzi and hanging mugwort. Now it’s a key Chinese festival and a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.

description:

Following the performances, the traditional ritual of Ankang's dragon boat customs commenced with dragon head worship. On the stage, the shield formation formed by dozens of people shouted three times, inviting the dragon formation to escort the dragon head to the center of the stage with firm steps. The chief sacrificial officer stood on a high platform, stroked the dragon's whiskers, dusted off the dust on it, and kowtowed three times before reciting a toast. The officer then completed the rituals of dragon worship and dragon joining in sequence, representing thousands of people praying to heaven for good weather and peace for all families.

With heavy drumbeats and powerful shouting and singing, 28 dragon boat teams began the traditional folk event, namely the dragon boat racing. In the "catching ducks and touching carps" section, contestants jumped off their boats and caught ducks and carps with bare hands in the turbulent river. The winners held high the "trophy" in their hands, sending blessings of wealth, auspiciousness, peace, and well-being to everyone.

Ankang's unique dragon boat customs have developed over time due to the city's reliance on water transportation on the Hanjiang River. To this day, the charm of dragon boat racing is still a highlight in Ankang's culture. Since the first Dragon Boat Racing was held in 2000, Ankang has successfully held the Dragon Boat Racing 22 times. The event has become an important brand of Ankang to promote cultural prosperity, economic development, and further opening up of the city.

title: Making Zongzi: Traditional Dragon Boat Festival Rice Dumplings | Rural Chinese Cooking #chinesefood
description:
Celebrate Dragon Boat Festival 2026 with authentic handmade zongzi — traditional sticky rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo leaves, made the old-fashioned way in a Chinese village.

About This Video
Step into a peaceful Chinese countryside kitchen as we prepare zongzi (sticky rice dumplings) from start to finish for the annual Dragon Boat Festival (Duanwu Jie). From soaking glutinous rice and preparing fresh bamboo leaves to folding each dumpling by hand and slow-cooking them to perfection, every step follows a tradition passed down through generations.
This video captures not just a recipe, but a way of life — the quiet rhythm of rural China, the warmth of family tradition, and the art of handmade cooking that has marked the Double Fifth Festival for over 2,000 years.

What is Zongzi?
Zongzi are pyramid-shaped glutinous rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo or reed leaves, the iconic food of China's Dragon Boat Festival. The tradition honors Qu Yuan, an ancient poet who drowned himself in protest. Villagers threw rice dumplings into the river to keep fish from eating his body — a custom that evolved into today's zongzi-making tradition.

Ingredients Used
• Glutinous (sticky) rice
• Fresh bamboo leaves
• Pork belly / red bean paste (sweet & savory options)
• Salted duck egg yolk
• Mushrooms & chestnuts
• Cotton string for tying

title: Living Heritage: Duanwu - Dragon Boat Festival
description:
Beating drums, synchronous strokes and an exhilarated crowd. Such spectacles of excitement are no rare scene across #China on #Duanwu, or #DragonBoatFestival, which falls on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month.

Although tales of its origin differ, the two-millennia-old festival is widely celebrated in memory of Qu Yuan, a patriotic poet during the Warring States Period (475-221 BC). When Qu realized his country's decline was beyond recovery, his remorse grew so strong that he committed suicide, on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month. Legend has it that locals rowed boats to save the poet and threw zongzi, or sticky rice dumplings, into the river to keep fish from devouring his body. This tradition was passed down in China, even travelling across borders, carrying China’s respect for a patriot to all parts of the world.

A host of other practices are also associated with Duanwu. The fifth day of the fifth month is considered unlucky and poisonous. To rid themselves of the misfortune, many people, especially those in South China, take herbal baths, hang moxa and calamus plants over their doors and wear sachets stuffed with Chinese herbs, all with strong scents, to do away with venomous insects. Realgar wine, a common antidote against venom, is often enjoyed along with meals. Five-colored thread bracelets are also worn to obviate evil spirits. 

Duanwu began as a day to remember a poet steeped with love for his country. His patriotism is still celebrated today, with rich and fun customs that unite hearts across the globe, in methods that hail to the harmony between nature and man.

[The Story of Pearl Girl 珠帘玉幕] After enduring countless hardships, Hepu's pearl diver Duanwu escapes the brutal pearl farm and crosses paths with Yan Zijing, a merchant from the Western Regions, and Zhang Jinran, a noble gentleman. Joining Yan's trade caravan, she fights her way up, lured by the glittering world of jewels within reach. But on the journey northwest, she unearths secrets of her own past and is drawn into Yan Zijing's complex web of revenge, losing loved ones and facing betrayal. Reborn as Su Muzhe, she ventures into Yangzhou, determined to seek the truth no matter the cost. With a clear mind free from hatred, she founds her own business, "Clear Mind," and joins forces with Zhang Jinran to clear an old injustice. Yan Zijing, once intent on shouldering his shame and revenge alone, eventually finds peace and braves life's storms with her. As the dust settles, Su Muzhe reaches new heights at the Jewel Exhibition, inspiring countless women who once yielded to fate to reclaim their own paths.

title: Festive China: Dragon Boat Festival
description:
The #DragonBoatFestival is the fifth day of the fifth month on the Chinese lunar calendar.

There are many legends about the 2,000-year-old festival, but the most famous one is commemorating the death of #QuYuan, a patriotic poet from the State of Chu during the Warring States Period (475-221 BC).

Qu Yuan is said to have been loyal and patriotic his whole life. When he realized the decline of Chu was beyond recovery, his remorse knowing he could no longer save it grew stronger and stronger. On the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, he threw himself into the river and died for his beloved homeland.   
 
Watch this video to learn more about the legend, as well as traditions and festive foods related to the festival. 

Festive China is a series of short clips focusing on traditional Chinese festivals and festivities, the cultural connotations of traditional holidays, their development and changes, and how they manifest in today's China.

description:
Near the summer solstice, communities across China gather to race boats, many adorned with painted carvings of dragons.

It’s clear to see how Duanwu Jie, China’s summer solstice holiday, got its English-language name—the Dragon Boat Festival. Around the fifth day of the fifth month on the traditional Chinese calendar, people gather to race boats adorned with brightly-painted carvings of dragons. Designs and types of contest vary widely. Some races are intense athletic challenges, while other events are more of a community get-together. Stories link the festival with the poet Qu Yuan, born in the late fourth century B.C. Said to be an able minister of the Chu state who was banished from the court, Qu Yuan despaired at his rivals’ corruption, and at last drowned himself in a river. By legend, an ensuing search for Qu by boat inspired the day’s races.

title: What is the Chinese Dragon Boat Festival (Duanwu) ?
description:
With a history over 2,000 years old, the Dragon Boat Festival is steeped in tradition and legend. It commemorates Qu Yuan, a Chinese poet and patriot. He lived 340-278 BC. 

Qu Yuan committed suicide by drowning himself in the Miluo River because of his despair over the king allying with the state of Qin.

The Dragon Boat Festival, also known as Duanwu, is celebrated annually on the fifth day of the fifth month of the Chinese lunar calendar. It falls on June 20 this year.

For thousands of years, the festival has been celebrated by eating zongzi and holding dragon boat races.

Zongzi, is a glutinous rice dumpling which is wrapped in bamboo or reed leaves and filled with other ingredients. Traditions of making the special dish vary across different parts of China.

The Dragon Boat Festival is also celebrated by many ethnic minority groups across China.

Folks here at CCTV-America help explain what it's all about.

title: Duanwu Festival
description:
The Dragon Boat Festival, also often known as the Tuen Ng or Duanwu Festival, is a traditional and statutory holiday originating in China.


The festival now occurs on the 5th day of the 5th month of the traditional lunar calendar, the source of its alternative name, the Double Fifth Festival. The Chinese calendar is lunisolar, so the date varies from year to year on the Gregorian calendar. In 2012, it fell on June 23; in 2013, on June 12; and in 2014, it occurred on June 2. The focus of most celebrations involves eating zongzi, drinking realgar wine, and racing dragon boats.


The sun is considered to be at its strongest around the time of summer solstice, as the daylight in the northern hemisphere is the longest. The sun, like the Chinese dragon, traditionally represents masculine energy, whereas the moon, like the phoenix, traditionally represents feminine energy. The summer solstice is considered the annual peak of male energy while the winter solstice, the longest night of the year, represents the annual peak of feminine energy. The masculine image of the dragon was thus naturally associated with Duanwu.


The usual English name for the holiday, 'Dragon Boat Festival,' directly translates into two alternative Chinese names for the holiday, Lóngchuánjié and Lóngzhōujié.


The official Chinese name of the festival is 端午节 on the mainland and 端午節 on Taiwan, Hong Kong, Singapore and Macao. This is pronounced variously in different Chinese dialects. In Mandarin, it is romanized as Duānwǔjié on the mainland and Taiwan; in Cantonese, it is romanized as Tuen1 Ng5 Jit3 on Hong Kong and Tung1 Ng5 Jit3 on Macao. All of these names refer to its original position as the first seventh-day in the fifth month of the traditional Chinese calendar, which was also known as 午. Both the People's Republic and the Republic of China use 'Dragon Boat Festival' as the official English translation of the holiday, while Hong Kong calls it the 'Tuen Ng Festival' and Macao calls it 'Dragon Boat Festival ' in English and Festividade do Barco-Dragí in Portuguese.


In Indonesian, the festival is known as 'Peh Cun', which is derived from the Hokkien phrase 扒船.


The story best known in modern China holds that the festival commemorates the death of the poet and minister Qu Yuan of the ancient state of Chu during the Warring States period of the Zhou Dynasty. A cadet member of the Chu royal house, Qu served in high offices. However, when the king decided to ally with the increasingly powerful state of Qin, Qu was banished for opposing the alliance and even accused of treason. During his exile, Qu Yuan wrote a great deal of poetry. Twenty-eight years later, Qin captured Ying, the Chu capital. In despair, Qu Yuan committed suicide by drowning himself in the Miluo River.


It is said that the local people, who admired him, raced out in their boats to save him or at least retrieve his body. This is said to have been the origin of dragon boat races. When his body could not be found, they dropped balls of sticky rice into the river so that the fish would eat them instead of Qu Yuan's body. This is said to be the origin of zongzi.


Despite the modern popularity of the Qu Yuan origin theory, in the former territory of the state of Wu, the festival commemorated Wu Zixu. Wu Zixu was a loyal advisor whose advice was ignored by the king to the detriment of the kingdom. Wu Zixu was forced to commit suicide by the king Fuchai, with his body thrown into the river on the fifth day of the fifth month. After his death, in places such as Suzhou, Wu Zixu is remembered during the Duanwu Festival to this day.
